question:
Assertion: Perspiration from human body helps in cooling the body.

Reason: A thin layer of water on the skin enhances its emissivity.
explanation:
Because during perspiration, the process of sweating, helps in cooling the human body. When we sweat, the sweat evaporates from the skin's surface, taking heat from the body and cooling it down. This is a well-known mechanism for temperature regulation in the body.
A thin layer of water on the skin does not enhance its emissivity. In fact, it would have the opposite effect. Emissivity is a measure of how efficiently a surface radiates thermal energy, and water on the skin would likely decrease emissivity rather than enhance it. So, while A is correct, $\mathrm{R}$ is not the correct explanation for $\mathrm{A}$.
So, correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question: Three objects colored black, grey and white can withstand hostile conditions upto $2800^\circ C$. These objects are thrown into a furnace where each of them attains a temperature of $2000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Which object will glow brightest?
explanation:
An ideal black body absorbs all the radiations incident upon it and has an emissivity $\in$ equal to unity. If a black body and an identical another body are kept at the same temperature, then the block body will radiate maximum power. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956445/preptracker/questions/q_38206_explanation_1764956445063.png For ideal black body $\in(\lambda)=\epsilon_{0}=1$
For grey body; $\in(\lambda)=\epsilon^{\prime} < 1$
Hence, the black object at a temperature of $2000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, will glow brightest.
So, correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A body that is a good radiator is also a good absorber of radiation at a given wavelength.


Reason: According to Kirchhoff's law, the absorptivity of a body is equal to its emissivity at a given wavelength
explanation:
That a body that is a good radiator is also a good absorber of radiation at a given wavelength. This is in accordance with Kirchhoff's law. This means that a good radiator (high emissivity) is also a good absorber (high absorptivity) of radiation, and it's a fundamental principle in radiative heat transfer. Therefore, both the Assertion and Reason are correct, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion.
So, correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question: Two slabs $A$ and $B$ of different materials but of the same thickness are joined end to end to form a composite slab. The thermal conductivities of A and B are $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$, respectively. A steady temperature difference of $12^\circ C$ is maintained across the composite slab. If $K_{1}=\dfrac{K_{2}}{2}$, the temperature difference across slab $A$ is
explanation:
Given situation can be shown in figure as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956035/preptracker/questions/q_38199_explanation_1764956035066.png Rate of heat flow through rod is given as
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{\Delta T}{l / K A}=\dfrac{K A(\Delta T)}{l}$
As, rate of heat flow is same for all sections
$\left(\dfrac{d Q}{d T}\right)_{P Q}=\left(\dfrac{d Q}{d T}\right)_{P R}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{100-T_{Q}}{\dfrac{L}{K_{1} A}}\right)=\left(\dfrac{T_{P}-T_{R}}{\dfrac{L}{K_{1} A}+\dfrac{L}{K_{2} A}}\right)$
But, given $K_{1}=\dfrac{K_{2}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(100-T_{Q}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{K_{1}}}=\dfrac{12}{\dfrac{1}{K_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{2 K_{1}}}$
$\Rightarrow\left(100-T_{Q}\right)=\dfrac{2.12}{3}=8$
$ \Rightarrow {T_P} - {T_Q} = 8^\circ C\,\,\,\left( {\because {T_P} = 100^\circ C} \right)$
So, correct option is (3)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Two metallic blocks $M_{1}$ and $M_{2}$ of same area of cross-section are connected to each other (as shown in figure). If the thermal conductivity of $M_{2}$ is $K$ then the thermal conductivity of $M_{1}$ will be : [Assume steady state heat conduction]
explanation:
The rate of transfer of heat
$\frac{{\Delta Q}}{{\Delta t}} = KA\left( {\frac{{\Delta T}}{l}} \right)$
$\frac{{\Delta {T_1}}}{{\Delta {T_2}}} = \frac{{{\ell _1}}}{{{K_1}}} \times \frac{{{K_2}}}{{{l_2}}} = \frac{{16}}{{{K_1}}} \times \frac{K}{8}$
$\frac{{20}}{{80}} = \frac{{16}}{{{K_1}}} \times \frac{K}{8}$
$ \Rightarrow {K_1} = 8K$
Note: Here $K$ is not kelvin.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Temperature difference of $120^\circ C$ is maintained between the two ends of a uniform $\operatorname{rod} A B$ of length $2 L$. Another bent $\operatorname{rod} P Q$, of same crosssection as $A B$ and length $\frac{{3\,L}}{2}$, is connected across $A B$ (see figure). In steady state, temperature difference between $P$ and $Q$ will be close to
explanation:
Assume $R$ be the thermal resistance of uniform rod of length $L$.
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_21_71eb17c7a2a3d7e067a9g-06.jpg?height=233&width=582&top_left_y=2207&top_left_x=1179

https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_21_71eb17c7a2a3d7e067a9g-07.jpg?height=185&width=568&top_left_y=262&top_left_x=316
Heat current: $I=\dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{e q}}=\dfrac{120}{8 R / 5}=\dfrac{75}{R}$
${(\Delta T)_{PQ}} = I\left( {\frac{{3R}}{5}} \right) = \frac{{75}}{R} \times \frac{{3R}}{5} = 45^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A black coloured solid sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is inside a cavity with vaccum inside. The walls of the cavity are maintained at temperature $T_{0}$. The initial temperature of the sphere is $3 T_{0}$. If the specific heat of the material of the sphere varies as $\alpha T^{3}$ per unit mass with the temperature $T$ of the sphere, where $\alpha$ is a constant, then the time taken for the sphere to cool down to temperature $2 T_{0}$ will be ( $\sigma$ is Stefan Boltzmann constant)
explanation:
Let $T$ is the temperature of sphere at some arbitrary time. The rate of heat looses by the sphere is
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}} = \sigma A\left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)$
$\frac{{ - M\left( {\alpha {T^3}} \right)dT}}{{dt}} = \sigma A\left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)$
$\int {\frac{{{T^3}dT}}{{\left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)}}} = - \int {\frac{{\sigma Adt}}{{M\alpha }}} $
Let $x=T^{4}-T_{0}^{4}$
$dx = 4{T^3}dT$
$\frac{1}{4}\int_{3{T_0}}^{2{T_0}} {\frac{1}{x}} dx = - \frac{{\sigma A}}{{M\alpha }}\int_0^t d t$
$[\ln (x)]_{3{T_0}}^{2{T_0}} = \left[ {\ln \left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)} \right]_{3{T_0}}^{2{T_0}} = - \frac{{4\sigma At}}{{M\alpha }}$
$\ln \left( {\frac{{16T_0^4 - T_0^4}}{{81T_0^4 - T_0^4}}} \right) = - \frac{{4\sigma At}}{{M\alpha }}$
$\ln \left( {\frac{{15}}{{80}}} \right) = - \frac{{4\sigma At}}{{M\alpha }}t = - \frac{{M\alpha }}{{4\sigma A}}$
$\ln \left( {\frac{{16}}{3}} \right) = \frac{{M\alpha }}{{16\pi {R^2}\sigma }}\ln \left( {\frac{{16}}{3}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following graphs correctly represents the relation between \[\ln E\] and \[\ln T\] where \[E\] is the amount of radition emitted per unit time from unit area of a body and \[T\] is the absolute temperature
explanation:
According to Stefan's law $E=\sigma T^{4}$
$\Rightarrow \ln E=\ln \sigma+4 \ln T \Rightarrow \ln E=4 \ln T+\ln \sigma$
The graph between $\ln E$ and $\ln T$ will be straight line, having positive slope $(m = 4)$ and intercept on $\ln E$ axis is equal to $\ln \sigma$
subject: Physics

question: According to Wien's displacement law:
explanation:
$A \propto T^{4}$ by stefan's Boltzmann law not by Wien's displacement law.
As $\lambda_{m} \alpha \dfrac{1}{T} \Rightarrow T_{3}>T_{2}>T_{1}$

options.text: Total area under the three graphs $A_{1}: A_{2}: A_{3}$ are equal

subject: Physics

question: A silver ball, painted black is kept inside a box which is maintained at a temperature of \[27^\circ C\]. The ball is maintained initially at a constant temperature of \[127^\circ C\] by making the radiation to fall on it through a small hole in the box. Later on due to some chemical reaction between silver and paint, the paint uniformly evaporates from the surface of ball exposing the silver. If same amount of radiation continues to fall on ball, then temperature of ball as a function of time is shown as: (Assume emissivity of silver is zero)
explanation: As emissivity of sliver is zero $(e = 0)$ so the total radiation that falls on the sphere will be completely absorbed and hence its temperature will remain constant since the sphere acts like a good emitter (Good absorbers are good emitters).
subject: Physics

question: The graph shown in the adjacent diagram, represents the variation of temperature \((T)\) of two bodies, \(x\) and y having same surface area, with time \[(t)\] due to the emission of radiation. Find the correct relation between the emissivity (\[e\]) and absorptivity (\[a\]) of the two bodies
explanation:
Rate of cooling $\left(-\dfrac{d T}{d t}\right) \propto$ emissivity (e)
From graph, $\left(-\dfrac{d T}{d t}\right)_{x}>\left(-\dfrac{d T}{d t}\right)_{y} \Rightarrow e_{x}>e_{y}$
As good absorbers are good emitters
So emissivity $(e) \propto$ Absorptive power $(a) \Rightarrow a_{x}>a_{y}$
subject: Physics

question: If a piece of metal is heated to temperature \(\theta\) and then allowed to cool in a room which is at temperature \(\theta_{0}\), the graph between the temperature \[T\] of the metal and time \[t\] will be closest to
explanation: According to Newton's law of cooling, the temperature goes on decreasing with time non-linearly and finally it reaches temperature $\theta_{0}$.
subject: Physics

question: Four spheres $A$, $B$, $C$ and $D$ are of same radius but made of different metals. Their densities are in ratio $6: 3: 4: 5$ and specific heats are in ratio $2: 5: 4: 6$. These are initially kept at the same temperature and placed in the same surroundings. The sphere which has the slowest rate of cooling is:
explanation:
The rate of heat flow is
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=e \sigma\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right) \Delta T$
where $R$ is the radius of the sphere and $\Delta T = {T^4} - T_0^4\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} T\,\& \,{T_0}$ are the temperature of the body and surrounding
$\dfrac{d T}{d t}=\dfrac{4 \pi e \sigma R^{2}}{m c} \Delta T=\dfrac{4 \pi e \sigma R^{2}}{\rho \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} c}(\Delta T)=\dfrac{3 e \sigma \Delta T}{\rho R c}$
$c$ - heat capacity
The rate of cooling $\dfrac{d T}{d t}=\dfrac{k}{R c}$
where $k$ is a constant $)$
For the material $D$ the value of $RC$ is maximum and rate of cooling is minimum.
subject: Physics

question: The solar constant for the earth is $\Sigma$. The surface temperature of the sun is $TK$. The sun subtends an angle $\theta$ at the earth.
explanation:
Let $R = $ radius of the sun, $d = $ distance of the earth from the sun.
Power radiated by the sun $=\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right) \sigma T^{4}=P$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956239/preptracker/questions/q_38035_explanation_1764956239059.png
Energy received per unit area per second normally on the earth.
$\begin{aligned}& \Sigma=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}}=\dfrac{4 \pi R^{2} \sigma T^{4}}{4 \pi d^{2}} \\& =\left(\sigma T^{4}\right)\left(\dfrac{R}{d}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4} \sigma T^{4}\left(\dfrac{2 R}{d}\right)^{2}\end{aligned}$
Angle subtended by the sun at the earth is
$\theta = \frac{{2R}}{d}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\sum \propto {\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\theta ^2}.$
subject: Physics

question: A space object has the shape of a sphere of radius $R$. Heat sources ensure that the heat evolution at a constant rate is distributed uniformly over its volume. The amount of heat liberated by a unit surface area is proportional to the fourth power of thermodynamic temperature. If the power liberated is same and radius is made half of the initial value then ratio of final temperature to initial temperature is
explanation:
The heat radiated per unit time is
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}}\alpha {R^2}{T^4}$
$ \Rightarrow {R^2}{T^4} = {\text{ constant }}$
${\left( {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}} \right)^4} = {\left( {\frac{{{R_2}}}{{{R_1}}}} \right)^2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}\right)^{1 / 2} \\& T_{2}=T_{1}\left(\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}=T_{1}\left(\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{1} / 2}\right)^{1 / 2} \\& T_{2}=\sqrt{2} T_{1}=1.414 T_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A planet is at an average distance $d$ from the sun, and its average surface temperature is $T$. Assume that the planet receives energy only from the sun, and loses energy only through radiation from its surface. Neglect atmospheric effects. If $T \propto d^{-n}$ the value of $n$ is
explanation:
let $P = $ power radiated by the sun, $R = $ radius of planet.
Energy received by planet $=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi R^{2}$
Energy radiated by planet $=\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right) \sigma T^{2}$.
For thermal equilibrium, $\dfrac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi R^{2}=4 \pi R^{2} \sigma T^{4}$.
$\Rightarrow \quad T^{4} \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}} \quad \text { or } T^{4} \propto d^{-1 / 2}$
subject: Physics

question: A slab consists of two parallel layers of copper and brass of the same thickness and having thermal conductivities in the ratio $1: 4$. The free end of brass is at $100^\circ C$ and that of copper at $0^\circ C$. the temperature of interface is
explanation:
Let $T$ is the temperature of the interface.
${K_{{\rm{Copper }}}} = 4K\,\& \,{K_{{\rm{brass }}}} = K$
As the rate of heat flow is same in two slabs
$\frac{{4K.A(100 - T)}}{l} = \frac{{KA(T - 0)}}{l}$
$T = 80^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: The ends $Q$ and $R$ of two thin wires, $P Q$ and $RS$, are joined together. Initially each of the wires has a length of $1m$ at $10^\circ C$. Now the end $P$ is maintained at $10^\circ C$, while the end $S$ is heated and maintained at $400^\circ C$. The system is thermally insulted from its surroundings. If the thermal conductivity of wire $PQ$ is twice that of the wire RS and the coefficient of linear thermal expansion of $PQ$ is $1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$, the change in length of the wire $P Q$ is
explanation:
From the given data;
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_21_71eb17c7a2a3d7e067a9g-03.jpg?height=111&width=318&top_left_y=1108&top_left_x=441
Thermal resistance $R=\dfrac{l}{K A}$
$\dfrac{R_{P Q}}{R_{R S}}=\dfrac{K_{R S}}{K_{P Q}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
Let $T$ is the temperature of the junction. As rate of heat flow is same in both parts.
so, $T - 10 = \frac{{400 - T}}{2} \Rightarrow T = 140^\circ C$
The temperature of the first rod as a function of distance $x$ is
$T(x)=10+130 x$
Each part of the rod has different change in temperature w.r.to the initial value.
The change in temperature of the rod as a function of $x$ w.r.to the initial temperature of the rod is
$\Rightarrow \Delta T=130 x$
Extension in a small element of length $d x$ is
$d l=d x \alpha \Delta T=130 \alpha x d x$
The total extension is
$\Delta l = 130\alpha \int_0^1 x dx = \frac{{130}}{2} \times 1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 1$
$ \Rightarrow \Delta \ell = 0.78\,mm.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Two ends of a conducting rod of varying cross-section are maintained at $100^\circ C$ and $0^\circ C$ respectively. In steady state
explanation:
Rate of flow of heat is constant throughout the rod.
Temperature difference is given by
$\Delta T=i R$
where $R=\dfrac{l}{K A} \Rightarrow R \propto \dfrac{1}{A}$
Area across $CD$ is less. Therefore, $\Delta T$ across $CD$ will be more.

options.text: Temperature difference may be equal or different depending upon thermal conductivity of the rod.
Temperature difference across $AB$ is less than that across $CD$
Temperature difference across $A B$ is greater

subject: Physics

question: A metal is heated in a furnace where a sensor is kept above the metal surface to read the power radiated $P$ by the metal. The sensor has a scale that displays $\log _{2}\left(P / P_{0}\right)$, where $P_{0}$ is a constant. When the metal surface is at a temperature of $487^\circ C$, the sensor shows a value 1. Assume that the emissivity of the metallic surface remains constant. What is the value displayed by the sensor when the temperature of the metal surface is raised to $2767^\circ C$
explanation:
Power radiated $P=e \sigma A T^{4}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { At } 487^{\circ} C ; P_{1}=e \sigma A(760)^{4} \\& \text { Given, } \log _{2} \dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{0}}=1 \Rightarrow P_{0}=\dfrac{P_{1}}{2}\end{aligned}$
At $2767^{\circ} C ; P_{2}=e \sigma A(3040)^{4}$
$\begin{aligned}\text { Reading } & =\log _{2}\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{0}}\right)=\log _{2}\left[\dfrac{e \sigma A(3040)^{4} \times 2}{e \sigma A(760)^{4}}\right] \\& =\log _{2}\left(4^{4} \times 2\right)=9\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Consider two identical homogenous spheres, $A$ and $B$, with the same initial temperatures. One of them is at rest on a horizontal plane, while the second one hangs on a thread. The same quantity of heat has been supplied to both balls. Which of the following statement is true about the temperatures of $A$and $B$ ?
explanation:
As heat is given both expands by the same values. $C.M$ of Sphere $B$ raises up and $C.M$ of sphere $A$ goes down slightly. The raise in $P.E$ of $B$ should come from the expense of heat supplied. Whereas decrease in $P.E$ of $A$ will be converted into heat.
$\Rightarrow T_{A}>T_{B}$
subject: Physics

question: Three very large plates of same area are kept parallel and close to each other. They are considered as ideal black surfaces and have very high thermal conductivity. The first and third plates are maintained at temperature $3\,T$ and $2\,T$ respectively. The temperature of the middle (i.e. second) plate under steady state condition is
explanation:
The three plates are shown in the figure
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956017/preptracker/questions/q_38003_explanation_1764956016990.png
Let $T^{\prime}$ is the temperature of the middle plate.
Here $T^{\prime}$ should by lower than $3\,T$ and higher than $2\,T$
In steady state energy absorbed by middle plate is equal to energy released by middle plate.
$\begin{gathered}\sigma A(3 T)^{4}-\sigma A\left(T^{\prime}\right)^{4}=\sigma A\left(T^{\prime}\right)^{4}-\sigma A(2 T)^{4} \$3 T)^{4}-\left(T^{\prime}\right)^{4}=\left(T^{\prime}\right)^{4}-(2 T)^{4} \\2\left(T^{\prime}\right)^{4}=(16+81) T^{4} \\T^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{97}{2}\right)^{1 / 4} T .\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: Parallel rays of light of intensity $I = 912W{m^{ - 2}}$ are incident on a spherical black body kept in surroundings of temperature $300\,K$. Take StefanBoltzmann constant $\sigma  = 5.7 \times {10^{ - 8}}{W^{ - 2}}M\,{K^4}$ and assume that the energy exchange with the surroundings is only through radiation. The final steady state temperature of the black body is close to
explanation:
In steady state the radiation absorbed by the sphere is equal to radiation looses by it.
As the parallel beam falls on the sphere the radiation that falls is $I\left(\pi R^{2}\right)$
$I\pi {R^2} = \sigma \left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)4\pi {R^2}$
$ \Rightarrow I = \sigma \left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)4$
$ \Rightarrow {T^4} - T_0^4 = 40 \times {10^8}$
$ \Rightarrow {T^4} - 81 \times {10^8} = 40 \times {10^8}$
$ \Rightarrow {T^4} = 121 \times {10^8}$
$ \Rightarrow T \approx 330\,K.$
subject: Physics

question: The figure shows the cross-section of the outer wall of a house built in a hill-resort to keep the house insulated from the freezing temperature of outside. The wall consists of teak wood of thickness $L_{1}$ and brick of thickness $\left(L_{2}=5 L_{1}\right)$, sandwiching two layers of an unknown material with identical thermal conductivities and thickness. The thermal conductivity of teak wood is $K_{1}$ and that of brick is $\left(K_{2}=5 K\right)$. Heat conduction through the wall has reached a steady state with the temperature of three surface being known. $\left(T_{1}=25 C, T_{2}=20 C\right.$ and $\left.T_{5}=-20 C\right)$. Find the interface temperatures $T_{3}$ and $T_{4}$.
explanation:
Let interface area be $A$, then the thermal resistance of wood, $R_{1}=\dfrac{L_{1}}{K_{1} A}$ and that of brick wall
$R_{2}=\dfrac{L_{2}}{K_{2} A}=\dfrac{5 L_{1}}{5 K_{1} A}=R_{1}$
Let thermal resistance of the each sandwitch layer
$=R$. Then the above wall can be visualised as a circuit
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_21_71eb17c7a2a3d7e067a9g-02.jpg?height=121&width=568&top_left_y=1285&top_left_x=361
Thermal current through each wall is same.
Hence $\dfrac{25-20}{R_{1}}=\dfrac{20-T_{3}}{R}=\dfrac{T_{3}-T_{4}}{R}=\dfrac{T_{4}+20}{R_{1}}$
$ \Rightarrow 25 - 20 = {T_4} + 20 \Rightarrow {T_4} = - 15^\circ C$
also, $20-T_{3}=T_{3}-T_{4} \Rightarrow T_{3}=\dfrac{20+T_{4}}{2}=2.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
subject: Physics

question: Three rods of identical area of cross-section and made from the same metal form the sides of an isosceles triangle $ABC$ right angle at $B$. The points $A$ and $B$ are maintained at temperatures $T$ and $\sqrt{2} T$ respectively. In the steady state the temperature of the point $C$ is $T_{C}$. Assuming that only heat conduction takes place, $\dfrac{T_{C}}{T}$ is equal to $\left(T_{c}>T\right)$
explanation:
$T_{B}>T_{A} \Rightarrow$ Heat will flow from $B$ to $A$ via two paths (i) $B$ to $A$ (ii) and along $BCA$ as shown.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956019/preptracker/questions/q_37991_explanation_1764956018736.png
Rate of flow of heat in path $BCA$ will be same
$\begin{aligned}\text { i.e., } & \left(\dfrac{Q}{t}\right)_{B C}=\left(\dfrac{Q}{t}\right)_{C A} \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{K\left(\sqrt{2} T-T_{C}\right) A}{a}=\dfrac{K\left(T_{C}-T\right) A}{\sqrt{2} a} \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{T_{C}}{T}=\dfrac{3}{1+\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A rod of length $l$ and cross-sectional area $A$ has a variable conductivity given by $K=\alpha T$ where $\alpha$ is a positive constant and $T$ is temperature in kelvin. Two ends of the rod are maintained at temperatures $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}\left(T_{1}>T_{2}\right)$. Heat current flowing through the rod will be
explanation:
Heat current:
$\begin{aligned}& i=-k A \dfrac{d T}{d x} \\& i d x=-k A d T\end{aligned}$
Given that
$\begin{aligned}& K=\alpha T \\& i \int_{0}^{l} d x=-A \alpha \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} T d T \\& i l=-A \alpha \dfrac{\left(T_{2}^{2}-T_{1}^{2}\right)}{2} \Rightarrow i=\dfrac{A \alpha\left(T_{1}^{2}-T_{2}^{2}\right)}{2 l}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The rate of heat flow through a cross section of the rod shown in the figure is, $\left(T_{2}>T_{1}\right.$ and thermal conductivity of the material of the rod is $K$.)
explanation:
Consider a circular disc of radius r present at a distance $x$ from the front end of the cylinder as shown in the figure
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956959/preptracker/questions/q_37983_explanation_1764956958822.png
The rate of heat flow across the disc is
$\begin{aligned}& i=\dfrac{K \pi r^{2} d T}{d x} \\& \int \dfrac{d x}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{K \pi}{i} \int d T\end{aligned}$
From the figure
$\tan \theta=\dfrac{r-r_{1}}{x}=\dfrac{r_{2}-r}{L}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \quad r-r_{1}=x \tan \theta \\& d r=d x \tan \theta \\& \dfrac{1}{\tan \theta} \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \dfrac{d r}{r^{2}}=\dfrac{K \pi}{i} \int_{1_{1}}^{r_{2}} d T \\& \dfrac{1}{\tan \theta}\left[\dfrac{1}{r_{1}}-\dfrac{1}{r_{2}}\right]=\dfrac{K \pi}{i}\left[T_{2}-T_{1}\right] \\& \dfrac{L}{r_{2}-r_{1}}\left[\dfrac{r_{2}-r_{1}}{r_{1} r_{2}}\right]=\dfrac{K \pi}{i}\left[T_{2}-T_{1}\right] \\& i=\dfrac{K \pi r_{1} r_{2}\left[T_{2}-T_{1}\right]}{L}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The container $A$ is constantly maintained at$100^\circ C$ and container $B$ contains ice at $0^\circ C$. Different rods are used to connect them. For a rod made of copper, it takes 30 minutes for the ice to melt and for a rod of steel of same crosssection taken in different experiment it takes 60 minutes for ice to melt. When these rods are simultaneously connected in parallel, the ice melts in:
explanation:
$Q = $ it where $i=$ heat flow rate
$=\dfrac{\Delta T}{R}=\dfrac{100}{R}$
$Q=\left(\dfrac{100}{R_{1}}\right)(30) \Rightarrow R_{1}=\left(\dfrac{100}{Q}\right) \times 30$
Also, $Q=\left(\dfrac{100}{R_{2}}\right) \times 30 \Rightarrow R_{2}=\left(\dfrac{100}{Q}\right) \times 60$
When the rods are connected in parallel
Now, $Q=\left(\dfrac{1}{R_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{R_{2}}\right) 100 t$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{R_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{R_{2}}=\dfrac{Q}{3000}+\dfrac{Q}{6000}=\dfrac{Q}{2000} \\& Q=\dfrac{Q \times 100 t}{2000} \Rightarrow t=20 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: A spherical black body with a radius of $12\,cm$ radiates $440\,W$ power at $500\,K$. If the radius were halved and the temperature doubled, the power radiated in watt would be
explanation:
Radiated power by black body
$P = \frac{Q}{T} = A\sigma {T^4}$
$ \Rightarrow P \propto A{T^4} \propto {r^2}\,{T^4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}}} \right)^2}{\left( {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{\,{T_2}}}} \right)^4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{440}}{{{P_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{12}}{6}} \right)^2}{\left( {\frac{{500}}{{1000}}} \right)^4}$
$ \Rightarrow {P_2} = 1760\,W$
subject: Physics

question: The area of the hole of heat furnace is ${10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$. It radiates $1.58 \times {10^5}cal$ of heat per hour. If the emissivity of the furnace is 0.80 , then its temperature is
explanation:
According to Stefan's law $E=\sigma \varepsilon A T^{4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{1.58 \times {{10}^5} \times 4.2}}{{60 \times 60}} = 5.67 \times {10^{ - 8}} \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 0.8 \times {T^4}$
$T = 2500\,K$
subject: Physics

question: Two spheres of the same material have radii $1\,m$ and $4\,m$, temperatures $4000\,K$ and $2000\,K$ respectively. Then the ratio of energy radiated per second by the first sphere as compared to that by the second is
subject: Physics

question: The temperature of body is increased from $27^\circ C$ to $127^\circ C$. The radiation emitted by it increase by a factor of
subject: Physics

question: If the radius of a star is $R$ and it acts as a black body, what would be the temperature of the star, in which the rate of energy production is $Q$? (where $\sigma$ is Stefan's constant)
explanation:
If $R$ is the of radius of the star and at its temperature, then the energy emitted by the star per second through radiation in accordance with Stefan's law will be given by
$Q=4 \pi R^{2} \sigma T^{4} \Rightarrow T=\left(\dfrac{Q}{4 \pi R^{2} \sigma}\right)^{1 / 4}$
subject: Physics

question: A black body at $227^\circ C$ radiates heat at the rate of $7\,cal \cdot c{m^{ - 2}}\,{s^{ - 1}}.$ At a temperature of $727^\circ C$, the rate of heat radiated in the same units will be
explanation:
Apply Stefan's law of radiation, $E=e \sigma A T^{4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{{T_2}}}} \right)^4}$
$ \Rightarrow {E_2} = 7{\left[ {\frac{{273 + 727}}{{273 + 227}}} \right]^4} = {\left( {\frac{{1000}}{{500}}} \right)^4} \times 7$
$ \Rightarrow {E_x} = 112\,cal\,c{m^{ - 2}}\,{s^{ - 1}}.$
subject: Physics

question: A black body is at $727^\circ C$. It emits energy at a rate which is proportional to
explanation:
Amount of heat energy radiated per second by unit area of a black body is directly proportional to fourth power of absolute temperature.
According to stefan's law,
$\begin{aligned}& E \propto T^{4} \\& \Rightarrow E \propto(727+273)^{4} \Rightarrow E \propto(1000)^{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A black body radiates heat at temperatures $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}\left(T_{1}>T_{2}\right)$. The frequency correspoding to maximum energy is
explanation:
If $\lambda_{m}$ is wavelength corresponding to maximum energy emission for a blackbody at temperature $T$, then according to the Wien's displacement law,
${\lambda _m}T = {\rm{constant}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since frequency $v_{m}=\dfrac{c}{\lambda_{m}}$, using eq. (1),
$\frac{T}{{{v_m}}} = $ constant $\Rightarrow v_{m} \propto T$
Hence $T_{2}>T_{1} \Rightarrow v_{2}>v_{1}$.
subject: Physics

question: The surface temperature of the stars is determined using
explanation: The surface temperature of the stars is determined by Wein's displacement law.
subject: Physics

question: The unit of Stefan's constant is
explanation:
According to Stefan's law, the energy radiated per second, $U = \sigma A{T^4}$
$\therefore \sigma = \frac{U}{{A{T^4}}} = \frac{W}{{{m^2}{K^4}}} = W{m^{ - 2}}{K^{ - 4}}$
subject: Physics

question: The amount of heat energy radiated by a metal at temperature $T$ is $E$. When the temperature is increased to $3T$, energy radiated is
explanation:
According to Stefan's law
$E \propto T^{4}$
$\therefore \dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{T}{3 T}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{1}{81} \Rightarrow E_{2}=81 E$
subject: Physics

question: A piece of iron is heated in a flame. It first becomes dull red then becomes reddish yellow finally turns to white hot. The correct explanation for the above observation is possible by using.
explanation:
Wein's displacement law, $\lambda_{m} T=$ constant
$\Rightarrow \lambda_{m}=\dfrac{1}{T}$
So as temperature increase $\lambda$ decrease.
subject: Physics

question: Two stars $A$ and $B$ radiate maximum energy at the wavelengths of $360\,nm$ and $480\,nm$ respectively. Then the ratio of the surface temperatures of $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
According to Wein's displacement law
${\lambda _{\max }}T = $ constant
$\therefore \frac{{{\lambda _A}}}{{{\lambda _B}}} = \frac{{{T_B}}}{{{T_A}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{T_A}}}{{{T_B}}} = \frac{{{\lambda _B}}}{{{\lambda _A}}} = \frac{{480\,nm}}{{360\,nm}} = \frac{4}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: The dimensional formula for the thermal resistance is
explanation:
$R = \frac{{{\text{Temperature}}\,{\text{difference}}}}{{{\text{Heat}}\,{\text{current}}}}$
$\therefore R = \frac{{[\theta ]}}{{\left[ {M{L^2}\,{T^{ - 2}}/T} \right]}}$
$ = \left[ {{M^{ - 1}}\,{L^{ - 2}}\,{T^3}\theta } \right]$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Two rods of same length and material transfer a given amount of heat in $12s$, when they are joined end to end. But when they are joined lengthwise, then they will transfer same heat in same conditions in
explanation:
Let the heat transferred be $Q$
When rods are joined end to end. Heat transferred by each rod
$ Q = \frac{{K(2\,A)\Delta \theta }}{l} \times 12\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When rods are joined lengthwise,
$Q = \frac{{KA\Delta \theta }}{{2l}}t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eqs.(1) and (2) we get $t = 48\,s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: A cylindrical metallic rod in thermal contact with two reservoirs of heat at its two ends conducts an amount of heat $Q$ in time t. The metallic rod is melted and the material is formed into a rod of half the radius of the original rod. What is the amount of heat conducted by the new rod when placed in thermal contact with the two reservoirs in time t?
explanation:
In steady state the amount of heat flowing from one face to the other face in time $t$ is given by
$Q=\dfrac{K A\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right) t}{l}$
Where $K$ is coefficient of thermal conductivity of material of rod
$ \Rightarrow \frac{Q}{t} \propto \frac{A}{l} \propto \frac{{{r^2}}}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
As the metallic rod is melted and the meterial is formed into a rod of half the radius
${V_1} = {V_2} \Rightarrow \pi r_1^2{l_1} = \pi r_2^2{l_2} \Rightarrow {l_1} = \frac{{{l_2}}}{4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{Q}{Q_{2}}=\dfrac{r_{1}^{2}}{l_{1}} \times \dfrac{l_{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{r_{1}^{2}}{l_{1}} \times \dfrac{4 l_{1}}{\left(r_{1} / 2\right)^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow Q=16 Q_{2} \Rightarrow Q_{2}=\dfrac{Q}{16}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Three identical rods $AB$ and $C$ are placed end to end. A temperature difference is maintained between the free ends of $A$ and $C$. The thermal conductivity of $B$ is thrice that of $C$ and half of that of A. The effective thermal conductivity of thesystem will be $\left( {{K_A}} \right.$ is the thermal conductivity of rod 1)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955833/preptracker/questions/q_37734_explanation_1764955833074.png
Let $L$ be length and $A$ be area of cross-section of each rod respectively.
Given: ${K_B} = 3\,{K_C},{K_B} = \frac{1}{2}{K_A}$
Thermal resistance of the rods are
${R_A} = \frac{L}{{{K_A}A}}$
${R_B} = \frac{L}{{{K_B}A}}$
${R_C} = \frac{L}{{{K_C}A}}$
The electrical equivalent circuit is as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955834/preptracker/questions/q_37734_explanation_1764955833762.png
Here, the rods are connected in series.
$\therefore {R_S} = {R_A} + {R_B} + {R_C}$
Let ${K_{eff{\rm{ }}}}$ be effective thermal conductivity of the system.
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{3 L}{K_{\text {eff }} A}=\dfrac{L}{K_{A} A}+\dfrac{L}{K_{B} A}+\dfrac{L}{K_{C} A} \\& \dfrac{3}{K_{\text {eff }}}=\dfrac{1}{K_{A}}+\dfrac{1}{\dfrac{K_{A}}{2}}+\dfrac{1}{\dfrac{K_{A}}{6}} \\& \dfrac{3}{K_{\text {eff }}}=\dfrac{1}{K_{A}}[1+2+6] \\& \Rightarrow K_{\text {eff }}=\dfrac{3}{9} K_{A}=\dfrac{1}{3} K_{A}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Two rods of copper and brass $\left(K_{C}>K_{B}\right)$ of same length and area of cross - sectional are joined as shown. End $A$ is kept at $100^\circ C$ and end $B$ at $0^\circ C$. The temperature at the junction
explanation:
${K_C} > {K_B}$
$\therefore {R_C} < {R_B}$
Temperature difference across copper $ = H{R_C} = {(TD)_C}$
And Temperature difference across brass $ = H{R_B} = {(TD)_B}$
Heat current $H$ will be same in both, as they are in series
Since $R_{C} < R_{B}$
$\therefore {(TD)_C} < {(TD)_B}$
or temperature of junction is more than $50^\circ C$

options.text: May be more or less than $50^\circ C$ depending upon the size of rods

subject: Physics

question: Two identical rods $AC$ and $CB$ made of two different metals having thermal conductivities in the ratio $2: 3$ are kept in contact with each other at the end $C$as shown in the figure. $A$ is at $100^\circ C$ and $B$ is at $25^\circ C$. Then the junction $C$ is at
explanation:
Rate of heat conduction
$i=K A \dfrac{\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{d}$
Let temperature of junction $=\theta$
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad & \dfrac{K_{1} A(100-\theta)}{d}=\dfrac{K_{2} A(\theta-25)}{d} \\& \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{\theta-25}{100-\theta} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2}{3}=\dfrac{\theta-25}{100-\theta}\end{aligned}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 55^\circ C.$
subject: Physics

question: In the figure $A B C$ is a conducting rod whose lateral surfaces are insulated. The length of the section $AB$ is one - half of that of $BC$, and the respective thermal conductivities of the two sections are as given in the figure. If the ends $A$ and $C$ are maintained at $0^\circ C$ and $70^\circ C$ respectively, the temperature of junction $B$ in the steady state is
explanation:
Heat currents in both the rods are equal
or ${H_{CB}} = {H_{BA}}$
or $\frac{{{\theta _C} - {\theta _B}}}{{(2l/3KA)}} = \frac{{{\theta _B} - {\theta _A}}}{{(l/3KA)}}$
$\therefore \frac{3}{2}\left( {70 - {\theta _B}} \right) = 2\left( {{\theta _B} - 0} \right)$
or ${\theta _B} = 30^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: Three rods made of the same material and having same cross - sectional area but different length $10\,cm,20\,cm$ and $30\,cm$ are joined as shown. The temperature of the junction is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ be the temperature of junction. ${i_1},{i_2}$, and ${i_3}$ are heat currents. Then ${i_1} = {i_2} + {i_3}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955821/preptracker/questions/q_37718_explanation_1764955821109.png
${\rm{or }}\frac{{30 - \theta }}{{(30/KA)}} = \frac{{\theta - 20}}{{(20/KA)}} + \frac{{\theta - 10}}{{(10/KA)}}$
${\rm{ or }}2(30 - \theta ) = 3(\theta - 20) + (\theta - 10){\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}\theta = 16.36^\circ C \approx \theta = 16.4^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: $A,B$ and $C$ are the three identical conductors but made from different materials. They are kept in contact as shown. Their thermal conductivities are $K,2\,K$ and $K/2$. The free end of $A$ is at $100^\circ C$ and the free end of $C$ is at $0^\circ C$. During steady state, the temperature of the junction of $A$ and $B$ is nearly____ $^\circ C$.
explanation:
Given that ${K_A} = K,{K_B} = 2\,K,\,{K_C} = \frac{K}{2}$
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_21_cea5da00d60797f1a52fg-08.jpg?height=177&width=449&top_left_y=1032&top_left_x=1306
Let $L$ be the length and A be the area of cross-section of each conductor respectively. Let ${T_1}$ and ${T_2}$ be temperatures of $AB$ and $BC$ junctions respectively.
At the steady state, ${i_A} = {i_B} = {i_C}$
For the junction $AB$
$\frac{{{K_A}A\left( {100 - {T_1}} \right)}}{L} = \frac{{{K_B}A\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{L}$
$\frac{{KA\left( {100 - {T_1}} \right)}}{L} = \frac{{2KA\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)}}{L}$
$\therefore \left( {100 - {T_1}} \right) = 2\left( {\,{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)$
$100 - {T_1} = 2\,{T_1} - 2\,{T_2};2\,{T_2} = 3\,{T_1} - 100\,$
${T_2} = \frac{3}{2}\,{T_1} - 50\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For the junction $BC$
$\dfrac{K_{B} A\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{L}=\dfrac{K_{C} A\left(T_{2}-0\right)}{L}$
$\dfrac{2 K A\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{L}=\dfrac{(K / 2) A\left(T_{2}\right)}{L}$
$ \Rightarrow 4{T_1} = 5{T_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's 1 & 2 we get
${T_1} = \frac{{500^\circ C}}{7} = 71^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: On heating one end of a rod, the temperature of whole rod will be uniform when
explanation:
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dT}} = - KA\frac{{d\theta }}{{dx}};\,{\rm{when}}\,K = \infty ,\frac{{d\theta }}{{dx}} = 0$
i.e., $\theta$ is independent of $x$ i.e., constant or uniform
subject: Physics

question: The two ends of a rod of length $L$ and a uniform cross-section area $A$ are kept at two temperatures $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}\left(T_{1}>T_{2}\right)$. The rate of heat transfer, $\dfrac{d Q}{d t}$, through the rod in a steady state is given by
explanation:
In steady state the rate of heat flowing from one face to the other face $t$ is given by
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{K A\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{L}$
subject: Physics

question: Four rods with different radii $r$ and length $l$ are used to connect two heat reservoirs at different temperatures. Which one will conduct most heat?
explanation:
Rate of heat conduction through a rod of radius $r$ and length $l$,
$i=\dfrac{K\left(\pi r^{2}\right)\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)}{l}$
For given $K\,{\rm{and}}\,\left( {{T_2} - {T_1}} \right),i \propto \frac{{{r^2}}}{l}$
Hence, rod with $r = 2\,cm$ and $l = \frac{1}{2}\,m$ will conduct more heat.
subject: Physics

question: The thickness of a metallic plate is $0.4\,cm$. The temperature between its two surface is $20^\circ C$. The quantity of heat flowing per second is 50 $cal$ from $5\,c{m^2}$ area. In $CGS$ system, the coefficient of thermal conductivity will be
explanation:
$\frac{Q}{t} = \frac{{KA(\Delta \theta )}}{l} \Rightarrow 50 = \frac{{5 \times 20\,K}}{{0.4}}$
$ \Rightarrow K = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$
subject: Physics

question: If a body coated at $600\,K$ surrounded by atmosphere at $300\,K$ has cooling rate $r_{0}$, the same body at $900\,K$ surrounded by the same atmosphere, will have cooling rate equal to
explanation:
Cooling rate $ \propto {T^4} - T_0^4$
$\begin{aligned}& r=\left\{\dfrac{(900)^{4}-(300)^{4}}{(600)^{4}-(300)^{4}}\right\} r_{0} \\& =\dfrac{16}{3} r_{0}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A spherical black body with radius $12\,cm$ radiates $640\,W$ power at $500\,K$. If the radius is halved and the temperature doubled, the power radiated in watts would be
subject: Physics

question: The total radiant energy per unit area normal to the direction of incidence, received at a distance $R$  from the centre of a star of radius $r$, whose outer surface radiates as a black body at a temeprature $TK$ is given by (where $\sigma$ is Stefan's constant)
explanation:
In reaching a distance $R$ this energy will be spread over a sphere of radius $R$, so the intensity of radiation will be given by
$S=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\dfrac{4 \pi r^{2} \sigma T^{4}}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\dfrac{r^{2} \sigma T^{4}}{R^{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Assuming the sun to have a spherical outer surface of radius $r$, radiating like a black body at temperature $t^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the power received by a unit surface (normal to the incident rays) at a distance $R$ from the centre of the sun is
explanation:
From Stefan's law, the rate at which energy is
radiated by sun at its surface is
$\begin{aligned}I=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\dfrac{\sigma \times 4 \pi r^{2} T^{4}}{4 \pi R^{2}}=\dfrac{\sigma r^{2} T^{4}}{R^{2}} \\\Rightarrow I=\dfrac{\sigma r^{2}(t+273)^{4}}{R^{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A black rectangular surfae of area $A$ emits energy $E$ per second at $27^\circ C$. If length and breadth are reduced to $\dfrac{1}{3}^{\text {rd }}$ of initial value and temperature is raised to $327^\circ C$, then energy emitted per second becomes
explanation:
Rate of energy emitted per second from the surface,
$E=e \sigma A T^{4}$
[ where symbols have their usual meanings]
When $l$ and $b$ change to $\dfrac{l}{3}$ and $\dfrac{b}{3}$,
$A^{\prime}=\dfrac{l b}{9}=\dfrac{A}{9}[\because A=l b]$
Now for two different cases
$\dfrac{E^{\prime}}{E}=\dfrac{A^{\prime}}{A} \dfrac{(227+373)^{4}}{(27+273)^{4}}=\dfrac{1}{9}\left(\dfrac{600}{300}\right)^{4}$
$\therefore E^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{9} \times(2)^{4} \times E \Rightarrow E^{\prime}=\dfrac{16 E}{9}$
subject: Physics

question: The wavelength for maximum energy emission by a star is $289.8\,nm$. Then intensity of radiation for the star is (Given : Stefan's constant $ = 5.67 \times {10^{ - 8}}W{m^{ - 2}}\,{K^{ - 4}}$, Wien's constant, $b = 2898\mu \,mK$
explanation:
Given, wavelength for maximum energy emission
${\lambda _m} = 289.8\,nm = 2898 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m$
Stefan's constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times {10^{ - 8}}W{m^{ - 2}}\,{K^{ - 4}}$
Wien's constant $b = 2898\mu mK = 2898 \times {10^{ - 6}}mK$ According to Wien's displacement law, $\lambda_{m}$ is given by
${\lambda _m} = \frac{b}{T} \Rightarrow T = \frac{b}{{{\lambda _m}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Substituting given values in eq.(1), we get
$T = \frac{{2898 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{2898 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}} = {10^4}\,K\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
According to Stefan's law, the energy radiated from a source is given by
$E = \sigma Ae{T^4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Where, $A = $ area of source
$\mathrm{e}=\text { emissivity }$
The intensity of radiation emitted
$I=\dfrac{E}{A}=\sigma e T^{4} \quad \text { [from eq.(3)] }$
Assuming the star to be a blackbody radiator, $e = 1$.
$ \Rightarrow I = \sigma {T^4}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
Substituting the value of $T$ from eq.(2) in eq.(4) we get,
$I = \sigma {\left( {{{10}^4}} \right)^4} = 5.67 \times {10^{ - 8}} \times {10^{16}} = 5.67 \times {10^8}W{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two spherical black bodies of radius $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ with surface temperature $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ respectively, radiate same power, then $r_{1}: r_{2}$ is
explanation:
The power radiated by the black body
$E=\sigma A T^{4}$
It is given that, both spherical bodies radiate same power
$\therefore E_{1}=E_{2}$
$T_{1}^{4} r_{1}^{2}=T_{2}^{4} r_{2}^{2}\left(\because\right.$ for spherical body, $\left.A=4 \pi r^{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A sphere, a cube and a thin circular plate all of same material and same mass initially heated to same high temperature are allowed to cool down under similar conditions. Then the
explanation: The rate of heat transfer is proportional to surface area. For a given fixed volume of material, the plate has the largest surface area and the sphere has the smallest surface area. So the circular plate will cool fastest and sphere the slowest.

options.text: Plate will cool the fastest and sphere the slowest
Cube will cool the fastest and plate the slowest
Plate will cool the fastest and cube the slowest
Sphere will cool the fastest and cube the slowest.

subject: Physics

question: Heat energy is incident on the surface at the rate of $1000\,J/\min $. If coefficient of absorption is 0.8 and coefficient of reflection is 0.1, then heat energy transmitted by the surface in $5\,{\rm{min}}$ is
explanation:
Given that, rate of incident energy $\left( {\mathop {{\rm{ }}E}\limits^. } \right) = 1000J/\min $
We know that,
$1 = a + r + t$
Here, coefficient of absorption $\left( a \right) = 0.8$
Coeficient of reflection $\left( r \right) = 0.1$, and coefficient of transmission $ = t$
$1 = 0.8 + 0.1 + t$
$ \Rightarrow t = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1$
The amount of transmitted heat energy in 5 min
${Q_t} = E \times t \times \left( {time} \right) = 1000 \times 0.1 \times 5 = 500J$
subject: Physics

question: If a black body emits 0.5 joules of energy per second when it is at $27^\circ C$, then the amount of energy emitted by it when it is at $627^\circ C$ will be
subject: Physics

question: The surface temperature of the sun which has maximum energy emission at $500\,nm$ is $6000\,K$.The temperature of a star which has maximum energy emission at $400\,nm$ will be
explanation:
By Wien's displacement law, ${\lambda _m}T = $constant
$\Rightarrow \lambda_{1} T_{1}=\lambda_{2} T_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {T_2} = \frac{{500 \times 6000}}{{400}} = 7500\,K$
subject: Physics

question: A slab of stone of area of $0.36\,{m^2}$ and thickness $0.1\,m$ is exposed on the lower surface to steam at $100^\circ C$. A block of ice at $0^\circ C$ rests on the upper surface of the slab. In one hour $4.8\,kg$ of ice is melted. The thermal conductivity of slab is (Given latent heat of fusion of ice $ = 3.36 \times {10^5}J\,k{g^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
Rate of heat flow through the slab is $\dfrac{Q}{t}=\dfrac{K A}{L}\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)$
Rate of heat taken by ice $Q=\dfrac{K A}{L}\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) t$
$Q=m L_{f}$
$\dfrac{K A}{L}\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) t=m L_{f}$
$K=\dfrac{m L_{f} L}{A\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) t} \Rightarrow K=\dfrac{4.8 \times 3.36 \times 10^{5} \times 0.1}{0.36 \times 100 \times 3600}$
$ = \frac{{4.8 \times 3.36}}{{0.36 \times 36}} = 1.24\,J/m/s\,^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: In a steady state, the temperature at the end $A$ and $B$ of $20\,cm$ long rod $AB$  are $100^\circ C$ and $0^\circ C$. The temperature of a point $9\,cm$ from $A$ is
explanation:
$H = \frac{{100 - 0}}{{{R_{AB}}}} = {\text{Heat}}\,{\text{current}} = \frac{{100}}{{{R_{AB}}}}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956012/preptracker/questions/q_37625_explanation_1764956011475.png
$100 - {\theta _C} = H \cdot {R_{AC}} = \frac{{100}}{{{R_{AB}}}} \cdot {R_{AC}}$
$ = \frac{{100 \times 9}}{{20}} = 45$
$\therefore {\theta _C} = 55^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of the thermal conductivity of copper is 9 times that of steel. In the compositecylindrical bar shown in the figure, what will be the temperature at the junction of copper and steel?
explanation:
Let $T$ be the temperature of the junction. As rate of heat flow in both rods is same
${i_{cu}} = {i_{steel{\rm{ }}}}$
$\frac{{ - {K_{cu}}A(T - 100)}}{{18\,cm}} = \frac{{ - {K_{steel{\rm{ }}}}A(0 - T)}}{{6\,cm}}$
$\frac{{ - 9{K_{steel{\rm{ }}}}A(T - 100)}}{{18\,cm}} = \frac{{ - {K_{steel{\rm{ }}}}A(0 - T)}}{{6\,cm}}$
$T = 75^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: Three rods of same dimensions have thermal conductivities $3\,K,2\,K$ and $K$. They are arranged as shown, with their ends at $100^\circ C,50^\circ C$ and $0^\circ C$. The temperature of their junction is
explanation:
Suppose $\theta$ be the temperature of junction. ${i_1},{i_2}$, and ${i_3}$ are heat currents. Then ${i_1} = {i_2} + {i_3}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955673/preptracker/questions/q_37617_explanation_1764955673042.png
${\rm{or }}\frac{{100 - 0}}{{(l/3KA)}} = \frac{{0 - 50}}{{(l/KA)}} + \frac{{\theta - 0}}{{(l/KA)}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}3(100 - \theta ) = 2(\theta - 50) + \theta $
Solving we get $\theta = \frac{{200^\circ }}{3}C$
subject: Physics

question: Two slabs are of the thicknesses $d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$. Their thermal conductivities are ${K_1}$ and ${K_2}$ respectively. They are in series. The free ends of the combination of these two slabs are kept at temperatures $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$. Assume $\theta_{1}>\theta_{2}$. The temperature $\theta$ of their common junction is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955669/preptracker/questions/q_37613_explanation_1764955669338.png
For the first slab
Heat current, $i_{1}=\dfrac{K_{1}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta\right) A}{d_{1}}$
For the second slab
Heat current, $i_{2}=\dfrac{K_{2}\left(\theta-\theta_{2}\right) A}{d_{2}}$
As the slabs are connected in series same amount of heat current flows through the slabs. $\therefore i_{1}=i_{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{K_{1}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta\right) A}{d_{1}}=\dfrac{K_{2}\left(\theta-\theta_{2}\right) A}{d_{2}} \\& \theta=\dfrac{K_{1} \theta_{1} d_{2}+K_{2} \theta_{2} d_{1}}{K_{2} d_{1}+K_{1} d_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The unit of thermal conductivity is :
explanation:
$\dfrac{d H}{d t}=\dfrac{K A}{L} \Delta T$ ( $\mathrm{K}=$ coefficient of thermal conductivity)
$\therefore \quad K=\dfrac{L d H}{A d t \Delta T}$
Unit of $K=W m^{-1} K^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question: The power radiated by a black body is $P$ and it radiates maximum energy at wavelength, $\lambda_{0}$. If the temperature of the black body is now changed so that it radiates maximum energy at wavelength $\dfrac{3}{4} \lambda_{0}$, the power radiated by it becomes $n P$. The value of $n$ is
explanation:
Wein's displacement law: $T \propto \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T}=\dfrac{\lambda}{\lambda^{\prime}}=\dfrac{4}{3}$
Power $P \alpha T^{4}$
$\dfrac{P^{\prime}}{P}=\left(\dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T}\right)^{4}=\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{256}{81}$
subject: Physics

question: A black body is at a temperature of $5760\,K$. The energy of radiation emitted by the body at wavelength $250\,nm$ is $U_{1}$, at wavelength $500\,nm$ is $U_{2}$ and that at $1000\,nm$ is $U_{3}$. Wien's constant, $b = 2.88 \times {10^6}nmK$. Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
Maximum amount of emitted radiation corresponding to ${\lambda _m} = \frac{b}{T}$
${\lambda _m} = \frac{{2.88 \times {{10}^6}nmK}}{{5760\,K}} = 500\,nm$
i.e., $U_{2}$ corresponds to maximum value
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955627/preptracker/questions/q_37566_explanation_1764955626998.png
From the graph ${U_1} < {U_2} > {U_3}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Greater the coefficient of thermal conductivity of a material, smaller is the thermal resistance of a rod of that material.


Reason: Thermal resistance is the ratio of temperature difference between the ends of the conductor and rate of flow of heat.
explanation:
Resistance $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { conductivity }}$
Resistance $=\dfrac{\text { heat flow }}{\Delta T}$
So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question:
Assertion: If thermal conductivity of a rod is 5 , then its thermal resistivity is 0.2 .


Reason: Thermal conductivity
$=\dfrac{1}{\text { thermal resistivity }}$
explanation:
$\begin{aligned}\text { Thermal Conductivity } & =\dfrac{1}{\text { themal resistivity }} \\& =\dfrac{1}{0.2}=5\end{aligned}$
So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

In the human body the heart act as the pump that circulates blood through different parts of the body, transferring heat by forced convection.
In forced convection, material is forced to move by a pump or by some other physical means.

Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question:
I. Gases are poor thermal conductors.
II. Liquids have conductivities intermediate between solids and gases.
III. Heat conduction can take place from cold body to hotter body.
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?
explanation: Statement I and II are correct but III is incorrect since heat conduction takes place from hot body to cold body.
subject: Physics

question:
I. Convection is a mode of heat transfer by actual motion of matter.
II. Convection is possible only in gases.
III. Convection can be natural or forced.
Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?
explanation: Statement I and III are correct but II is incorrect since convection is possible in both liquids and gases.
subject: Physics

question: A brass boiler has a base area $0.15\,{m^2}$ and thickness $1.0\,cm$. It boils water at the rate of $6.0\,kg/\min $ when placed on a gas stove. The temperature of the part of the flame in contact with the boiler will be. (Thermal conductivity of brass $ = 109\,J/s - m - K,$ heat of vapourisation of water $ = 2256 \times {10^3}\,J/kg$)
explanation:
Given, base area of boiler $(A) = 0.15\,{m^2}$
Thickness $(d) = 1\,cm = 1 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
Rate of water boils
$ = 6.0\,kg/\min = \frac{{6.0}}{{60}}\,kg/s = 0.1\,kg/s.$
Thermal conductivity of brass
$K=109 J / s-m-K$
Latent heat of vapourization of water
$L = 2256 \times {10^3}\,J/Kg$
Let $\theta_{1}$ be the temperature of the part of the boiler in contact with the stove.
Rate of heat energy supplied
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{K A \Delta \theta}{d}=m L \\& \dfrac{K A\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{d}=m L\end{aligned}$
As the water boils at $100^\circ C$
${\theta _2} = 100\,^\circ C\,{\text{and}}\,{\theta _1} = 238\,^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: Which one of the following is \(v_{m}-T\) graph for perfectly black body? \[{\upsilon _m}\] is the frequency of radiation with maximum intensity. \(T\) is the absolute temperature.
explanation:
From Wien's displacement law
$\therefore T \lambda_{m}=b \Rightarrow T \propto v_{m}$
The graph between $T\,\& \,{\upsilon _m}$ is straight line passing through origin. So option (3) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: Shown below are the black body radiation curves at temperatures \(T_{1}\) and \(T_{2}\left(T_{2}>T_{1}\right)\). Which of the following plots is correct
explanation:
According to Wien's displacement law
$\lambda_{m} \propto \dfrac{1}{T} \Rightarrow \lambda_{m_{2}} < \lambda_{m_{1}}\left[\because T_{1} < T_{2}\right]$
There fore $I-\lambda$ graph for $T_{2}$ has lesser wavelength $\left(\lambda_{m}\right)$ and so curve for $T_{2}$ will shift towards left side.
subject: Physics

question: A long metallic bar is carrying heat from one of its ends to the other end under steady-state. The variation of temperature \(\theta\) along the length \(x\) of the bar from its hot end is best described by which of the following figures?
explanation:
Let $\theta_{0}$ is the temperature of the hot end and $\theta$ is the temperature at $a$ distance $x$ from hot end
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{k A\left(\theta_{0}-\theta\right)}{x} \\& \Rightarrow \theta_{0}-\theta=\dfrac{x}{k A} \dfrac{d Q}{d t} \Rightarrow \theta=\theta_{0}-\dfrac{x}{k A} \dfrac{d Q}{d t}\end{aligned}$
Hence $\dfrac{d Q}{d t}$ is constant throughout the rod. The graph between $\theta$ and $x$ is a straight line.
subject: Physics

question: The plots of intensity versus wavelength for three black bodies at temperatures \(T_{1}, T_{2}\), and \(T_{3}\) respectively are as shown. Their temperature are such that
explanation:
According to Wien's law $\lambda_{m} \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$ and from the figure
$\left(\lambda_{m}\right)_{1} < \left(\lambda_{m}\right)_{3} < \left(\lambda_{m}\right)_{2}$ therefore $T_{1}>T_{3}>T_{2}$.
subject: Physics

question: A ball of surface temperature \(T\) is in thermal equilibrium with its environment. Which of the curve gives the energy \(E\) radiated by the sphere as a function of time ' \[t\] '?
explanation:
Temperature does not change when a body is in thermal equilibrium. Hence energy radiated remains same.
so option (1) is correct.

The level of alcohol in limb $X$ falls while that in limb $Y$ rises.

The level of alcohol falls in both limbs.

subject: Physics

question:
A solid copper cube of edges $1\,cm$each is suspended in an evacuated enclosure. Its temperature is found to fall from
$100^\circ C$ to $99^\circ C$ in 100 s. Another solid copper cube of edges $2\,cm$, with similar surface nature, is suspended in a similar manner. The timer required for this cube to cool from $100^\circ C$ to $99^\circ C$ will be approximately
explanation:
Rate of
${\rm{cooling }} = \frac{{\Delta \theta }}{t} = \frac{{A\varepsilon \sigma \left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)}}{{mc}}$
$ \Rightarrow t\alpha \frac{m}{A}\left[ {\Delta \theta ,t,\sigma ,\left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right){\text{are}}\,{\text{constant}}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow t\alpha \frac{m}{A}\alpha \frac{{{\rm{ density }} \times {\rm{ Volume }}}}{{{\rm{ Area }}}}\alpha \frac{{{\ell ^3}}}{{{\ell ^2}}} \Rightarrow t\alpha \ell $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{t_1}}}{{{t_2}}} = \frac{{{\ell _1}}}{{{\ell _2}}}$
$\frac{{100}}{{{t_2}}} = \frac{{1\,cm}}{{2\,cm}} \Rightarrow {t_2} = 200\sec $
subject: Physics

question: A sphere and a cube of same material and same total surface area are placed in the same evacuated space turn by turn after they are heated to the same temperature. Find the ratio of their initial rates of cooling in the enclosure.
explanation:
Rate of emission of energy $=\sigma T^{4} S$
Let $m_{1}$ be the mass of sphere, $C$ is the specific heat and $(d \theta / d t)$, the rate of cooling.
For sphere
$\sigma {T^4}A = {m_1}C{\left( {\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}}} \right)_s}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Let $m_{2}$ be the mass of cube, $C$ is its specific heat and $(d \theta / d t)$, the rate of cooling.
For cube
$\sigma {T^4}A = {m_2}C{\left( {\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}}} \right)_c}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$\dfrac{(d \theta / d t)_{s}}{(d \theta / d t)_{c}}=\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}$
We know that
$\dfrac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}=\dfrac{a^{3} \rho}{(4 / 3) \pi r^{3} \rho}=\dfrac{R_{s}}{R_{c}}$
Where $a$ is the side of cube and $r$ is the radius of sphere, $\rho$ is the density.
$\therefore \dfrac{R_{s}}{R_{c}}=\dfrac{3 a^{3}}{4 \pi r^{3}}$
Since Area ($A$) is same,
$\begin{aligned}& 6 a^{2}=4 \pi r^{2} \Rightarrow a^{2}=(2 / 3) \pi r^{2} \\& \therefore \dfrac{R_{s}}{R_{c}}=\dfrac{3\left(2 \pi r^{2} / 3\right)^{3 / 2}}{4 \pi r^{3}}=\dfrac{2 \pi \sqrt{2 \pi}}{\sqrt{3}(4 \pi)}\end{aligned}$
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \pi}{12}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{6}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical metal balls at temperature $200^\circ C$ and $400^\circ C$ kept in air at $27^\circ C$. The ratio of net heat loss by these bodies is:
subject: Physics

question: The surface temperature of the sun is $T_{0}$ and it is at average distance $d$ from a planet. The radius of the sun is $R$. The temperature of the planet at which the energy is radiated
explanation:
Let $r$ is radius of planet
$T_{1}=$ Temperature of planet at which energy is radiated
Power radiated by Sun, $P=4 \pi R^{2} \sigma T_{0}^{4}$
Power received by planet
$=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi r^{2}=\dfrac{R^{2} T_{0}^{4} \pi r^{2} \sigma}{d^{2}}$
Power radiated by planet $=4 \pi r^{2} \sigma T_{1}^{4}$
For thermal equilibrium,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{R^{2} T_{0}^{4} \pi r^{2} \sigma}{d^{2}}=4 \pi r^{2} \sigma T_{1}^{4} \\& R^{2} T_{0}^{4}=4 d^{2} T_{1}^{4} \\& T_{1}=T_{0} \sqrt{\dfrac{R}{2 d}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

Rough surfaces are better radiators than smooth surface
Highly polished mirror like surfaces are very good radiators
