question: The temperature of the two outer surface of a composite slab, consisting of two meterials having coefficient of thermal conductivity $K$ and $2 K$ and thickness $x$ and $4 x$, respectively are $T_{2}$ and $T_{1}\left(T_{2}>T_{1}\right)$. The rate of heat transfer through the slab, in a steady state $i$ $s\left( {\frac{{A\left( {{T_2} - {T_1}} \right)K}}{x}} \right)f$, the value of $f$ is
explanation:
The equivalent thermal conductivity is
$R = {R_1} + {R_2}$
$\frac{{{l_1} + {l_2}}}{{{K_{eq}}A}} = \frac{{{l_1}}}{{{K_1}A}} + \frac{{{l_2}}}{{{K_2}A}}$
${K_{eq}} = \frac{{{l_1} + {l_2}}}{{\frac{{{l_1}}}{{{K_1}}} + \frac{{{l_2}}}{{{K_2}}}}} = \frac{{x + 4x}}{{\frac{x}{K} + \frac{{4x}}{{2K}}}} = \frac{5}{3}K.$
Hence rate of flow of heat through the given combination is
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{Q}{t} & =\dfrac{K_{e q} \cdot A\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)}{(x+4 x)}=\dfrac{\dfrac{5}{3} K A\left(T_{2}-T_{1}\right)}{5 x} \\& =\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{3} K A\left(T_{2}-T\right)_{1}}{x}\end{aligned}$
On comparing it with given equation we get $f=\dfrac{1}{3}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Two plates of same thickness, of coefficients of thermal conductivity $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$ and areas of cross section $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ are connected as shown in the figure. The common coefficient of thermal conductivity $K$ wil be
explanation:
As it is clear from figure.
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{d Q_{1}}{d t}+\dfrac{d Q_{2}}{d t} \\& \dfrac{K\left(A_{1}+A_{2}\right) d T}{d x}=K_{1} A_{1} \dfrac{d T}{d x}+K_{2} A_{2} \dfrac{d Q}{d x} \\& K=\dfrac{K_{1} A_{1}+K_{2} A_{2}}{A_{1}+A_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Consider two insulating sheets with thermal resistance $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ as shown in the figure. The temperature $\theta$ is
explanation:
For the two sheets, shown in figure, rate of heat transfer is same, i,e.,
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d Q_{1}}{d t}=\dfrac{d Q_{2}}{d t} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta}{R_{1}}=\dfrac{\theta-\theta_{2}}{R_{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \theta_{1} R_{2}-\theta R_{2}=\theta R_{1}-\theta_{2} R_{1} \\& \theta=\dfrac{\theta_{1} R_{2}+\theta_{2} R_{1}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Three identical rods $A, B$ and $C$ of equal lengths and equal diameter are joined in series as shown in the figure. Their thermal conductivites are $2\,K,{\rm{ }}K$ and $K/2$ respectively. Calculate the temperature at two junction points
explanation:
The rate of heat flow is same in all the three rods.
$\frac{{\left( {100 - {T_1}} \right)2KA}}{l} = \frac{{\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)KA}}{l} = \frac{{\left( {{T_2} - 0} \right)KA}}{{2l}}$
$2\left( {100 - {T_1}} \right) = \left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right) = \frac{{{T_2}}}{2}$
${T_1} = 85.7^\circ C\,{\rm{and}}\,\, = \frac{{2{T_1}}}{3} = 57.1^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: Two rectangular blocks, having identical dimensions, can be arranged either in configuration $I$ or in configuration II as shown in the figure, one of the blocks has thermal conductivity $K$ and the other $2K$. The temperature difference between the two ends along the $x$ axis is the same in both the configurations. It takes 9 min to transport a certain amount of heat from the hot end to the cold end in the configuration I. The time to transport the same amount of heat in the configuration II is
explanation:
In configuration I
total thermal resistance
$R_{1}=\dfrac{\ell}{K A}+\dfrac{\ell}{2 K A}=\dfrac{3}{2} \dfrac{\ell}{K A}$
In configuration II
$R_{2}=\left(\dfrac{1 \times \dfrac{1}{2}}{1+\dfrac{1}{2}}\right) \dfrac{\ell}{K A}=\dfrac{1}{3} \dfrac{\ell}{K A}$
We have $Q=\dfrac{(\Delta T)}{R} t$
As $Q$ is constant
$\begin{aligned}& t \propto R \\& \dfrac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}=\dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}} \Rightarrow t_{2}=\dfrac{2}{9} \times 9=2 \mathrm{~min} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: A hollow spherical ball of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $2 a$ is made of a uniform material of constant thermal conductivity $K$. The temperature within the ball is maintained at $2 T_{0}$ and outside the ball is $T_{0}$. Find the temperature at $r = \frac{{3a}}{2}$
explanation:
In steady state, the net outward heat flow is constant, and does not depend on radial position.
Let $c_{1}=i=-K\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right) \dfrac{d T}{d r}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dT}}{{dr}} = - \frac{{{c_1}}}{{4\pi K{r^2}}}$
$\int_{2{T_0}}^T d T = - \frac{{{c_1}}}{{4\pi K}}\int_a^r {\frac{1}{{{r^2}}}} dr$
$T - 2{T_0} = \frac{{{c_1}}}{{4\pi K}}\left[ {\frac{1}{r} - \frac{1}{a}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When $r=2 a \Rightarrow T=T_{0}$
${T_0} - 2{T_0} = \frac{{{c_1}}}{{4\pi K}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{2a}} - \frac{1}{a}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's (1) & (2) we get
$T=\dfrac{4 T_{0}}{3} \text { at } r=\dfrac{3 a}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: Ice starts forming in lake with water at $0^\circ C$ and when the atmospheric temperature is $ - 10^\circ C$. If the time taken for $1\,cm$ of ice be 7 hours. Find the time taken for the thickness of ice to change from $1\,cm\,{\rm{to}}\,2\,cm.$
explanation:
The time required to increase the thickness of ice from $x_{1}$ to $x_{2}$
$t=\dfrac{\rho L}{2 K \theta}\left(x_{2}^{2}-x_{1}^{2}\right) \Rightarrow t \propto\left(x_{2}^{2}-x_{1}^{2}\right)$
Where $\theta$ is the temperature of atmosphere
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{t}{t^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\left(x_{2}^{2}-x_{1}^{2}\right)}{\left(x_{1}^{\prime 2}-x_{2}^{\prime 2}\right)} \Rightarrow \dfrac{7}{t^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\left(1^{2}-0^{2}\right)}{\left(2^{2}-1^{2}\right)} \Rightarrow t^{\prime}=21$ hours.
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following circular rods, (given radius $r$ and lenght $l$) each made of the same material and whose ends are maintained at the same temperature will conduct more heat?
explanation:
Heat conduction through a rod is
$H = \frac{{\Delta Q}}{{\Delta t}} = KA\left( {\frac{{{T_1} - {T_2}}}{l}} \right) \Rightarrow H \propto \frac{{{r^2}}}{l}\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
(1) When $r=2 r_{0}, l=2 l_{0}$
$H \propto \dfrac{\left(2 r_{0}\right)^{2}}{2 l_{0}} \Rightarrow H \propto \dfrac{2 r_{0}^{2}}{l_{0}}$
(2) When $r=2 r_{0}, l=l_{0}$
$H \propto \dfrac{\left(2 r_{0}\right)^{1}}{l_{0}} \Rightarrow H \propto \dfrac{4 r_{0}^{2}}{l_{0}}$
(3) When $r=r_{0}, l=l_{0}$
$H \propto \dfrac{r_{0}^{2}}{l_{0}}$
(4) When $r=r_{0}, l=2 l_{0}$
$H \propto \dfrac{r_{0}^{2}}{2 l_{0}}$
So heat conduction will be more in case (2)
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of thermal conductivity of two rods of different material is $5: 4$. The two rods of same area of cross-section and same thermal resistance will have the lengths in the ratio.
explanation:
Given $A_{1}=A_{2}$ and $\dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{5}{4}$
$\because R_{1}=R_{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{K_{1} A}=\dfrac{l_{2}}{K_{2} A} \Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{5}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: The temperature of an isolated black body falls from $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ in time $t$. Let $c$ be a constant.
explanation:
$P=\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)\left(\sigma T^{4}\right)=m s\left(-\dfrac{d T}{d t}\right)$
Where $m$ is the mass and $s$ is the specific heat of the body
$\begin{gathered}4 \pi r^{2} \sigma T^{4}=-\dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \rho s \cdot \dfrac{d T}{d t} \\\dfrac{d T}{d t}=\dfrac{-3 \sigma T^{4}}{\rho s r} \\\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}}-\dfrac{d T}{T^{4}}=(\text { constant }) \int_{0}^{t} d t \\\text { or } \quad c\left[\dfrac{1}{T_{2}^{3}}-\dfrac{1}{T_{1}^{3}}\right]=t\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: The surface temperature of the sun is $T{\mkern 1mu} K$ and the solar constant for a planet is $S$. The sun subtends an angle $2 \theta$ at the planet. Then:
explanation:
Let radius of sun $ = R$
Distance of the earth from the sun $ = d$ Power radiated from the sun
$=\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right) \sigma T^{4}=P$
Energy received per unit area is $S=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =4 \pi R^{2} \sigma \dfrac{T^{4}}{4 \pi d^{2}}=\sigma T^{4} \dfrac{R^{2}}{d^{2}} \\& =\dfrac{1}{4} \sigma T^{4}\left(\dfrac{2 R}{d}\right)^{2}\end{aligned}$
Angle subtend by sun at the earth,
$\begin{gathered}\quad \theta=\dfrac{2 R}{d} \\S=\text { constant } \times T^{4} \times \theta^{2} \\S \alpha \theta^{2}\end{gathered}$
$S\,\alpha \,{\theta ^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length $1.0\,m$ and radius ${10^{ - 3}}\,m$ is carrying a heavy current and it is assumed to radiate as a black body. At equilibrium its temperature is $900\,K$ while that of the surroundings is $300\,K$. The resistivity of the material of the wire at $300\,K$ is ${\pi ^2} \times {10^{ - 8}}ohm - m$ and its temperature coefficient of resistance is $7.8 \times 10^{-3} C^{-1}$. Find the current in the wire. Given $\sigma  = 5.68 \times {10^{ - 8}}W{m^{ - 2}}\,{K^{ - 4}}$.
subject: Physics

question: The tungsten filament of an electric lamp has a surface area $A$ and a power rating $P$. If the emissivity of the filament is $\varepsilon$ and $\sigma$ is Stefan's constant, the steady temperature of the filament will be
explanation:
At steady state condition
$\begin{aligned}P & =\varepsilon \sigma A T^{4} \\T^{4} & =\dfrac{P}{\varepsilon \sigma A} \Rightarrow T=\left(\dfrac{P}{A \sigma \varepsilon}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A particular star (assuming it as a black body) has a surface temperature of about $5 \times {10^4}\,K$. The wavelength ( in nanometers ) at which its radiation becomes maximum is $(b = 0.0029\,m - K)$
explanation:
According to Wien's displacement law
${\lambda _m} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{{0.0029}}{{5 \times {{10}^4}}} = 58 \times {10^{ - 9}} = 58\,nm$
subject: Physics

question: We consider the radiation emitted by the human body. Which of the following statements is true
explanation: Every body at all time, at all temperature emits radiation (except at $T = 0$ ). The radiation emitted by the human body is in the infra-red region.

options.text: The radiation emitted is in the infra-red region
The radiation is emitted only during the day
The radiation emitted lies in the ultraviolet region and hence is not visible
The radiation is emitted during the summers and absorbed during the winters.

subject: Physics

question: A cylinder $A$ of a material of thermal conductivity $K_{1}$ is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius equal to the cylinder. The shell is made of a material of thermal conductivity $K_{2}$. The two ends of the combined system are maintained at two different temperatures. If the effective thermal conductivity of the system is $\dfrac{4 K_{1}+5 K_{2}}{9}$, find the ratio of outer radius of the shell to the radius of the cylinder.
explanation:
Radius of cylinder $=R$
Outer radius of shell $ = nR$
$R_{e q}=\dfrac{R_{1} R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{l}{K_{1} \pi R^{2}} \times \dfrac{l}{K_{2}\left(n^{2}-1\right) \pi R^{2}}}{\dfrac{l}{K_{1} \pi R^{2}}+\dfrac{l}{K_{2}\left(n^{2}-1\right) \pi R^{2}}}$
Where $l$ is the length of the cylinders.
But $R_{e q}=\dfrac{l}{K \pi n^{2} R^{2}}$
On solving, $K=\dfrac{\left[K_{1}+K_{2}\left(n^{2}-1\right)\right]}{n^{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{4 K_{1}+5 K_{2}}{9}=\dfrac{K_{1}+5 K_{2} / 4}{9 / 4} \\& n=3 / 2\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: Six identical metallic rods are joined together in a pattern as shown in the figure. Points $A$ and $D$ are maintained at temperature $60^\circ C$ and $240^\circ C$. The temperature of the junction $B$ will be
explanation:
Thermal resistance of a rod is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956956/preptracker/questions/q_37102_explanation_1764956955607.png
$R = \frac{l}{{KA}}$
The equivalent resistance between $B$ and $C$ is As rate of heat flow is same
$\frac{{{T_A} - {T_B}}}{R} = \frac{{{T_B} - {T_C}}}{R} = \frac{{{T_C} - {T_D}}}{R}$
$60 - {T_B} = {T_B} - {T_C}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${T_C} - 240 = {T_B} - {T_C}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Solving (1) and (2)
${T_B} = 120^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: The figure shows a system of two concentric spheres of radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ and kept at temperatures $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ respectively. The radial rate of flow of heat in a substance between the two concentric spheres is proportional to
explanation:
Consider a concentric spherical shell of radius $r$ and thickness $d r$ as shown in the figure. The radial rate of flow of heat through this shell in steady state will be
$\begin{gathered}H=\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=-K A \dfrac{d T}{d r} \\=-K\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right) \dfrac{d T}{d r} \\\Rightarrow \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} \dfrac{d r}{r^{2}}=-\dfrac{4 \pi K}{H} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} d T \\\ddots\end{gathered}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954294/preptracker/questions/q_37098_explanation_1764954293786.png
Which on integration and simplification gives
$H=\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{4 \pi K r_{1} r_{2}\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{r_{2}-r_{1}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d Q}{d t} \propto \dfrac{r_{1} r_{2}}{\left(r_{2}-r_{1}\right)}$
subject: Physics

question: In winter atmospheric temperature falls below $0^\circ C$ and water in the lake start freezing. Let at time $t$ thickness of ice on the surface of the lake is $y$ and air temperature $-\theta$. The temperature of water in contact with the lower surface of ice is $0^\circ C$. The area of the lake is $A$ and latent heat of fusion of ice is $L$. The thickness of ice $y$ as a function of $t$ is
explanation:
When water gets converted into ice heat liberates and this heat flows through ice slab.
The heat flow through the ice slab of thickness
$y$ is $\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=K A \dfrac{[\theta-(-\theta)]}{y}$
The small heat $dQ$ comes when water converts into ice
$\begin{aligned}& d Q=d m L=(\rho A d y) L \\& \dfrac{\rho L}{K \theta} \int_{0}^{y} y d y=\int_{0}^{t} d t \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\rho L}{K \theta} y^{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A metal plate $4\,mm$ thick has a temperature difference of $32^\circ C$ between its faces. It transmits $200\,kcal/h$ through an area of $5\,c{m^2}$. Thermal conductivity of the material is
explanation:
Here, $\Delta x = 4mm = 4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m,\Delta T = 32^\circ C$
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dT}} = 200kcal/h = \frac{{200 \times 1000 \times 4.2}}{{60 \times 60}}\,J/s$
$ = 233.33\,J/s$
$A = 5\,c{m^2} = 5 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$
We know that, $\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=K A\left(\dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}\right)$
$\therefore$ Thermal conductivity of material,
$K = \frac{{\Delta Q/\Delta T}}{{A(\Delta T/\Delta x)}}$
$K = \frac{{233.33 \times 4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 32}} = 58.33\,W/m - \,^\circ C.$
subject: Physics

question: There are two identical vessels filled with equal amounts of ice. The vessels are of different metals. If the ice melts in the two vessels in 20 and 35 minutes respectively, the ratio of the coefficients of thermal conductivity of the two metals is
explanation:
The heat required to melt ice is
$\begin{aligned}& Q=\dfrac{K A\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{l} t \Rightarrow K_{1} t_{1}=K_{2} t_{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}=\dfrac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}=\dfrac{35}{20}=\dfrac{7}{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A black body is heated from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The ratio of radiations emitted will be:-
explanation:
Here, initial temperature,
${T_1} = 27^\circ C = 300\,K$
Final temperature, ${T_2} = 927^\circ C = 1200\,K$
According to Stefan's law, the radiant energy is $E \alpha T^{4}$
Here, $\dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\dfrac{T_{1}^{4}}{T_{2}^{4}}$
$=\left(\dfrac{300}{1200}\right)^{4}=\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{1}{256}$
Hence, $E_{1}: E_{2}=1: 256$
subject: Physics

question: A sphere of $3\,cm$ radius acts like a black body. If it is in equilibrium with its surroundings and absorb $30k$watt of energy from the surrounding. Then the temperature of the sphere will be:
explanation:
Radius of sphere $r = 3\,cm = 0.03\,m$
The energy absorbed
$E = 30\,kW = 30 \times {10^3}watt$
Energy absorbed by black body when the temperature of surroundings is $T$, is given by
$E = \sigma A{T^4}{\text{ }}$
${\text{or }}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} 30 \times {10^3} = 5.672 \times {10^{ - 8}} \times 4\pi {(0.03)^2} \times {T^4}$
$30 \times {10^3} = 0.064 \times {10^{ - 8}} \times {T^4}{T^4} = \frac{{30 \times {{10}^3}}}{{0.064 \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}}$
$ = 46.875 \times {10^{12}}$
$ = 2.616 \times {10^3} = 2616\,K$
subject: Physics

question: If the temperature of the sun were to be increased from $T$ to $2\,T$ and its radius from $ R$ to $2 R$, then the ratio of the radiant energy received on the earth to what it was previously will be
explanation:
$Q \propto r^{2} T^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{4}$
$=(2)^{2} \times(2)^{4}=64 .$
subject: Physics

question: Suppose the sun expands so that its radius becomes 100 times its present radius and its surface temperature becomes half of its present value. The total energy emitted by it then will increase by a factor of:
explanation:
From Stefan's law, the emissive power of a body at absolute temperature $T$ is
$E=e \sigma T^{4} A$
When $R^{\prime}=100 R$ and $T^{\prime}=\dfrac{T}{2}$ then energy emitted is
$\begin{aligned}& E^{\prime}=\sigma 4 \pi(100 R)^{2}\left(\dfrac{T}{2}\right)^{4} \\& \therefore E^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{100}{4}\right)^{2} \times E \\& \therefore \dfrac{E^{\prime}}{E}=625\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two black metallic spheres of radius $4 m$, at $2000\,k$ and $1\,m$ at $4000\,k$ will have ratio of energy radiation as
subject: Physics

question: A man has total surface area of $1.5\,{m^2}$. If the temperature of his body is $310\,K$ then find the total rate of radiation of energy from the body.
subject: Physics

question: If the temperature of a hot body is increased by $50 \%$ then the increase in the quantity of emitted heat radiation will be
explanation:
$Q \propto T^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{4}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{T}{T+T / 2}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{16}{81} \Rightarrow Q_{2}=\dfrac{81}{16} Q_{1}$
$\%$ increase in energy $=\dfrac{Q_{2}-Q_{1}}{Q_{1}} \times 100=400 \%$.
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of radiant energies radiated per unit surface area by the two bodies is $16: 1$, the temperature of hotter body is $1000\,K$, then the temperature of colder body will be
explanation:
$Q \propto T^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\dfrac{T_{1}^{4}}{T_{2}^{4}} \Rightarrow T_{2}^{4}=\left(\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}\right) T_{1}^{4}$
$ \Rightarrow T_2^4 = \frac{1}{{16}} \times {(1000)^4} = {\left( {\frac{{1000}}{2}} \right)^4}$
$ \Rightarrow {T_2} = 500\,K$
subject: Physics

question: A black body radiates energy at the rate of $E W / m^{2}$ at a high temperature $T$. When the temperature is reduced to $\dfrac{T}{2}$, the radiant energy will be
explanation: $E \propto T^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\dfrac{T_{1}^{4}}{T_{2}^{4}}=2^{4} \Rightarrow E_{2}=\dfrac{E}{16}$
subject: Physics

question: At temperature $T$, the power radiated by a body is $Q$ watts. At the temperature $3T$ the power radiated by it will be
explanation:
Power radiation is
$Q\,\alpha \,{T^4}$
$\frac{{{Q_1}}}{{{Q_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{\,{T_2}}}} \right)^4} = {\left( {\frac{T}{{3\,T}}} \right)^4}$
$ \Rightarrow {Q_2} = 81{Q_1} = 81Q$
subject: Physics

question: From stefan's law the radiation emitted by a body depends on
explanation: $\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\sigma A T^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{d Q}{d t} \alpha A$
subject: Physics

question: A hot and a cold body are kept in vacuum separated from each other. Even though the bodies are not in contact temperature changes for both. The correct explanation for the above observations is possible by using:
explanation: Convection and conduction mode of heat transfer require physical contact between the bodies to transfer heat but radiation does not require any medium. As they transfer heat as electromagnetic waves/radiation. This is explained by stefan's law
subject: Physics

question: Heat travels through vacuum by:
explanation: Heat radiations are electromagnetic waves of high wavelength. They can travel in vacuum
subject: Physics

question:
Experimental investigations show that the intensity of solar radiation is maximum for a wavelength $480\,nm$ in the visible region.
Estimate the surface temperature of the sun. (Given, Wien's constant $( = 2.88 \times {10^{ - 3}}mK.)$
explanation:
The Wien's radiation law is given by
$\lambda_{m} T=b$
For the given problem, $b = 2.88 \times {10^{ - 3}}mK$.
Hence, $\lambda_{m} T=2.88 \times 10^{-3}$
$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2.88 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{{\lambda _m}}}$
$ = \frac{{2.88 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{480 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}}} = 6000\,K$
subject: Physics

question: A black body radiated maximum energy around a particular wavelength at a given temperature. If the temperature of the black body is now changed so that it radiates maximum energy around a wavelength $n$ time the original wavelength $(n < 1)$ then power radiated by it will increase by a factor of:
explanation:
From Wien's law
$\begin{aligned}& \lambda_{1} T_{1}=b \\& n \lambda_{1} \cdot T_{2}=b \Rightarrow T_{2}=T_{1} / n \\& P_{1}=C T_{1}^{4} \\& P_{2}=C T_{2}^{4}=C\left(T_{1} / n\right)^{4}=1 / n^{4}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The maximum wavelength of radiation emitted at $2000\,K$  is $4\mu m$. What will be the maximum wavelength of radiation emitted at $2400\,K$
explanation: $\therefore \dfrac{\lambda_{m_{2}}}{\lambda_{m_{1}}}=\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{m_{2}}=\dfrac{2000}{2400} \times 4=3.33 \mu m$.
subject: Physics

subject: Physics

question: The rate of dissipation of heat by a black body at temperature $T$ is $Q$. What will be the rate of dissipation of heat by another body at temperature $2T$ and emissivity 0.25 ?
explanation:
By Stefan's law
$Q=\sigma A T^{4}$
The rate of dissipation of heat by a body of emissivity e is given by
$Q^{\prime}=e \sigma A T^{\prime 4}$
Here, $T^{\prime}=2 T, e=0.25$
$\therefore Q^{\prime}=0.25 \times \sigma(2 T)^{4}=4 \sigma T^{4}=4 Q$
subject: Physics

subject: Physics

explanation: A perfectly black body is a good absorbers of radiations falls on it. So it's absorptive power is 1 .

explanation: When light incident on pin hole enters into the box and suffers successive reflection at the inner wall. At each reflection some energy is absorbed. Hence the ray once it enters the box can never come out and pin hole acts like a perfect black body.

options.text: A pin hole in a box

options.text: The entire parts of the refrigerator is cooled quickly due to convection
The heat gained from the environment is low
The heat gained from the environment is high

question: In an open room, a hot body is placed then heat transfer takes place by
subject: Physics

question: Two identical slabs are connected side by side having thickness $l$ each and area of cross section $A$. Thermal conductivities are $K$ and $2K$. The equivalent thermal resistance is
explanation:
Thermal resistance
$R = \frac{l}{{KA}} + \frac{l}{{2KA}} = \frac{{3l}}{{2KA}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: One end of a thermally insulated rod is kept at a temperature $T_{1}$ and the other at $T_{2}$. The rod is composed of two sections of length $l_{1}$ and $l_{2}$ and thermal conductivities $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$ respectively. The temperature at the interface of the two sections is
explanation:
Let $T$ be the temperature of the interface. As the two sections are in series, the rate of flow of heat in them will be equal.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951718/preptracker/questions/q_36884_explanation_1764951717503.png
where $A$ is the area of cross-section.
$\begin{aligned}& K_{1} A\left(T_{1}-T\right) l_{2}=K_{2} A\left(T-T_{2}\right) l_{1} \\& K_{1} T_{1} l_{2}-K_{1} T l_{2}=K_{2} T l_{1}-K_{2} T_{2} l_{1} \\& \left(K_{2} l_{1}+K_{1} l_{2}\right) T=K_{1} T_{1} l_{2}+K_{2} T_{2} l_{1} \\& T=\dfrac{K_{1} T_{1} l_{2}+K_{2} T_{2} l_{1}}{K_{2} l_{1}+K_{1} l_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two bars of thermal conductivities $K$ and $3 K$ and length $1\,cm$ and $2\,cm$ respectively have equal cross-sectional area, they are joined lengths wise as shown in figure. If the temperature at the ends of this composite bar are $0^\circ C$ and $100^\circ C$ respectively (see figure), then the temperature of the interface is
explanation:
Temperature of interface
$\theta = \frac{{\frac{{{\theta _1}}}{{{R_1}}} + \frac{{{\theta _2}}}{{{R_2}}}}}{{\frac{1}{{{R_1}}} + \frac{1}{{{R_2}}}}}$
${\theta _1} = 0^\circ C,{\theta _2} = 100^\circ C$
${R_1} = \frac{1}{{kA}},{R_2} = \frac{2}{{3kA}}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 60^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A metal rod of length $2\,m$ has crosssectional area $3\,A$ and $2\,A$ as shown in the figure. $100^{\circ}$ and $120^{\circ}$ temperature are maintained at the ends respectively. The temperature at middle point $C$ is
explanation:
Let $\theta$ is the temperature of the mid point
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\left(\theta_{1}-\theta\right) A_{1}}{l_{1}}=\dfrac{\left(\theta-\theta_{2}\right) A_{2}}{l_{2}} \\& \dfrac{(100-\theta) 3 A}{1}=\dfrac{\left(\theta-120^{\circ}\right) 2 A}{1}\end{aligned}$
$300 - 3\theta = 2\theta - 240^\circ $
$300 + 240 = 2\theta + 3\theta \Rightarrow 540 = 5\theta \Rightarrow \theta = 108^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A cylindrical rod having temperature $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ at its ends. The rate of flow of heat is ${Q_1}\,cal/\sec $. If all the linear dimensions are doubled keeping temperature constant then rate of flow of heat $Q_{2}$ will be:
explanation:
Rate of heat flow
$\begin{aligned}& \left(\dfrac{Q}{t}\right)=\dfrac{k \pi r^{2}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{L} \alpha \dfrac{r^{2}}{L} \\& \therefore \dfrac{Q_{1}}{Q_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}\right)=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \times\left(\dfrac{2}{1}\right)=\dfrac{1}{2} \\& \Rightarrow Q_{2}=2 Q_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two rods $A$ and $B$ are of equal lengths. Their ends are kept between the same temperature and their area of cross section are ${A_1}$ and ${A_2}$ and thermal conductivities $K_{1}$ and ${K_2}$. The rate of heat transmission in the two rods will be equal, if
explanation:
$i_{1}=\dfrac{K_{1} A_{1}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{l}$ and
$i_{2}=\dfrac{K_{2} A_{2}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{l}$
given $i_{1}=i_{2} \Rightarrow K_{1} A_{1}=K_{2} A_{2}$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of the coefficient of thermal conductivity of two different materials is $5: 3$. If the thermal resistance of the two rods of these materials of same length is same, then the ratio of the area of these rods will be
explanation:
The thermal resistance of a rod of length $l$ and area of cross section $A$ is
$R=\dfrac{l}{k A}$
$K$ - Thermal conductivity of the rod.
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\dfrac{3}{5}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: If the temperature difference on the two sides of a wall increase from $100^\circ C$ to $200^\circ C,$ its thermal conductivity
explanation: Thermal conductivity is independent of temperature of the wall, it is a constant for the material, so it will remain unchanged.

question: Mud houses are cooler in summer and warmer in winter because:
explanation: Mud is a bad conductor of heat. So it prevents the flow of heat between surroundings and inside.

options.text: Mud is a superconductor of heat
Mud is a good conductor of heat
Mud is a bad conductor of heat

question: In order that the heat flows from one part of a solid to another part, what is required
explanation: Heat energy always flow from higher temperature to lower temperature. Hence, temperature difference w.r.to length (temperature gradient) is required to flow heat from one part of a solid to another part
subject: Physics

question: A sphere at temperature $600\,K$ is placed in an environment of temperature is $200\,K$. Its cooling rate is $H$. If its temperature reduced to $400\,K$ then cooling rate in same environment will be
explanation:
Rate of cooling $\propto\left(T^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right)$
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \dfrac{H}{H^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\left(T_{1}^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right)}{\left(T_{2}^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right)} \\H^{\prime}=\dfrac{\left(T_{2}^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right) H}{\left(T_{1}^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right)}=\left[\dfrac{(400)^{4}-(200)^{4}}{(600)^{4}-(200)^{4}}\right] H=\dfrac{3}{16} H .\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: A black body radiates $20\,W$ at temperature $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If temperature of the black body is changed to $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ then its radiating power will be
explanation:
For a black body $\dfrac{Q}{t}=P=A \sigma T^{4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{T_2}}}{{{T_1}}}} \right)^4} \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_2}}}{{20}} = {\left( {\frac{{273 + 727}}{{273 + 227}}} \right)^4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_2}}}{{20}} = {(2)^4} \Rightarrow {P_2} = 320\,W.$
subject: Physics

question: If the sun's surface radiates heat at $6.3 \times {10^7}W{m^{ - 2}}$. Calculate the temperature of the sun assuming it to be a black body $\left( {\sigma  = 5.7 \times {{10}^{ - 8}}W{m^{ - 2}}\,{K^{ - 4}}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: If wavelengths of maximum intensity of radiations emitted by the sun and the moon are $0.5 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,m$ and  ${10^{ - 4}}\,m$ respectively ratio of their temperatures is
explanation: $\lambda_{m} T=\text { constant } \Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{10^{-4}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}=200 .$
subject: Physics

question: The absolute temperatures of two black bodies are $2000\,K$ and $3000\,K$ respectively. The ratio of wavelengths corresponding to maximum emission of radiation bewteen them will be
explanation: $\lambda_{m} T=\lambda_{m}^{\prime} T^{\prime} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{m}}{\lambda_{m}^{\prime}}=\dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T}=\dfrac{3000}{2000}=\dfrac{3}{2}$.
subject: Physics

question: The energy spectrum of a black body exhibits a maximum around a wavelength $\lambda_{0}$. The temperature of the black body is now changed such that the energy is maximum around a wavelength $\dfrac{3 \lambda_{0}}{4}$. The power radiated by the black body will now increase by a factor of
explanation:
According to Wien's law $\lambda_{m} T=$ constant
$\Rightarrow \lambda_{m_{1}} T_{1}=\lambda_{m_{2}} T_{2} \Rightarrow T_{2}=\dfrac{\lambda_{m_{1}}}{\lambda_{m_{2}}} T_{1}=\dfrac{\lambda_{0}}{3 \lambda_{0} / 4} \times T_{1}=\dfrac{4}{3} T_{1}$
Now
$P \propto T^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{4 / 3 T_{1}}{T_{1}}\right)^{4}=\dfrac{256}{81}$
subject: Physics

question: Absorption co-effiecient of an open window is
explanation: Open window behaves like a perfectly black body. Option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: The energy supply being cut-off, an electric heater element cools down to the temperature of its surroundings, but it will not cool further because
explanation: When element and surrounding have same temperature heat will not flow from the filament and it's temperature remains constant.

question: There is a black spot on a body. If the body is heated and carried in a dark room then it glows more. This can be explained on the basis of
subject: Physics

question: The thermal conductivity of brick is $1.7\,W\,{m^{ - 1}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$, and that of cement is $2.9\,W\,{m^{ - 1}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$. What thickness of cement will have same insulation as the brick of thickness $20\,cm$.
explanation:
Since $Q=\dfrac{K A\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right) t}{L}$. For same insulation the rate of heat flow must be same and even $A\,\& \,\left( {{T_1} - {T_2}} \right)$ are also same. Hence, $\dfrac{K}{L}$ remain constant. If $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$ be the thermal conductivities of brick and cement respectively and $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ be the required thickness then
$\frac{{{K_1}}}{{{L_1}}} = \frac{{{K_2}}}{{{L_2}}}{\rm{ or }}\frac{{1.7}}{{20}} = \frac{{2.9}}{{{L_2}}}$
$\therefore {L_2} = \frac{{2.9}}{{1.7}}20 = 34.12\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Consider a compound slab consisting of two different material having equal thickness and thermal conductivities $K$ and $2K$ respectively. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the slab is
explanation:
The equivalent thermal resistance is
$\begin{aligned}& R=R_{1}+R_{2} \\& \dfrac{2 l}{k^{\prime} A}=\dfrac{l}{K_{1} A}+\dfrac{l}{K_{2} A} \\& K^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 K_{1} K_{2}}{K_{1}+K_{2}}=\dfrac{2 \cdot K \cdot 2 K}{K+2 K}=\dfrac{4}{3} K .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In which of the following process, convection does not take place primarily

question: A cylinder of radius $R$ made of a material of thermal conductivity $K_{1}$ is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius $R$ and outer radius $2R$ made of material of thermal conductivity $K_{2}$. The two ends of a combined system are maintained at two different temperatures. There is no loss of heat across the cylindrical surface and the system is in steady state. The effective thermal conductivity of the system is:
explanation:
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{d Q_{1}}{d t}+\dfrac{d Q_{2}}{d t}$
$\begin{gathered}K \dfrac{4 \pi R^{2}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{L}=K_{1} \dfrac{\pi R^{2}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{L} \\+K_{2} \dfrac{3 \pi R^{2}\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right)}{L} \\\Rightarrow K=\dfrac{K_{1}+3 K_{2}}{4}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Combination of conducting rod/surfaces and Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Thermal Conductivity

question: A slab consists of two parallel layers of two different materials of same thickness having thermal conductivities $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$. The equivalent conductivity of the combination is
explanation:
In parallel combination equivalent thermal resistance is
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{R}=\dfrac{1}{R_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{R_{2}} \\& \dfrac{K\left(A_{1}+A_{2}\right)}{l}=\dfrac{K_{1} A_{1}}{l}+\dfrac{K_{2} A_{2}}{l} \\& K=\dfrac{K_{1} A_{1}+K_{2} A_{2}}{A_{1}+A_{2}}=\dfrac{K_{1}+K_{2}}{2} \quad\left[\text { As } A_{1}=A_{2}\right] .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A metal rod of length $2\,m$ has cross sectional areas $2\,A$ and $A$ as shown in figure. The ends are maintained at temperatures $100^\circ C$ and $70^\circ C$. The temperature at middle point $C$ is
explanation:
As rate of heat flow is same in both rods
$\frac{{\left( {{\theta _1} - \theta } \right){A_1}}}{{{l_1}}} = \frac{{\left( {\theta - {\theta _2}} \right){A_2}}}{{{l_2}}}$
$\frac{{(100 - \theta )2A}}{l} = \frac{{(\theta - 70)A}}{l}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A partition Wall has two layers $A$ and $B$, in contact, each made of a different material. They have the same thickness but the thermal conductivity of layer $A$ is twice that of layer $B$. If the steady state temperature difference across the wall is $60\,K$, then the corresponding difference across the layer $A$ is
explanation:
Let $\Delta {T_1}\,\& \,\Delta {T_2}$ be the temperatures across the two layers. As the two layers are in series the rate of heat flow is same in both.
$\frac{{{K_A}A\Delta {T_1}}}{\ell } = \frac{{{K_B}A\Delta {T_2}}}{\ell }$
$2\,{K_B}\Delta {T_1} = {K_B}\Delta {T_2}$
$ \Rightarrow 2\Delta {T_1} = \Delta {T_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Given that $\Delta {T_1} + \Delta {T_2} = 60\,K\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From (1) & (2)
$\Delta {T_1} = 20\,K{\rm{ }}\,\& \,\Delta {T_2} = 40\,K$
So option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: The temperature gradient in a rod of $0.5\,m$ length is $80^\circ C/m$. It the temperature of hotter end of the rod is $30^\circ C/m$, then the temperature of the cooler end is
explanation:
The temperature gradient is $\dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta l}$
$\frac{{30 - \theta }}{{0.5}} = 80 \Rightarrow 30 - \theta = 40 \Rightarrow \theta = - 10^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: The heat is flowing through two cylindrical rods of same material. The diameters of the rods are in the ratio $1: 2$ and their lengths are in the ratio $2: 1$. If the temperature difference between their ends is the same, the ratio of rate of flow of heat through them will be
explanation:
$\dfrac{Q}{t}=\dfrac{K A \Delta \theta}{l} \Rightarrow \dfrac{Q}{t} \propto \dfrac{A}{l} \propto \dfrac{d^{2}}{l}$
[ $d=$ Diameter of rod $]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{(Q / t)_{1}}{(Q / t)_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}\right)^{2} \times \dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}$

$=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2} \times\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)=\dfrac{1}{8}$
subject: Physics

question: Two identical vessels are filled with equal amounts of ice. The vessels are made from different materials. If the ice melts in the two vessels are in times $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ respectively then their thermal conductivities are in the ratio
explanation:
$Q = mL$ in both cases
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \dfrac{K_{1} A(\Delta T) t_{1}}{d}=\dfrac{K_{2} A(\Delta T) t_{2}}{d} \\& \therefore K_{1} / K_{2}=t_{2} / t_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: One ends of a conducting rod is maintained at temperature $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and at the other end ice is melting at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The rate of melting of ice is doubled if:
explanation:
Rate of melting ice $\propto$ rate of heat transfer $(d Q / d t)$
Further $\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{\Delta T}{(\ell / K A)}$
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{\Delta T}{l} \times A$
If $\Delta T, A$ and $\ell$ are all doubled, then $(d Q / d t)$ and hence rate of melting of ice, are doubled.

options.text: The temperature is made $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and length of the rod is made four time.
The temperature is made $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the area of cross-section of the rod is doubled
The temperature is made $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and area of cross-section of rod and length both are doubled.
Area of cross section of the rod is halved and length is doubled.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The ideal-gas equation relates the pressure, volume, and absolute temperature of a gas with its amount in moles.

Reason: The ideal-gas equation is derived based on the assumption that gases consist of a large number of particles moving in random directions with no interactions except during elastic collisions.
explanation: The ideal-gas equation is $P V=n R T$, where $P$ is the pressure, $V$ is the volume, $n$ is the number of moles, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is the temperature. It is based on the kinetic theory of gases, which assumes that gas particles do not interact except during collisions, making the Reason the correct explanation of the Assertion. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question: For an ideal gas at a temperature of $27^{\circ} {C}$ and at constant pressure, the coefficient of  volume expansion  is nearly
explanation:
Since, $P$ is constant
$P d V=n R d T \Rightarrow \dfrac{d V}{d T}=\dfrac{n R}{P}$
Coefficient of volume expansion will be
$\alpha_{V}=\dfrac{d V}{V d T}=\dfrac{n R}{P V}=\dfrac{1}{T}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha_{V}=\dfrac{1}{300 K} \quad\left(\because 27^{\circ} {C}=300 K\right)$
$=33 \times 10^{-4} K^{-1}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

$\therefore F = \frac{9}{5} \times \frac{F}{2} + 32$
$ \Rightarrow F - 0.9F = 32.\,\,\,\,\therefore F = 320^\circ F$

question: On a new temperature scale, the melting point of ice is $20^{\circ} {X}$ and the boiling point of water is $110^{\circ} {X}$. A temperature of $40^{\circ} {C}$ would be indicated on this new temperature scale as
explanation:
According to question, the new scale has $20^{\circ} {X}$ as melting point $110^{\circ} {X}$ as boiling point.
Let $40^{\circ} {C}=\theta^{\circ} X$.
$\therefore \dfrac{\theta-20}{110-20}=\dfrac{40-0}{100-0}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\left[20+(110-20) \times \dfrac{40}{100}\right]^{0} X \Rightarrow \theta=56^{\circ} . X$
$\therefore$ So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: If the reading in Fahrenheit scale is twice the reading in Celsius scale, then the reading in Fahrenheit scale is
explanation:
We know that, ${ }^{\circ} {C}=\left({ }^{\circ} {F}-32\right) \times \dfrac{5}{9}$
Given ${ }^{\circ} {F}=2 \times\left({ }^{\circ} {C}\right)$
Thus, ${ }^{\circ} {C}=\left(2^{\circ} {C}-32\right) \times \dfrac{5}{9}$
${ }^{\circ} {C} \times \dfrac{9}{5}=2{ }^{\circ} {C}-32 \Rightarrow 2{ }^{\circ} {C}-{ }^{\circ} {C} \times \dfrac{9}{5}=32$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{{ }^{\circ} {C}}{5}=32 \Rightarrow{ }^{\circ} {C}=160^{\circ}$
Hence, in Fahrenheit
$\left({ }^{\circ} {F}\right)=2{ }^{\circ} {C}=2 \times 160=320^{\circ} {F}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: The Fahrenheit and Kelvin scales of temperature will have the same reading at a temperature of
explanation:
Let the temperature be $X$
$\Rightarrow F=K=X$
$F$ is temp in Fahrenheit, $K$ is temperature in Kelvin.
Relation between $F$ and $K$ is
$\dfrac{F-32}{9}=\dfrac{K-273}{5}$
Put $X$ in place for $F, K$
$\dfrac{X-32}{9}=\dfrac{X-273}{5}=4 X-2457+160=0$
$X=574.6$
Thus, the temperature is $574.6^{\circ} {F}$ or $574.6\,K$ So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: On celsius  scale the temperature of body increases by $40^\circ C$. The increase in temperature on Fahrenheit scale is ____ .
explanation:
On celcius scale temperature increases by $\Delta C=40^{\circ} C$
By the relation, $C=\dfrac{5}{9}(F-32)$
$\therefore \Delta C=\dfrac{5}{9} \Delta F$
$\Rightarrow 40=\dfrac{5}{9} \Delta F$
$\Delta F=72^{\circ} F$
So, option (2) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: If absolute zero is $ - 273.15^\circ C$ on Celsius temperature scale, then the absolute zero on the Fahrenheit scale is
explanation:

$ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - 273.15}}{5} = \frac{{F - 32}}{9}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad F = - 459.67^\circ F$

question: If a thermometer reads freezing point of water as $20^\circ C$ and boiling point as $150^\circ C,$  how much thermometer read when the actual temperature is $60^\circ C$ ?
explanation:

$\frac{{x - 20}}{{150 - 20}} = \frac{{60}}{{100}} \Rightarrow x = 98^\circ C$

question: Two temperature scales ${A}$ and ${B}$ are released by ${\dfrac{A-42}{110}=\dfrac{B-72}{220}}$. At which temperature two scales have the same reading?
explanation:
Let at temperature ${T}$ both the scales ${A}$ and ${B}$ have the same reading.
${\dfrac{T-42}{110}=\dfrac{T-72}{220}}$
${\Rightarrow 2 T-84=T-72}$
${\Rightarrow T=+12^{\circ}}$

question: On a temperature scale ' $X$ ', the boiling point of water is $65^\circ X$ and the freezing point is $ - 15^\circ X$. Assume that the $X$ scale is linear. The equivalent temperature corresponding to $ - 95^\circ X$ on the Farenheit scale would be
explanation:
$\dfrac{X-X_{\text {freeze }}}{X_{\text {boil }}-X_{\text {freeze }}}=\dfrac{t-32^{\circ}}{212^{\circ}-32^{\circ}}$
as $\dfrac{\text { Reading on scale }- \text { Lower fixed point }}{\text { Upper fixed point }- \text { Lower fixed point }}$
$=\text { constant for any scale. }$
$\dfrac{-95^{\circ}-\left(-15^{\circ}\right)}{65^{\circ}-\left(-15^{\circ}\right)}=\dfrac{t-32^{\circ}}{180^{\circ}} ; \dfrac{-80^{\circ}}{80^{\circ}}=\dfrac{t-32^{\circ}}{180^{\circ}}$
$t=-180^{\circ}+32^{\circ} ; t=-148^{\circ} F$
subject: Physics

question: The graph between two temperature scales \(P\) and \(Q\) is shown in the figure. Between upper fixed point and lower fixed point there are 150 equal divisions of scale \(P\) and 100 divisions on scale \(Q\). The relationship for conversion between the two scales is given by

question: The triple point of neon and carbon dioxide are 24.57$K$ and $216.55\,K$  respectively. These temperatures on the celsius and fahrenheit scales are respectively.
explanation:
Triple point of neon, ${T_1} = 24.57\,K$
Triple point of $C{O_2},\,{T_2} = 216.55\,K$
On celsius of neon,
$t_1^oC = 24.57 - 273.15 = - {\text{ }}248.58^\circ C$
Triple point of $C{O_2},t_2^{\text{o}}C = 216.55 - 273.15$
$ = - 56.60^\circ C$
The temperatures in Fahrenheit scale are
${F_1} = 32 + \frac{9}{5}( - 248.58) = - 415.44^\circ F$
${F_2} = 32 + \frac{9}{5}( - 56.6) = - 69.88^\circ F$
subject: Physics

question: Two absolute scales $A$ and $B$ have triple points of water defined to be $200\,A$ and $350\,B$. What is the relation between $T_{A}$ and $T_{B}$ ?
explanation:
Given triple point of water on scale $A=200 A$
Triple point of water on scale $B=350 B$
We know that triple point of water on absolute scale
$\begin{aligned}& =273.16 K \\& 200 A=350 B=273.16 K \\& 1 A=\dfrac{273.16}{200} K \text { and } 1 B=\dfrac{273.16}{350} K\end{aligned}$
If $T_{A}$ and $T_{B}$ are the triple point of water on two scales $A$ and $B$ then
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{273.16}{200} T_{A}=\dfrac{273.16}{350} T_{B} \\& \dfrac{T_{A}}{T_{B}}=\dfrac{200}{350}=\dfrac{4}{7} \text { or } T_{A}=\dfrac{4}{7} T_{B} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In a mercury thermometer the ice point (lower fixed point) is marked as $10^{\circ}$ and steam point is marked as $130^{\circ}$. At $40^\circ C$ temperature, what will this thermometer read

question: A liquid cools from a temperature of $368\,K$ to $358\,K$ in 22 minutes. In the same room, the same liquid takes 12.5 minutes to cool from $358\,K$ to $353\,K$. The room temperature is
explanation:
By Newton's law of cooling,
$\dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{t} \propto\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right)$
$\therefore$ For $\theta_{1}=368 K, \theta_{2}=358 K, t=22 \mathrm{~min}$
$\therefore \frac{{368 - 358}}{{22}} = k\left( {\frac{{95^\circ C + 85^\circ C}}{2} - {\theta _0}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{5}{{11}} = k\left( {90 - {\theta _0}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Again, $\theta_{2}=358 K, \theta_{3}=353 K, t=12.5 \mathrm{~min}$
$\frac{{358 - 353}}{{12.5}} = k\left( {\frac{{85^\circ C + 80^\circ C}}{2} - {\theta _0}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} = K\left( {82.5 - {\theta _o}} \right){\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq (1) and (2), ${\theta _0} = 27.5^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: Match the terms in Column I with their correct descriptions in Column II related to Newton’s Law of Cooling and associated thermal properties. Choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A-The specific heat capacity $(S)$ is the heat required per unit mass to change the temperature by a certain amount ($\Delta T$), therefore it matches with Q.
B - Newton's Law of Cooling states that the rate of heat loss of a body is directly proportional to the difference in the temperatures between the body and the surroundings, thus it matches with R.
C - The latent heat of fusion ($L_{f}$) is the heat per unit mass required for a substance to change from the solid phase to the liquid phase at the same temperature and pressure, which corresponds to S.
D - $T$ is described as the temperature of the surrounding medium in Newton's Law of Cooling, matching with P. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P,        B - R,           C - S,       D - Q
A - R,  B - Q,         C - P,    D - S
A - P,  B - Q,         C - R,    D - S
A - Q,   B - R,           C - S,    D - P

question: An object cools from $100^{\circ} {C}$ to $40^{\circ} {C}$ in 10 $\min$, when the surrounding temperature is $10^{\circ} {C}$. Then the time taken by the object to cool from $70^{\circ} {C}$ to $20^{\circ} {C}$ is (take, $\ln 2=0.7, \ln 3=1.1, \ln 6=1.8$)
explanation:
By Newton's law of cooling,
$\frac{{ - dT}}{{dt}} = b\left[ {T - {T_0}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (1),
$\int_{T_{i}}^{T} \dfrac{d T}{T-T_{0}}=-b \int_{0}^{t} d t$
$ \Rightarrow \quad T = {T_0} + \left( {{T_i} - {T_0}} \right){e^{ - bt}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From the first condition of question, Putting these value in Eq. (2),
$40=10+(100-10) e^{-b \times 10}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{30}{90}=e^{-10 b} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{3}=e^{-10 b}$
$\Rightarrow \ln \left(\dfrac{1}{3}\right)=-10 b$
$\Rightarrow b=\dfrac{\ln 3}{10}=\dfrac{1.1}{10}$
Now, by next condition of question, From Eq. (2),
$20=10+(70-10) e^{-b t}$
$\dfrac{10}{60}=e^{-\dfrac{1.1}{10} t}$
$\Rightarrow \ln \left(\dfrac{1}{6}\right)=-\dfrac{1.1}{6} t$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{100 \times \ln 6}{11}=\dfrac{100 \times 1.8}{11}=16.3 {~min}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A body cools from $70^{\circ} {C}$ to $40^{\circ} {C}$ in 5 min. Calculate the time it takes to cool from $60^{\circ} {C}$  to $40^{\circ} {C}$. The temperature of the surrounding is   $20^{\circ} {C}$.
explanation:
By using Newton's law of cooling,
$\frac{{{T_i} - {T_f}}}{t} = K\left( {\frac{{{T_i} + {T_f}}}{2} - {T_s}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{70-40}{5}=K\left(\dfrac{70+40}{2}-20\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{30}{5}=K\left(\dfrac{110}{2}-20\right) \Rightarrow 6=K(35)$
$\Rightarrow K=\dfrac{6}{35}$
According to second case, again by using Eq. (1), we can write,
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{60-40}{t_{2}}=\dfrac{6}{35} \times\left(\dfrac{60+40}{2}-20\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{20}{t_{2}}=\dfrac{6}{35} \times 30 \Rightarrow \dfrac{20}{t_{2}}=\dfrac{6}{7} \times 6$
$ \Rightarrow {t_2} = \frac{{20 \times 7}}{{36}} = \frac{{140}}{{36}} = 3.89\,\min $
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A metal ball of mass $1\,kg$ is heated using a $40\,W$ heater in a room at $30^{\circ} {C}$. The temperature of the ball becomes steady at $70^{\circ} {C}$. Assuming Newton's law of cooling, the rate of loss of heat to the surrounding when the ball is at $40^{\circ} {C}$ is
explanation:
By Newton's law of cooling,
$-\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=k\left(T-T_{0}\right)$
$\Rightarrow k(70-30)=-\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=40 \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{40}{40}=1$
When, $T_{1}=40^{\circ} {C}$, then $-\dfrac{d Q_{40}}{d t}=k\left(T_{1}-T_{0}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{d Q_{40}}{d t}=1(40-30)=10 {~W}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Newton's law of cooling is a special case of
explanation:
According to Stefan’s law heat energy radiated is given by
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}} = \varepsilon \sigma A\left( {{T^4} - T_0^4} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
If $m$ be the mass of body and $c$ be the specific heat, then heat loss at temperature $T$ is
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}} = - mc\frac{{dT}}{{dt}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$-m c \dfrac{d T}{d t}=\varepsilon \sigma A\left(T^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right)$
$\Rightarrow-\dfrac{d T}{d t}=\dfrac{\varepsilon \sigma A}{m c}\left(T^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right)$
Here, $\quad T=T_{0}+\Delta T$
$\Rightarrow-\dfrac{d T}{d t}=\dfrac{\varepsilon \sigma A}{m c}\left[\left(T_{0}+\Delta T\right)^{4}-T_{0}^{4}\right]$
$=\dfrac{\varepsilon \sigma A}{m c} T_{0}^{4}\left[\left(1+\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{0}}\right)^{4}-1\right]$
$=\dfrac{\varepsilon \sigma A}{m c} T_{0}^{4}\left[1+\dfrac{4 \Delta T}{T_{0}}-1\right] \quad\left(\because \Delta T \ll T_{0}\right)$
$=\dfrac{\varepsilon \sigma A 4 T_{0}^{3}}{m c} \Delta T \Rightarrow-\dfrac{d T}{d t}=k \Delta T$
where, $k=\dfrac{\varepsilon \sigma A 4 T_{0}^{3}}{m c}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{d T}{d t}=-k\left(T-T_{0}\right)$
This is Newton's law of cooling.
Hence, Newton's law of cooling is a special case of Stefan's law.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A heating element of mass $100\,g$ and having specific heat of $1 J /\left(g^{\circ} C\right)$ is exposed to the surrounding air at $27^{\circ} {C}$. The element attains a steady state temperature of $127^{\circ} {C}$, while absorbing $100\,W$ of electric power. If the power is switched off, then approximate time taken by the element to cool down to $126^{\circ} {C}$ will be (neglect radiation)
explanation:
Change in temperature,
$\Delta T=(127-126)^{\circ} {C}=1^{\circ} {C}$
Specific heat of heating element,
$s=1 J /\left(g^{\circ} {C}\right)=1 \times 10^{3} J /\left(k g^{\circ} {C}\right)$
$\therefore$ Heat rejected to cool from $127^{\circ} {C}$ to
$126^{\circ} {C}$,
$Q=m s \Delta T=0.1 \times 1 \times 10^{3} \times 1=100 J$
$\therefore$ Time taken $=\dfrac{Q}{P}=\dfrac{100}{100}=1 {~s}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A body cools from $70^{\circ} {C}$ to $40^{\circ} {C}$ in 5 min . Calculate the time it takes to cool from $60^{\circ} {C}$ to $30^{\circ} {C}$. The temperature of the surroundings is $20^{\circ} {C}$.
explanation:
Body cools from $70^{\circ} {C}$ to $40^{\circ} {C}$ in 5 minute, By Newton's law of cooling,
$\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{t}=k\left[\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{0}\right]$
or, $\dfrac{70-40}{5}=k\left[\dfrac{70+40}{2}-20\right]$
$ \Rightarrow k = \frac{6}{{35}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Again, let the body $\operatorname{cool}$ from $60^{\circ} {C}$ to $30^{\circ} {C}$ in time $t$ minutes.
$i.e,$ $T_{1}^{\prime}=60^{\circ} {C}$ and $T_{2}^{\prime}=30^{\circ} {C}$,
$\therefore$ By Newton's law of cooling,
$\dfrac{T_{1}^{\prime}-T_{2}^{\prime}}{t}=k\left[\dfrac{T_{1}^{\prime}+T_{2}^{\prime}}{2}-T_{0}\right]$
$\dfrac{60-30}{t}=\dfrac{6}{35}\left[\dfrac{60+30}{2}-20\right] \quad[\therefore$ From Eq.(1) $]$
$\dfrac{30}{t}=\dfrac{6}{35}[25] \Rightarrow t=7$ minutes.
Hence option (2) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A hot body placed in air cools down. The rate of decrease of temperature is proportional to the temperature difference between the hot body and the surrounding. The body loses $60 \%$ and $80 \%$ of maximum heat in time $t_{1}$, and $t_{2}$, respectively. If temperature of air is $0^{\circ} C$, the ratio $t_{2} / t_{1}$ will be
explanation:
Let $\theta_{1}=$ Initial temperature of the hot body. $\quad \theta_{0}=$ Temperature of air $=0^{\circ} {C}$
$\theta=$ Temperature of the body at time $t$.
By Newton's law of cooling,
$-\dfrac{d \theta}{d t} \propto\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right) \Rightarrow \dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=-k\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta} \dfrac{d \theta}{\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)}=-k \int_{0}^{t} d t$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{1}{k} \ln \dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}}{\theta-\theta_{0}}$
As $\theta_{0}=0^{\circ} {C}, \quad t=\dfrac{1}{k} \ln \dfrac{\theta_{1}}{\theta}$
As heat content $Q \propto \theta$, we can write
$t=\dfrac{1}{k} \ln \dfrac{Q_{1}}{Q}$.
At $t=t_{1}, Q=(1-0.6) Q_{1}=0.4 Q_{1}$
$\therefore \quad {t_1} = \frac{1}{K}\ln \frac{{{Q_1}}}{{0.4{Q_1}}} = \frac{1}{K}\ln \frac{5}{2}.$
At $t=t_{2}, Q=(1-0.8) Q_{1}=0.2 Q_{1}$
$\therefore t_{2}=\dfrac{1}{k} \ln \dfrac{Q_{1}}{0.2 Q_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{k} \ln 5$
$\therefore \dfrac{t_{2}}{t_{1}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{k} \ln 5}{\dfrac{1}{k} \ln 5 / 2}=\dfrac{\ln 5}{\ln \left(\dfrac{5}{2}\right)}$
Hence, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A cup of coffee cools from $150^{\circ} F$ to $144^{\circ} F$ in 1 min in a room at $72^{\circ} {F}$. How much time it take to cool from $110^{\circ} {F}$ to $104^{\circ} {F}$ in the same room?
explanation:
According to Newton's law of cooling,
$m c \dfrac{\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{t_{1}}=K\left(\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{0}\right)$
$m c\left(\dfrac{150-144}{1}\right)=K\left(\dfrac{150+144}{2}-72\right)$
$6\,mc = 75K\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
In second case, $T_{1}^{\prime}=110^{\circ} {F}, T_{2}^{\prime}=104^{\circ} {F}$
Time $=t_{2}=$ ?
By Newton's law of cooling, we have
$m c\left(\dfrac{110-104}{t_{2}}\right)=K\left(\dfrac{110+104}{2}-72\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{6mc}}{{{t_2}}} = 35\,K\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get,
$\dfrac{6 m c}{\left(\dfrac{6 m c}{t_{2}}\right)}=\dfrac{75 K}{35 K} \Rightarrow t_{2}=\dfrac{15}{7}=2.14 {~min}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: In a room where temperature is ${30^{\circ} {C}}$, a body cools from ${61^{\circ} {C}}$ to ${59^{\circ} {C}}$ in 4 minutes. The time taken by the body to cool from ${51^{\circ} {C}}$ to ${49^{\circ} {C}}$ under the same conditions would be

From Newton's cooling law,$\dfrac{\left(T_{1}-T_{2}\right)}{t}=k\left[\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{0}\right]$
For first case,${T_{1}=61^{\circ} {C}, T_{2}=59^{\circ} {C}, T_{0}=30^{\circ} {C}, t=4}$ minutes
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{61-59}{4}=k\left[\dfrac{61+59}{2}-30\right] \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{1}{60}$
For second case,${T_{1}=51^{\circ} {C}, T_{2}=49^{\circ} {C}, t=?, T_{0}=30^{\circ} {C}}$
${\therefore\left[\dfrac{51-49}{t}\right]=k\left[\dfrac{51+49}{2}-30\right]}$
$\frac{2}{t} = k20\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {\because k = \frac{1}{{60}}} \right]$
${\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{2 \times 60}{20}=6}$ minutes.
So correct option is (2)

question: Two solid spheres $A$ and $B$ made of the same material have radii ${r_A}$ and $r_{B}$, respectively. Both the spheres are cooled from the same temperature under the conditions valid for Newton's law of cooling. The ratio of the rate of change of temperature $A$ and $B$ is

question: The temperature of a body falls from ${52^{\circ} {C}}$ to ${36^{\circ} {C}}$ in 10 minutes when placed in a surrounding of constant temperature ${20^{\circ} {C}}$. What will be the temperature of the body after another 10 min? (Use Newton's law of cooling)

question: A body cools in 7 minutes from $60^\circ C$ to $40^\circ C$. The temperature of the surrounding is $10^\circ C$. The temperature of the body after the next 7 minutes will be
explanation:
${T_1} = 60^\circ {\rm{C}},{T_2} = 40^\circ {\rm{C}},$
$t = 7 \times 60\sec ,{T_0} = 10^\circ C$
By Newton's Law of cooling,$-\dfrac{d T}{d t}=K\left(T-T_{0}\right)$
$\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{t}=k\left[\left(\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}\right)-T_{0}\right]$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{60-40}{7 \times 60}\right)=k\left[\left(\dfrac{60+40}{2}\right)-10\right]$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{20}{7 \times 60}=k \times 40 \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{1}{14 \times 60}$
Let the final temperature be $T^\circ C$
$\dfrac{40-T}{7 \times 60}=k\left[\left(\dfrac{40+T}{2}\right)-10\right]$
$\dfrac{40-T}{7 \times 60}=k\left(\dfrac{20+T}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{40-T}{7 \times 60}=\dfrac{1}{14 \times 60}\left(\dfrac{20+T}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 80-T=20+T$
$ \Rightarrow T = 30^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A bowl filled with very hot soup cools from $98^\circ C$ to $86^\circ C$ in 2 minutes when the room temperature is $22^\circ C$. How long will it take to cool from $75^\circ C$ to $69^\circ C$?
explanation:
Case I : ${\theta _1} = 98^\circ C,{\theta _2} = 86^\circ C,{\theta _0} = 22^\circ C$ ${t_1} = 2\,mm$
According to Newton's law of cooling
$\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=-K\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right)$
So, $\dfrac{98-86}{2}=K\left(\dfrac{98+86}{2}-22\right)$
Solving, $K=\dfrac{3}{35}$
Case II : ${\theta _1} = 75^\circ C,{\theta _2} = 69^\circ C,{\theta _0} = 22^\circ C$.
Let time is $t$.
$\dfrac{75-69}{t}=K\left(\dfrac{75+69}{2}-22\right)$
$\dfrac{6}{t}=K \times 50=\dfrac{3}{35} \times 50$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{7}{5}\min = 1.4\,\min $
subject: Physics

question: A body cools from $80^\circ C$ to $60^\circ C$ in 5 minutes.The temperature of the surrounding is $20^\circ C$.The time it takes to cool from $60^\circ C$ to $40^\circ C$ is
explanation:
Initial temperature $\left( {{T_i}} \right) = 80^\circ C$
Final temperature $\left( {{T_f}} \right) = 60^\circ C$
Temperature of the surrounding $\left( {{T_0}} \right) = 20^\circ C$
Time, $t = 5\,{\rm{min}}$
According to Newton's law of cooling
$-\dfrac{d T}{d t}=K\left(T-T_{0}\right)$. Here $T=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(T_{i}+T_{f}\right)$.
Rate of cooling, $=K\left[\dfrac{\left(T_{i}+T_{f}\right)}{2}-T_{0}\right]$
$\dfrac{\left(T_{f}-T_{i}\right)}{t}=K\left[\dfrac{80+60}{2}-20\right]$
$\dfrac{80-60}{5}=K[70-20]$$4=K[50] ; K=\dfrac{4}{50}=\dfrac{2}{25}$
In second condition,
Initial temperature $ = {T_i} = 60^\circ C$
Final temperature $ = {T_f} = 40^\circ C$
Time taken for cooling is $t$.
According to Newton's law of cooling,
$\frac{{60 - 40}}{t} = \frac{2}{{25}}\left[ {\frac{{60 + 40}}{2} - 20} \right]\,;$
$\dfrac{20}{t}=\dfrac{2}{25}[30]$
$ \Rightarrow t = 500\,s$.
subject: Physics

question: An object is cooled from $75^\circ C$ to $65^\circ C$ in 2 $\min$. The time, it takes to cool from $55^\circ C$ to $45^\circ C$
explanation:
As we know, from Newton's law of cooling
$\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{t}=\alpha\left(\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{0}\right)$
where $k$ is constant of proportionality
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{75 - 65}}{2} = \alpha \left( {\frac{{75 + 65}}{2} - 30} \right){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Also, $\frac{{55 - 45}}{t} = \alpha \left( {\frac{{55 + 45}}{2} - 30} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equation (1) and (2), we get
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{t}{2}=\dfrac{40}{20} ; t=4 \mathrm{~min}$.
subject: Physics

question: A cup of tea cools from $80^\circ C$ to $60^\circ C$ in $1 \mathrm{~min}$. The ambient temperature is $30^\circ C$. In next $1 \mathrm{~min}$, its temperature will be
explanation:
$\dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{1}=K\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right)$
$\therefore \dfrac{80^{\circ}-60^{\circ}}{1}=K\left(\dfrac{80^{\circ}+60^{\circ}}{2}-30^{\circ}\right)$$20^{\circ}=K \times 40^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow K=\dfrac{1}{2}$
For next $1 \mathrm{~min}$,
$\dfrac{60^{\circ}-\theta}{1}=\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{60^{\circ}+\theta}{2}-30^{\circ}\right)$
$120^{\circ}-2 \theta=\dfrac{60^{\circ}+\theta-60^{\circ}}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 240^{\circ}-4 \theta=\theta$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{{240^\circ }}{5} = 48^\circ C$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A hot body, obeying Newton's law of cooling is cooling down from its peak value $80^\circ C$ to an ambient temperature of $30^\circ C$. It takes 5 mintues in cooling down from $80^\circ C$to $40^\circ C$. How much time will it take to call down from $62^\circ C$ to $32^\circ C$? (Given In $2=0.693$, In $5=1.609$ )
explanation:
From Newton's law of cooling,
$t=\dfrac{-1}{k} \log _{e}\left(\dfrac{\theta_{2}-\theta_{0}}{\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}}\right)$
From the given information
$5 = \frac{{ - 1}}{k}{\log _e}\frac{{(40 - 30)}}{{(80 - 30)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
And, $t = \frac{{ - 1}}{k}{\log _e}\frac{{(32 - 30)}}{{(62 - 30)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing equation (2) by (1),
$\dfrac{t}{5}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{k} \log _{e} \dfrac{(32-30)}{(62-30)}}{\dfrac{1}{k} \log _{e} \dfrac{(40-30)}{(80-30)}}$
On solving we get, time taken to cool down from $62^\circ C$ to $32^\circ C,t = 8.6$ minutes.
subject: Physics

question: Hot water cools from $60^\circ C$ to $50^\circ C$ in the first 10 minutes and to $42^\circ C$ in the next 10 minutes. The temperature of the surroundings is:
explanation:
By Newton's law of cooling
$\dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{t}=-K\left[\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right]$
where $\theta_{0}$ is the temperature of surrounding.
Now, hot water cools from $60^\circ C$ to $50^\circ C$ in 10 minutes,
$\frac{{60 - 50}}{{10}} = - K\left[ {\frac{{60 + 50}}{2} - {\theta _0}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Again, it cools from $50^\circ C$ to $42^\circ C$ in next 10 minutes.
$\frac{{50 - 42}}{{10}} = - K\left[ {\frac{{50 + 42}}{2} - {\theta _0}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing equation (1) by (2) we get
$ \Rightarrow {\theta _0} = 10^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A large cylindrical rod of length $L$ is made by joining two identical rods of copper and steel of length $\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)$ each. The rods are completely insulated from the surroundings. If the free end of copper rod maintained at $100^\circ C$ and that of steel at $0^\circ C$ then the temperature of junction is (Thermal conductivity of copper is 9 times that of steel)
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956026/preptracker/questions/q_38095_explanation_1764956025742.png
Let conductivity of steel $K_{\text {steel }}=k$
Conductivity of copper $K_{\text {copper }}=9 k$
${\theta _{copper{\rm{ }}}} = 100^\circ C;{\theta _{steel{\rm{ }}}} = 0^\circ C$
${l_{steel{\rm{ }}}} = {l_{copper{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{L}{2}$
Let $\theta$ is the temperature of the junction. As heat flow is same in both rods
$\frac{{9KA(100 - \theta )}}{{L/2}} = \frac{{KA(\theta - 0)}}{{L/2}} \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A body cools in a surrounding which is at a constant temperature of \(\theta_{0}\). Assume that it obeys Newton's law of cooling. Its temperature \(\theta\) is plotted against time \[t\]. Tangents are drawn to the curve at the points \(P\left(\theta=\theta_{1}\right)\) and \(Q\left(\theta=\theta_{2}\right)\). These tangents meet the time axis at angles of \(\phi_{2}\) and \(\phi_{1}\), as shown
explanation:
For $\theta-t$ plot of cooling $=\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=$ slope of the curve.
where $\dfrac{d \theta}{d t} \propto\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)$
At $P$
$\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=\tan \phi_{2}=k\left(\theta_{2}-\theta_{0}\right)$, where $k = $ constant
At $Q$
$\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=\tan \phi_{1}=k\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}\right) \Rightarrow \dfrac{\tan \phi_{2}}{\tan \phi_{1}}=\dfrac{\left(\theta_{2}-\theta_{0}\right)}{\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}\right)}$
subject: Physics

question: A liquid in a beaker has temperature \(\theta(t)\) at time \[t\] and \(\theta_{0}\) is temperature of surroundings, then according to Newton's law of cooling the correct graph between \[l\,n\left( {\theta - {\theta _0}} \right)\] and \(t\) is:
explanation:
According to Newton's law of cooling
$\frac{{d\theta }}{{dt}} = - K\left( {\theta - {\theta _0}} \right)$
$\int {\frac{{d\theta }}{{\theta - {\theta _0}}}} = - K\int d t$
${\log _e}\left( {\theta - {\theta _0}} \right) = - Kt + C\,(C - {\text{ is}}\,{\text{a}}\,{\text{constant }})$
The garph between $\log _{e}\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)$ and $t$ is straight line.
subject: Physics

question: A pan filled with hot food cools from $94^\circ C$ to $86^\circ C$ in 2 minutes. When the room temperature is $20^\circ C$. How long will it cool from $74^\circ C$ to $66^\circ C$ ?
explanation:
According to Newton's law of cooling
$\dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}=k\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right)$
case-1
${\theta _1} = 94^\circ C,{\theta _2} = 86^\circ C,{\theta _0} = 20^\circ C$
$\frac{8}{2} = k\left( {\frac{{94 + 86}}{2} - 20} \right) = 70k$
$k = \frac{4}{{70}}$
Case - (2)
${\theta _1} = 74^\circ C,{\theta _2} = 66^\circ C$
$\frac{{74 - 66}}{t} = \frac{4}{{70}}\left( {\frac{{74 + 66}}{2} - 20} \right) = \frac{4}{{70}} \times 50$
$t = \frac{{8 \times 7}}{{4 \times 5}} = 2.8{\rm{ minutes }}$
subject: Physics

question: A cup of tea cools from $65.5^\circ C$ to $62.5^\circ C$ in 1 minute in a room at $22.5^\circ C$. How long will it take to cool from $46.5^\circ C$ to $40.5^\circ C$ in the same room?
explanation:
According to Newton's law of cooling,
$\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{t}=K\left(\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{s}\right)$
where $T_{s}$, is the temperature of surroundings.
$\text { Case - (1) }$
${T_1} = 65.5^\circ C,{T_2} = 62.5^\circ C,{T_s} = 22.5^\circ C,t = 1\,\min $
$\therefore \frac{{65.5^\circ C - 62.5^\circ C}}{{1\min }}$
$ = K\left( {\frac{{65.5^\circ C + 62.5^\circ C}}{2} - 22.5^\circ C} \right)$
$\frac{{3^\circ C}}{{1\,\min }} = K\left( {41.5^\circ C} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Case - (2)
${T_1} = 46.5^\circ C,{T_2} = 40.5^\circ C,{T_s} = 22.5^\circ C$
$\therefore \frac{{46.5^\circ C - 40.5^\circ C}}{t}$
$ = K\left( {\frac{{46.5^\circ C - 40.5^\circ C}}{2} - 22.5^\circ C} \right)$
$\frac{{6\,^\circ C}}{t} = K\left( {21^\circ C} \right)$
From eq's $1\,\& \,2$
$\frac{{\frac{{3^\circ C}}{{1\,\min }}}}{{\frac{{6^\circ C}}{t}}} = \frac{{K\left( {41.5^\circ C} \right)}}{{K\left( {21^\circ C} \right)}}$
$t \approx 4\,\min $
subject: Physics

question: A hot body is allowed to cool. The surrounding temperature is constant at $30^\circ C$. The body takes time ${t_1}$ to cool from $90^\circ C$ to$89^\circ C$ and time ${t_2}$ to cool from $60^\circ C$ to $59.5^\circ C$. Then,
subject: Physics

question: A cup of coffee cools from $90^\circ C$ to $80^\circ C$ in $t$ minutes, when the room temperature is $20^\circ C$. The time taken by a similar cup of coffee to cool from $80^\circ C$ to $60^\circ C$ at a room temperature same at $20^\circ C$ is
explanation:
From newton's laws of cooling
$\begin{array}{r}\dfrac{T_{1}-T_{2}}{\Delta t}=k\left[\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{s}\right] \\\dfrac{90-80}{t}=k\left[\dfrac{90+80}{2}-20\right] \\\dfrac{80-60}{t^{\prime}}=k\left[\dfrac{80+60}{2}-20\right] \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{10}{t} \times \dfrac{t^{\prime}}{20}=\dfrac{65}{50} \Rightarrow t^{\prime}=\dfrac{13}{5} t\end{array}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: According to Newton's law of cooling, the rate of loss of heat, $-\dfrac{d Q}{d t}$ of the body is directly proportional to the difference of temperature.


Reason: The rate of heat loss is directly proportional to area of the cooling body.
explanation: Option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
