question: A body cools from $80^\circ C$ to $50^\circ C$ in $5\,\min $. Calculate the time it takes to cool from $60^\circ C$ to $30^\circ C$. The temperature of the surroundings is $20^\circ C$.
explanation:
Given, initial temperature ${T_1} = 80^\circ C$
Final temperature ${T_2} = 50^\circ C$
Temperature of the surroundings ${T_0} = 20^\circ C$
${t_1} = 5\,\min $
According to Newton's law of cooling.
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Rate of cooling, } \dfrac{d T}{d t}=k\left[\dfrac{T_{1}+T_{2}}{2}-T_{0}\right] \\& \dfrac{(80-50)}{5}=k\left(\dfrac{80+50}{2}-20\right) \\& \dfrac{30}{5}=k(65-20) \\& \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{6}{45}=\dfrac{2}{15}\end{aligned}$
In second case,
Initial temperature $T_1^\prime = 60^\circ C$
Final temperature $T_2^\prime = 30^\circ C$
$t^{\prime}=?$
${\rm{Now, }}\frac{{(60 - 30)}}{{{t^\prime }}} = \frac{2}{{15}}\left( {\frac{{60 + 30}}{2} - 20} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow {t^\prime } = \frac{{30 \times 15}}{{2 \times 25}} = 9\,\min .$
subject: Physics

question: A sphere, a cube and a thin circular plate, all of same material and same mass are initially heated to same high temperature.
explanation:
Rate of cooling
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta Q}{t} \propto A$
Since area of plate is largest so it will cool fastest and sphere has the least surface area so the cooling is slowest.

options.text: Plate will cool fastest and cube the slowest
Sphere will cool fastest and cube the slowest
Plate will cool fastest and sphere the slowest
Cube will cool fastest and plate the slowest

subject: Physics

question: Forensic experts use Newton's law of cooling to find out when victim of crime died. They take the temperature of the body when they find it and by knowing the average temperature of the human body. Initially and measuring the room temperature they can find $K$ and then $t$. Suppose that a body was discovered at 12.00 $PM$ in the midnight in a notel room and its temperature was $80^\circ F$. The temperature of the atmosphere is $60^\circ F$ Two hours later the temperature of the body dropped to $75^\circ F$. Find the time of death. (Assume that average temperature of a living person is $98^\circ F$ )
explanation:
We know that
$t=-K \ln \left(\dfrac{T_{2}-T_{0}}{T_{1}-T_{0}}\right)$
From the given information
$2hr = - K\ln \left( {\frac{{75 - 60}}{{80 - 60}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The time of death is
${t_d} = - K\ln \left( {\frac{{80 - 60}}{{98 - 60}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From the above two equations we get
${t_d} = 4.5hr$
i.e., the death happened around $7:30\,PM$
subject: Physics

question: A body cools from $80^{\circ} C$ to $64^{\circ} C$ in $5 \mathrm{~min}$ and same body cools from $80^\circ C$ to $52^\circ C$ in $10 \mathrm{~min}$, what is the temperature of the surrounding?
explanation:
Let $T$ be the temperature of the surrounding. According to Newton's law of cooling,
$\frac{{80 - 64}}{5} = k\left[ {\frac{{80 + 64}}{2} - T} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${\rm{and }}\frac{{80 - 52}}{{10}} = k\left[ {\frac{{80 + 52}}{2} - T} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Solving Eqs.(1) and (2), we get
$T = 24^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: The room temperature is $ + 20^\circ C$ when outside temperature is $- 20^\circ C$ and room temperature is $ + 10^\circ C$ when outside temperature is $ - 40^\circ C$. Find the temperature of the heater heating the room.
explanation:
Let $T$ is the temperature of the heater. The rate of change of temperature is
$\dfrac{d T}{d t}=-K\left(T-T_{0}\right)$
Here heat flows from heater to the room and and then from room the atmosphere. So at thermal equilibrium $K_{1}$ and $K_{2}$ are corresponding constants related to heater to room and room to atmosphere
In case (1)
$K_{1}\left(T-T_{r_{1}}\right)=K_{2}\left(T_{r_{1}}-T_{\text {out } 1}\right)$
and In case (2)
$K_{1}\left(T-T_{r_{2}}\right)=K_{2}\left(T_{r_{2}}-T_{\text {out } 2}\right)$
Dividing these equations
$\frac{{T - {T_{{r_1}}}}}{{T - {T_{{r_2}}}}} = \frac{{{T_{{r_1}}} - {T_{out{\rm{ }}1}}}}{{{T_{{r_2}}} - {T_{out{\rm{ }}1}}}}$
$ = \frac{{T - 20}}{{T - 10}} = \frac{{20 - ( - 20)}}{{10 - ( - 40)}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} T = 60^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A liquid takes 5 minutes to cool from $80^\circ C$ to $50^\circ C$. How much time will it take to cool from $T = 60^\circ C$ to $30^\circ C$ ? temperature of the surrounding is $20^\circ C$
explanation:
From Newton's law of cooling
$t=\dfrac{-1}{K} \log \left[\dfrac{\theta_{2}-\theta_{0}}{\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}}\right]$
Where $K$ is a constant and $\theta_{0}$ is the temperature of the surroundings
$\begin{aligned}& 5=\dfrac{-1}{K} \log \left[\dfrac{50-20}{80-20}\right] \\& t=\dfrac{-1}{K} \log \left(\dfrac{30-20}{60-20}\right) \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{5}{t}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{K} \log _{e} 2}{\dfrac{1}{K} \log 2^{2}} \\& \Rightarrow t=10 \text { minutes. }\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: When a calorimeter contains $40\,g$ of water at $50^\circ C$, then the temperature falls to $45^\circ C$ in 10 minutes. The same calorimeter contains $100\,g$of water at $50^\circ C$, it takes 20 minutes for the temperature to become $45^\circ C$. Find the water equivalent of the calorimeter.
explanation:
Let $W$ is the water equivalent of the calorimeter. $\dfrac{m_{1} s_{1}+W s_{1}}{t_{1}}=\dfrac{m_{2} s_{2}+W s_{2}}{t_{2}}$
where $W$ is the water equivalent
$\begin{gathered}\Rightarrow \dfrac{40 \times 1+W \times 1}{10}=\dfrac{100 \times 1+W \times 1}{20} \Rightarrow 80+2 W \\=100+W \Rightarrow W=20 g\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: A system $S$ receives heat continuously from an electrical heater of power $10\,W$. The temperature of  $S$ becomes constant at $50^\circ C$ when the surrounding temperature is $20^\circ C$. After the heater is switched off, $S$ cools from $35.1^\circ C$ to $34.9^\circ C$ in 1 minute. The heat capacity of $S$ is
explanation:
At equilibrium the heat gained from heater is equal to the heat lost to the surrounds.
When the heater is on.
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=k(50-20)=10$,
Where $k = $ constant
$\Rightarrow k=\dfrac{1}{3}$
At an average temperature of $35^\circ C$, heat lost to the surroundings is
$\dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{1}{3}(35-20) J / s=5 J / s$
Heat lost in 1 minute
$=\dfrac{d Q}{d t} \times 60 J$
$ = 5 \times 60\,J = 300\,J$
Decreases in temperature $ = 0.2^\circ C = \Delta \theta $
$Q=c \Delta \theta$
Heat capacity $ = c = \frac{Q}{{\Delta \theta }} = \frac{{300\,J}}{{{{0.2}^0}C}} = 1500\,J/^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A body with an initial temperature $\theta_{1}$ is allowed to cool in a surrounding which is at constant temperature of $\theta_{0}\left(\theta_{0} < \theta_{1}\right)$. Assume that Newton's law of cooling is obeyed. Let $k = $ constant. The temperature of the body after time $t$ is best expressed by
explanation:
$\dfrac{d \theta}{d t}=-k\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)$, where $k = $ constant.
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \int_{\theta_{i}}^{\theta} \dfrac{d \theta}{\theta-\theta_{0}}=-\int_{0}^{t} k \cdot d t \\& {\left[\ln \left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)\right]_{\theta_{i}}^{\theta}=-k t} \\& \ln \left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right)-\ln \left(\theta_{i}-\theta_{0}\right)=-k t \\& \dfrac{\theta-\theta_{0}}{\theta_{i}-\theta_{0}}=e^{-k t} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \theta=\theta_{0}+\left(\theta_{i}-\theta_{0}\right) e^{-k t} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A body cools from $50^\circ C$ to $49^\circ C$ in $5\,s$. How long will it take to cool from $40^\circ C$ to $39^\circ C$ ? (Assume temperature of surroundings as $30^\circ C$ and Newton's law of cooling is valid)
explanation:
From Newton's law of cooling,
$\dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}}{t} \propto\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right)$
where, $\theta_{0}=$ temperature of surroundings
$\therefore \frac{{50 - 49}}{{{t_1}}} \propto \left( {\frac{{50 + 49}}{2} - 30} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and ${\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \frac{{40 - 39}}{{{t_2}}} \propto \left( {\frac{{40 + 39}}{2} - 30} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing Eq. (2) by Eq. (1), we get
$ \Rightarrow {t_2} = \frac{{39}}{{19}} \times 5 = 10\,s$
subject: Physics

question: A hot body temperature $T$ looses heat to the surrounding temperature $T_{S}$ by radiation. If the difference in the temperature is small then, the rate of loss of heat by the hot body is proportional to
explanation:
The rate of loss of heat of a hot body is
$\frac{{dQ}}{{dt}}\alpha \left( {{T^4} - T_s^4} \right)$
$T = {T_s} + \Delta T\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(\Delta T\,{\text{is}}\,{\text{small}})$
${T^4} = {\left( {{T_s} + \Delta T} \right)^4} = T_s^4{\left( {1 + \frac{{\Delta T}}{{{T_s}}}} \right)^4} \approx T_s^4\left( {1 + \frac{{4\Delta T}}{{{T_s}}}} \right)$
${T^4} - T_s^4 = T_s^4\left( {1 + \frac{{4\Delta T}}{{{T_s}}}} \right) - T_s^4 = 4\Delta TT_s^3 = 4T_s^3\left( {T - {T_s}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dQ}}{{dt}}\alpha \left( {T - {T_s}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: A hot body placed in the air is cooled down according to Newton's law of cooling, the rate of decrease of temperature being $k$ times the temperature difference from the surrounding. Starting from $t=0$, find the time at which the temperature difference between body and surrounding is equal to half of the initial temperature difference.
explanation:
The newton's law of cooling equation is
$\begin{aligned}& \int_{\theta_{1}}^{\theta_{2}} \dfrac{d \theta}{\theta-\theta_{0}}=-k \int_{0}^{t} d t \\& \ln \left(\dfrac{\theta_{2}-\theta_{0}}{\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}}\right)=-k t \\& \theta_{2}-\theta_{0}=\left(\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}\right) e^{-k t}\end{aligned}$
Given that
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}}{2}=\theta_{2}-\theta_{0} \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{1}{2}=e^{-k t} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{\ln (2)}{k}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A bucket full of hot water cools from $75^\circ C$ to $70^\circ C$ in time $T_{1}$, from $70^\circ C$ to $65^\circ C$ in time $T_{2}$ and from $65^\circ C$ to $60^\circ C$ in time $T_{3}$, then
explanation:
According to Newton's law of cooling Rate of cooling $\propto$ Mean temperature difference
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{\text { Fall in temperature }}{\text { Time }} \propto\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}-\theta_{0}\right) \\& \because\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)_{1}>\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)_{2}>\left(\dfrac{\theta_{1}+\theta_{2}}{2}\right)_{3} \\& \Rightarrow T_{1} < T_{2} < T_{3}\end{aligned}$

question: A pendulum clock loses 10.8 seconds a day when the temperature is $38^\circ C.$ and gains 10.8 seconds a day when the temperature is $18^\circ C.$ The coefficient of linear expansion of the metal of the pendulum clock is
explanation:
Time loss/gain of the pendulum clock is
$\Delta T=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \Delta \theta T$
$\therefore \quad 10.8 = \frac{1}{2}\alpha (38 - \theta ) \times 86400\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Also, $1.8 = \frac{1}{2}\alpha (\theta - 18) \times 86400\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \,(2)$
Dividing eq(1) by (2), we get
$1 = \frac{{38 - \theta }}{{\theta - 18}} \Rightarrow \theta = 28^\circ C$
$\therefore$ From eq(1)
$10.8=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha(28-18) \times 86400$
$\therefore \alpha=2.5 \times 10^{-5}{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question: A piece of metal has a weight of $49\,g$ in air and $39\,g$ in a liquid of density $1.2 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ kept at $32^{\circ} {C}$. When the temperature of the liquid is raised to $42^{\circ} {C}$ the metal piece has a weight of $40\,g$. If the density of the liquid at $42^{\circ} {C}$ is $1.0 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$, then the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is
explanation:
The volume of metal at $32^{\circ} {C}$.
$V_{32^{\circ} {C}}=\dfrac{\text { loss of weight }}{\text { specific gravity } \times g}$
$=\dfrac{(49-39) g}{1.2 \times g}=8.33 {~cm}^{3}$
Similarly, volume of metal at $42^{\circ} {C}$.
$V_{42^{\circ} {C}}=\dfrac{(49-40) {g}}{1 {~g}}=9 {~cm}^{3}$
Now, $V_{42^{\circ} {C}}=V_{32^{\circ} {C}}\left[1+\gamma\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \gamma=\dfrac{V_{42^{\circ} {C}}-V_{32^{\circ} {C}}}{V_{32^{\circ} {C}}\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}=\dfrac{9-8.33}{8.33(42-32)}$
$=\dfrac{0.67}{83.3}=0.008 /{ }^{\circ} {C}$
$\alpha=\dfrac{\gamma}{3}=\dfrac{0.008}{3}=\dfrac{8}{3} \times 10^{-3} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ of same length, same radius and same Young's modulus are heated through the same range of temperatures. If the coefficient of linear expansion of $A$ is $3 / 2$ times that of $B$, then the ratio of the thermal stress produced in the two wires $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
Ratio of linear expansion coefficient of $A$
and $B=\dfrac{\alpha_{A}}{\alpha_{B}}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
Now, thermal stress $\sigma_{t}=Y \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$
$\therefore \dfrac{\left(\sigma_{t}\right)_{A}}{\left(\sigma_{t}\right)_{B}}=\dfrac{Y_{A} \cdot \alpha_{A} \cdot \Delta T}{Y_{B} \cdot \alpha_{B} \cdot \Delta T}=\dfrac{Y}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{3}{2}=\dfrac{3}{2}$,
$\left[\because Y_{A}=Y_{B}=Y\right]$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Two rods of same area of cross-section have lengths $L$ and $2\,L$ and coefficients of linear expansions $2 \alpha$ and $\alpha$ respectively. If they are welded to form a composite rod of length $3\,L$, then the coefficient of linear expansion of the composite rod is
explanation:
Initial length of composite rod
$=L+2 L=3 L$
Increase in length of $1^{\text {st }}$ rod
$\Delta L_{1}=L(2 \alpha) \Delta T$
Increment in length of $2^{\text {nd }}$ rod
$\Delta L_{2}=L \alpha^{\prime} \Delta T=2 L \alpha \Delta T\left[\because \alpha^{\prime}=2 \alpha\right]$
$\therefore$ Total length increment, $\Delta L=\Delta L_{1}+\Delta L_{2}$
$=2 L \alpha \Delta T+2 L \alpha \Delta T=4 L \alpha \Delta T$
If $\alpha_{c}$ is the coefficent of thermal expansion of the composite rod, then $\Delta T$
$4 L \alpha \Delta T=3 L \alpha_{c} \Delta T$
Where $\alpha_{c}$ is coefficient of linear expansion of composite rod.
$\Rightarrow \alpha_{c}=\dfrac{4}{3} \alpha$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Equivalent Temperature Coefficient of Linear Expansion

question: The length of a metal rod at $30^{\circ} {C}$ is $30\,cm$. If its temperature is raised to $105^{\circ} {C}$, its length is increased by $0.027\,cm$. Then, the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is
explanation:
Given,
Rise in temperature
$\Delta T=105^{\circ}-30^{\circ} {C}=75^{\circ} {C}$
Increase in length,
$\Delta l=0.027 {~cm}=0.027 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
We know that, $\Delta l=l \alpha \Delta T$
Thus, $0.027 \times 10^{-2}=0.30 \times \alpha \times 75$
$\Rightarrow \alpha=\dfrac{0.027 \times 10^{-2}}{0.30 \times 75}=0.0012 \times 10^{-2}$
$=12 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A blacksmith fixes circular iron frame on the wooden wheel of a bullock cart. The diameter of the wooden wheel and the circular iron frame  are $5.012\,m$ and $5\,m$ respectively at $27^{\circ} {C}$ The temperature (in ${ }^{\circ} {C}$) to which iron ring must be heated so as to fit the wooden wheel is (Coefficient of linear expansion of iron $=1.2 \times 10^{-5}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Thermal expansion of ring is given by
$\Delta L=L_{0} \alpha \Delta T$
where, $\Delta L=L_{1}-L_{0}$
$\Delta T=T_{2}-T_{1}$
$L_{0}=$ Initial diameter, $L_{1}=$ Final diameter
[ $L_{0}=$ Diameter of circular frame, $L_{1}=$ Diameter of wooden wheel]
$\Delta T=\dfrac{\Delta L}{\alpha L_{0}}$
$T_{2}-T_{1}=\dfrac{L_{1}-L_{0}}{\alpha L_{0}}$
$T_{2}-27^{\circ} {C}=\dfrac{5.012-5.000}{5 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}$
$\Rightarrow T_{2}=\dfrac{0.012}{5 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-5}}+27^{\circ} {C}=227^{\circ} {C}$
Hence, the required temperature is $227^{\circ} {C}$.
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Find the ratio of the length of a steel rod and a copper rod, if the steel rod is $4\,cm$ longer, than the copper rod at any temperature $T$. (The coefficient of linear expansion for steel and copper are $1.1 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$ and $1.7 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$, respectively)
explanation:
Thermal expansion of steel and copper are
$\Delta L_{\text {steel }}=L_{s} \alpha_{s} \Delta {t}$ and $\Delta L_{\text {copper }}=L_{c} \alpha_{c} \Delta t$
Given, $L_{s}-L_{C}=4 {~cm}$ for any temperature
$\therefore \quad L_{s} \alpha_{s} \Delta t=L_{c} \alpha_{c} \Delta t$
$\dfrac{L_{s}}{L_{c}}=\dfrac{\alpha_{c}}{\alpha_{s}}=\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-5}}=\dfrac{1.7}{1.1}=\dfrac{17}{11}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A hole of diameter $5\,cm$ is drilled in a metal sheet at $30^{\circ} {C}$. The linear expansion of the metal is $2 \times 10^{-5} K^{-1}$. The diameter of the hole when the temperature is raised to $230^{\circ} {C}$, is equal to
explanation:
The situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952663/preptracker/questions/q_110195_explanation_1764952662642.png $\alpha=2 \times 10^{-5} K^{-1} \Rightarrow T=230^{\circ} {C}, \Delta T=\left(T-T_{0}\right)$
we know,
$L = {L_0}[1 + a\Delta T]$
$ = 5\left[ {1 + 2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times (230 - 30)} \right]cm$
$L=5\left[1+\left(2 \times 10^{-5} \times 200\right)\right]=5.02 {~cm}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Two metal rods $A$ and $B$ each of length $50\,cm$ and diameter $4.0\,mm$ are joined together at temperature $30^{\circ} {C}$. What is the change in length of the combined rod at $230^{\circ} {C}$? (Given, linear expansion coefficients of rods $A$ and $B$ are respectively, $2.0 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$ and $\left.1.0 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} {C}\right)$
explanation:
The situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952661/preptracker/questions/q_110191_explanation_1764952661091.png Change in length of combined rod $=$ Change in length of $\operatorname{rod} A+$ Change in length of $\operatorname{rod} B$
So, $\Delta l=\Delta l_{A}+\Delta l_{B}=l_{A} \alpha_{A} \Delta T+l_{B} \alpha_{B} \Delta T$
Here, $\Delta T=T_{f}-T_{i}=230-30=200^{\circ} {C}$
$l_{A}=l_{B}=50 {~cm}=50 \times 10^{-2} {~m}$
$\alpha_{A}=2 \times 10^{-5}{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$ and $\alpha_{B}=1 \times 10^{-5}{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$
So, $\Delta l=\left(50 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times\right.$
$\left.200+50 \times 10^{-2} \times 1 \times 10^{-5} \times 200\right) {m}$
$=3 \times 10^{-3} {~m}=3 {~mm}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Equivalent Temperature Coefficient of Linear Expansion

question: A circular copper ring at $30^{\circ} {C}$ has a hole with an area of $9.98 {~cm}^{2}$. It is made to slip onto a steel rod of cross-sectional area of $10 {~cm}^{2}$, by raising the temperature of both ring and rod simultaneously by an amount of $\Delta T$. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the copper and steel are $17 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$ and $11 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$, respectively, then minimum value of $\Delta T$ should be
explanation:
The situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952660/preptracker/questions/q_110189_explanation_1764952659566.png Ring just slips over the rod when area of hole is equal to the area of cross-section of the rod.
$\Rightarrow A_{\text {ring }}$ at $T_{2}$ temperature $=A_{\text {rod }}$ at $T_{2}$temperature
$\Rightarrow\left[A_{0}(1+\beta \Delta T)\right]_{\text {ring }}=\left[A_{0}(1+\beta \Delta T)\right]_{\text {rod }}$
Here, $A_{0}($ ring $)=9.98 {~cm}^{2}$
$A_{0}({rod})=10 {~cm}^{2}$
$\beta_{\text {ring }}=2 \times 17 \times 10^{-6} C^{-1} \quad(\because \beta=2 \alpha)$
$\beta_{\text {rod }}=2 \times 11 \times 10^{-6} C^{-1}$
So, we have from Eq. (1),
$9.98\left(1+2 \times 17 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \Delta T\right)$
$=10\left(1+2 \times 11 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \Delta T\right)$
or $998+2 \times 998 \times 17 \times 10^{-6} \Delta T$
$=1000+2 \times 1000 \times 11 \times 10^{-6} \Delta T$
$\Rightarrow 2 \times 10^{-6} \Delta T(17 \times 998-1000 \times 11)$
$=1000-998$
$\Rightarrow 2 \times 10^{-6} \Delta T(5966)=2$
$\Rightarrow \Delta T=\dfrac{10^{6}}{5966}=167.61^{\circ} {C}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of coefficient of linear expansion to the co - efficient of areal expansion of a rectangular sheet of a  solid is
explanation:
We know that,
$\alpha=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l_{0} \Delta T}$
A real expansion is given by,
$\beta=\dfrac{\Delta A}{A_{0} \Delta T}=\dfrac{2 \Delta l}{l_{0} \Delta T}$
where, $\beta=$ a real expansion,
$\Delta A=$ change in area and $A_{0}=$ initial area.
$\therefore \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta}=\dfrac{\dfrac{\Delta l}{l_{0} \Delta T}}{2 \dfrac{\Delta l}{l_{0} \Delta T}}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A metal ball initially at a pressure of $10^{5} {~Pa}$ is heated from $20^{\circ} {C}$ to $127^{\circ} {C}$ keeping its volume constant. The coefficient of linear expansion of metal is $10^{-5}{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$ and bulk modulus of metal is $2 \times 10^{11} {~N} / {m}^{2}$. The pressure inside the ball becomes
explanation:
As we know that,
$B=\dfrac{-P}{\Delta V / V}$
where, $\Delta V / V$ is volumetric strain.
$ \Rightarrow \quad P = - B\frac{{\Delta V}}{V}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and since $-\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}=\gamma \Delta T$
where, $\gamma=$ thermal coefficient of volumetric expansion.
From equation (1) we get
$\therefore P=B \gamma \Delta T$
$=B 3 \alpha \Delta T \quad(\because \gamma=3 \alpha)$
$=2 \times 10^{11} \times 3 \times 10^{-5}(127-20) {N} / {m}^{2}$
$=6 \times 10^{6}(107)=642 \times 10^{6} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
$=6.42 \times 10^{8} {~N} / {m}^{2}$
$\simeq 6 \times 10^{8} {~Pa}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: The moment of inertia $I$ of a uniform rod about a perpendicular bisector increases to $I+\Delta I$, if the temperature is increased slightly by $\Delta T$. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the material of the rod is $\alpha$, then $\Delta I / I$ is (Assume $\dfrac{\Delta T}{T} \ll 1$)
explanation:
The initial moment of inertia of rod about perpendicular bisector,
$I = \frac{1}{{12}}M{L^2}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
After increasing temperature by $\Delta T$ moment of inertia, becomes
$I+\Delta I=\dfrac{1}{12} M(L+\Delta L)^{2}$
$I\left(1+\dfrac{\Delta I}{I}\right)=\dfrac{1}{12} M L^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}\right)^{2}$
Due to thermal linear expansion, $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}=\alpha \Delta t$
$\therefore I\left( {1 + \frac{{\Delta I}}{I}} \right) = I\left( {1 + 2\frac{{\Delta L}}{L}} \right)$
${\mkern 1mu} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \left[ {\because {{\left( {\frac{{\Delta L}}{L}} \right)}^2} < < 1} \right]$
$\Rightarrow 1+\dfrac{\Delta I}{I}=1+2 \alpha \Delta T$
$\therefore \dfrac{\Delta I}{I}=2 \alpha \Delta T$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A steel rod at $25^{\circ} {C}$ is observed to be  $1\,m$   long when measured by another metal scale which is correct at $0^{\circ} {C}$. The exact length of steel rod at $0^{\circ} {C}$ is  $({\alpha _{{\text{steel}}}} = 12 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$   and    ${\alpha _{{\text{metal}}}} = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C)$
explanation:
As both steel rod and metal scale expands at $25^{\circ} {C}$, we use $\left(\theta^{\prime}-\theta\right)=(25-0)^{\circ} {C}$
True value of length of rod at $25^{\circ} {C}$
$=$ Scale reading
$ \times \left( {1 + {\alpha _{{\rm{metal }}}}\left( {{\theta ^\prime } - \theta } \right)} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Substituting values in Eq. (1), we get
Length of rod at $25^{\circ} {C}$
$=1 \times\left[1+20 \times 10^{-6}(25-0)\right] {m}$
$=1+5 \times 10^{-4}=1.0005 {~m}$
Now, if $l_{2}=$ length of steel rod at $25^{\circ} {C}$ and
$l_{1}=$ length of steel rod at $0^{\circ} {C}$, then we have
$l_{2}=l_{1}\left(1+\alpha_{\text {steel }} \Delta \theta\right)$
Substituting values, we have
$1.0005=l_{1}\left[1+12 \times 10^{-6} \times(25-0)\right]$
$\Rightarrow l_{1}=\dfrac{1.0005}{1.0003} m=1.000199 m \approx 1.0002 m$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A solid cylinder of radius $r_{1}=2.5 {~cm}$, length $l_{1}=5.0 {~cm}$ and temperature $40^{\circ} {C}$ is suspended in an environment of temperature $60^{\circ} {C}$. The thermal radiation transfer rate for the cylinder is $1.0\,W$. If the cylinder is stretched until its radius becomes $r_{2}=0.50 {~cm}$, the thermal radiation transfer rate is changed to
explanation:
$\Rightarrow$ Rate of heat transfer $\propto$ Surface area of body
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{Q_2}}}{{{Q_1}}} = \frac{{{S_2}}}{{{S_1}}} \Rightarrow {Q_2} = {Q_1} \times \frac{{{S_2}}}{{{S_1}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Now, volume remains same in stretching, so
$\pi r_{1}^{2} l_{1}=\pi r_{2}^{2} l_{2}$
$\Rightarrow l_{2}=\dfrac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}} \cdot l_{1}=\dfrac{(2.5)^{2}}{(0.5)^{2}} \times 5=125 {~cm}$
So, area before stretching, $S_{1}=2 \pi r_{1}\left(l_{1}+r_{1}\right)$
and area after stretching, $S_{2}=2 \pi r_{2}\left(l_{2}+r_{2}\right)$
Hence by Eq. (1), we get
$Q_{2}=Q_{1} \times \dfrac{S_{2}}{S_{1}}=Q_{1} \times \dfrac{2 \pi r_{2}\left(l_{2}+r_{2}\right)}{2 \pi r_{1}\left(l_{1}+r_{1}\right)}$
$=Q_{1} \times \dfrac{r_{2}\left(l_{2}+r_{2}\right)}{r_{1}\left(l_{1}+r_{1}\right)}$
$=\dfrac{1 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-2}(125+0.5) \times 10^{-2}}{2.5 \times 10^{-2}(5+2.5) \times 10^{-2}} W$
$=3.346=3.35 {~W}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A sheet of steel at $20^{\circ} {C}$ has size as shown in the figure below. If the co-efficient of linear expansion for steel is $10^{-5}{ }^{\circ} C^{-1}$, then what is the change in the area at $60^{\circ} {C}$ ?
explanation:
As we know that, coefficient of area expansion is
$\beta=\dfrac{\Delta A}{A \times \Delta T} \Rightarrow 2 \alpha=\dfrac{\Delta A}{A \times \Delta T} \quad[\because \beta=2 \alpha]$
$\Delta A=2 \alpha \times A \times \Delta T$
$=2 \times 10^{-5} \times 40 \times 20 \times 10^{-4} \times(60-20) {m}^{2}$
$=0.64 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^{2}=0.64 {~cm}^{2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: If $\alpha_{v}$ and $T$ are the coefficient of volume expansion and temperature for an ideal gas respectively, then
explanation:
Change in volume due to increase of temperature ( $\Delta T$ ) is given by
$\Delta V = {\alpha _V}V\Delta T\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For an ideal gas, we know that
$PV = nRT\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$ \Rightarrow P\Delta V = nR\Delta T \Rightarrow \Delta V = \frac{{nR\Delta T}}{P}{\rm{ }}\left( 3 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (3), we get
$\alpha_{v} \cdot V \Delta T=\dfrac{n R \Delta T}{P}$
$\Rightarrow \alpha_{v}=\dfrac{n R}{P V}=\dfrac{n R}{n R T} \quad[$ from Eq.(2) $]$
$\Rightarrow \alpha_{v}=\dfrac{1}{T}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: The area of a circular copper coin increases by $0.4 \%$ when its temperature is raised by $100^{\circ} {C}$. The coefficient, of linear expansion of the coin is :
explanation:
Given, increase in area, $\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}=0.004$
Increase in temperature, $\Delta T=100^{\circ} {C}$ We know that, the coefficient of areal expansion $\beta$ is given by
$\beta=\left(\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta T}=\dfrac{0.004}{100}=4 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$
$\therefore$ Coefficient of linear expansion, of the coin is
$\alpha=\dfrac{\beta}{2}=\dfrac{4 \times 10^{-5}}{2} /{ }^{\circ} {C}=2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} {C}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: The pressure to be applied to the ends of a steel cylinder to keep its length constant upon raising its temperature by $100^{\circ} {C}$ is (thermal expansion coefficient, $\alpha=11 \times 10^{-6} / K$, Young's modulus $=200 {GPa}$)
explanation:
Pressure $=$ Thermal stress
$=$ Young's modulus $\times$ Thermal strain
$=Y \alpha \times \Delta T$
$ = 200 \times {10^9} \times 11 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 100\,Pa$
$ = 0.22 \times {10^9}\,Pa$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The resistance of a thermometer is $100 \Omega$ at the triple point of water ($273 K$) and is $300 \Omega$ at the melting point of gold $(\sim 873 {~K})$. The temperature at which the resistance of the thermometer is $200 \Omega$ is
explanation:
By the formula, $\theta=\dfrac{R_{\theta}-R_{0}}{R_{600}-R_{0}} \times 600$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{{200 - 100}}{{300 - 100}} \times 600$
$\theta = 300^\circ C \Rightarrow \theta = 300 + 273 = 573\,K$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A second's pendulum clock has a steel wire. The clock is calibrated at $20^{\circ} {C}$. How much time   does the clock lose or gain in one week when the temperature is increased to $30^{\circ} {C} ?\left(\alpha_{\text {steel }}=1.2 \times 10^{-5}{ }^{\circ}\,\, {C}^{-1}\right)$.
explanation:
The time period of second s pendulum is 2 $sec$ . As the temperature increases length time period increases.Clock becomes slow and it loses the time.The change in time period is
$\Delta T = \frac{1}{2}T\alpha \Delta \theta = \left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)(2)\left( {1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,({\rm{ }}30^\circ - 20^\circ ){\rm{ }}$
$ = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,s$
$\therefore $New time period is
$T' = T + \Delta T = \left( {2 + 1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right) = 2.00012\,s$
Time lost in one week
$\Delta = \left( {\frac{{\Delta T}}{{{T^\prime }}}} \right)t = \frac{{\left( {1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)}}{{(2.00012)}}$
${\rm{(}}7 \times 24 \times 3600{\rm{)}} = 36.28\,s$
subject: Physics

question:
A fine steel wire of length $4\,m$ is fixed rigidly in a heavy brass frame as shown in figure. It is just taut at ${20^{\circ} {C}}$. Find the tensile stress developed in steel wire if whole system is heated to ${120^{\circ} {C}}$.
(Given ${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\alpha _{{\text{brass}}}} = 1.8 \times {10^{ - 50}}{C^{ - 1}},$
${\alpha _{{\text{steel}}}} = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 50}}{C^{ - 1}},$
${{Y_{{\rm{steel }}}} = 20 + {{10}^{11}}N{m^{ - 2}},{Y_{{\rm{brass }}}}}$ $\left. { = 1.7 \times {{10}^7}N{m^{ - 2}}} \right)$
explanation:
Stress ${=Y(}$ strain ${)=Y_{S}\left(\alpha_{b}-\alpha_{s}\right) \Delta T}$
$ = \left( {2 \times {{10}^{11}}} \right)\left( {0.6 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}} \right)(100)$
$ = 120 \times {10^6}N{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two rods $A$ and $B$ of identical dimensions are at temperature $40^\circ C.$ If  $A$ is heated upto $160^\circ C$ and $B$  upto $T^\circ C$ then new lengths are the same. If the ratio of the coefficients of linear expansion of $B$ and $A$ is $2: 5$, then  find  the value of  $T$ is
explanation:
Let initial length of identical rods be ${l_0}$. Thermal expansion in length,
$\Delta l = {l_0}\alpha (\Delta T) = {l_0}\alpha \left( {{T_2} - {T_1}} \right)$
So, change in length of rods are,
$\Delta {l_A} = {l_0}{\alpha _A}(160 - 40)$
$\Delta {l_B} = {l_0}{\alpha _B}(T - 40)$
Since new lengths are same,
$\therefore \Delta {l_A} = \Delta {l_B}$
$\therefore {l_0}{\alpha _A} \times 120 = {l_0}{\alpha _B}(T - 40)$
$\therefore \frac{{{\alpha _A}}}{{{\alpha _B}}} = \frac{{(T - 40)}}{{120}}$
$\frac{{T - 40}}{{120}} = \frac{5}{2}$
$T - 40 = \frac{5}{2} \times 120 = 300$
$T = 300 + 40 = 340^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A steel rod of length $50\,cm$ has a cross-sectional area of $0.4\,c{m^2}$. What force would be required to stretch this rod by the same amount as the expansion produced by heating it through $10^\circ C$. $\left( {\alpha  = {{10}^{ - 5}}\,{K^{ - 1}}} \right.$ and $\left. {Y = 2 \times {{10}^{11}}\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}} \right)$
explanation:
We have, Force $ = YA\,\alpha \,.\,\Delta \theta $
$ = 2 \times {10^{11}} \times 0.4 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 10$
$ = 0.8 \times {10^3} = 800\,N$
subject: Physics

question: For a certain thermocouple, if the temperature of the cold junction is  $0^\circ C,$  the neutral temperature and inversion temperatures are  $285^\circ C$ and $570^\circ C,$ respectively. If the cold junction is brought to $10^\circ C,$ then the new neutral and inversion temperatures are respectively
explanation:
Neutral temperature remains same.
Inversion temperature, ${T_i} = 2{T_n} - {T_c} = 2 \times 285 - 10 = 560^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A vertical column \[50\;cm\] long at \[50^\circ C\] balances another column of same liquid \[60\;cm\] long at \[100^\circ C.\] The coefficient of absolute expansion of the liquid is
explanation:
$\dfrac{h_{1}}{h_{2}}=\dfrac{\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}}=\dfrac{\left(1+\gamma \theta_{1}\right)}{\left(1+\gamma \theta_{2}\right)}$
$\left[ {\because \rho = \frac{{{\rho _0}}}{{(1 + \gamma \,\theta )}}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{50}}{{60}} = \frac{{1 + 50\,\gamma }}{{1 + 100\,\gamma }} \Rightarrow r = 0.005/^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: Coefficient of cubical expansion of water is zero at
explanation: Coefficient of cubical expansion of water at $4^\circ C$ is zero, because the density of water at $4^\circ c$ is maximum.
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of real expansion of mercury is  $0.18 \times {10^{ - 3}}{C^{\, - 1}}.$  If the density of mercury at $0^\circ C$ is $13.6\,g/cc,$ its density at  $473\,K$ will be
explanation:
Here, $t_{1}=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=273 \mathrm{~K}, t_{2}=473 \mathrm{~K}$
$\begin{aligned}& \gamma_{r}=0.18 \times 10^{-3} C^{-1} \\& d_{1}=13.6 \mathrm{~g} / c c, d_{2}=?\end{aligned}$
$d_{2}=\dfrac{d_{1}}{1+\gamma_{r}(\Delta t)}$
$ = \frac{{13.6}}{{1 + 0.18 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times (473 - 273)}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {d_2} = \frac{{13.6}}{{1.036}}$
$ = 13.12\,g/cc$
subject: Physics

question: How much should the temperature of a brass rod be increased so as to increase its length by $1 \%$ ? (Take, $\alpha$ for brass is $0.00002^{\circ} C^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Here, $\Delta T=$ ?, $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}=\dfrac{1}{100}$,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,a = 0.00002^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
${\rm{ As }}\,\quad \Delta L = \alpha L\Delta T$
$\therefore \quad \,\,\alpha \Delta T = \frac{{\Delta L}}{L}$
${\rm{or}}\quad \Delta T = \frac{{\Delta L}}{{L\,\alpha }} = \frac{1}{{100 \times 0.00002}}$
$\Delta T = \frac{{{{10}^5}}}{{2 \times {{10}^2}}} = 500^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: The wooden wheel of radius $R$ is made of two semicircular parts (see figure). The two parts are held together by a ring made of a metal strip of cross-sectional area $S$ and length $L . L$ is slightly less than $2 \pi R$. To fit the ring on the wheel, it is heated so that its temperature rises by $\Delta T$ and it just steps over the wheel. As it cools down to surrounding temperature, it presses the semicircular parts together. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha$ and its Young's modulus is $Y$, the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part is
explanation:
Elongation due to change in temperature.
$\Delta \,l = L\,\alpha \,\Delta T\quad \quad \quad (1)$
where,
$\alpha=$ coefficient of linear expansion which is compensated by elastic strain when temperature becomes normal,
$i.\,e.\quad \Delta l = \frac{{TL}}{{YS}}\quad \quad \quad (2)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$\frac{{TL}}{{YS}} = L\,\alpha \,\Delta \,T$
$T = YS\,\alpha \,\Delta T$
At equilibrium, force exerted by one-half on other,
$F=2 T=2 Y S \alpha \Delta T$
subject: Physics

question: The variation of lengths of two metal rods ${A}$ and ${B}$ with change in temperature is shown in the figure. The ratio of ${\dfrac{\alpha_{A}}{\alpha_{B}}}$ is
explanation:
${l=l_{0}(1+\alpha \Delta T) \Rightarrow l-l_{0}=l_{0} \alpha \Delta T}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{l_0}{\alpha _A}\Delta T}}{{{l_0}{\alpha _B}\Delta T}} = \frac{{104 - 100}}{{106 - 100}} = \frac{4}{6}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\alpha _A}}}{{{\alpha _B}}} = \frac{2}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: Two large holes are cut in a metal sheet. If this is heated, distances ${A B}$ and ${B C}$, (as shown)
explanation: On heating, the expansion will take place, hence both the distances will increase.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A metallic bar of Young's modulus, ${0.5 \times 10^{11} {Nm}^{-2}}$ and coefficient of linear thermal expansion ${10^{-5^{\circ}} {C}^{-1}}$, length 1 m and area of cross-section ${10^{-3} {~m}^{2}}$ is heated from ${0^{\circ} {C}}$ to ${100^{\circ} {C}}$ without expansion or bending. The compressive force developed in it is:
explanation:
Thermal strain ${=}$ Longitudinal strain ${=\alpha \Delta T}$
${\Rightarrow}$ Longitudinal strain, ${=10^{-5} \times 10^{2}=10^{-3}}$
${\Rightarrow}$ Compressive stress ${=}$ Strain ${\times}$ Young's Modulus${=10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{11}}$
${=0.5 \times 10^{8}}$
${\Rightarrow}$ Compressive force
${=0.5 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{-3}=50 \times 10^{3} {~N}}$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of volumetric expansion of lamina is $5 \times {10^5}/^\circ C$. Its coefficient of linear expansion will be :
explanation: $\alpha = \frac{\gamma }{3} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{3} = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: The coefficient of linear expansion of a metal is $1 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$. The percentage increase in area of a square plate of that metal when it is heated through $100^\circ C$ is
explanation:
$\beta=2 \alpha, \dfrac{\Delta A}{A} \times 100=\beta \Delta t 100$
$=2 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 \times 100=0.2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A metal plate of area $1.2\,{m^2}$ increases its area by $2.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^2}$ when it is heated from $0^\circ C$ to $100^\circ C$. The coefficient of cubical expansion of the metal expressed in per $^\circ C$ is
explanation:
$\beta=\dfrac{A_{2}-A_{1}}{A_{1}\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}, \gamma=\dfrac{3}{2} \beta$
$\Rightarrow 2.4 \times 10^{-4}=1.2 \times \dfrac{2 \gamma}{3} \times 100$
$\Rightarrow \gamma=3 \times 10^{-6}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: Ratio among linear expansion coefficient $(\alpha)$, areal expansion $(\beta)$ and volume expansion coefficient $(\gamma)$ is
explanation: $\alpha=\dfrac{\beta}{2}=\dfrac{\gamma}{3} \Rightarrow \alpha: \beta: \gamma=1: 2: 3$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A sheet of brass is $100\,cm$ long and $40\,cm$ broad at $273\,K$. The area of the surface increase by 2 $c{m^2}$ at $100^\circ C$. What is the coefficient of linear expansion of brass?
explanation:
$\frac{{\Delta A}}{{{A_0}\Delta T}} = \beta = \frac{2}{{4000 \times 100}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 6}}^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
$\alpha = \frac{\beta }{2} = 2.5 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\rm{/}}^\circ C$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: The volume of a metal block is $4000\,c{m^3}$ at 273 $K$. What would be its volume at $373 K$ ? (give $\alpha$ of the metal is $18 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
$V_{t}=V_{0}(1+\gamma \Delta T) \Rightarrow V_{0}=4 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~m}^{3}$
$\alpha=\dfrac{\gamma}{3} \Rightarrow \gamma=3 \alpha=3 \times 18 \times 10^{-6}$
$\therefore V_{t}=V_{0}(1+\gamma \Delta T)$
$\Rightarrow V_{t}=4 \times 10^{-3}\left(1+3 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{2}\right)$
$V=4 \times 10^{-3}\left(1+5.4 \times 10^{-3}\right)$
$V = 4.002 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,{m^3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A rod is fixed between two rigid supports at $50^\circ C$. The coefficient of linear expansion of material of $rod\,2 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$ and young's modulus is $1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}$. What is the stress developed in the rod if temperature of rod becomes $70^\circ C$?
explanation:
Stress developed in the rod due to heating is $\gamma \alpha \Delta \theta$
Stress $=2 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 \times 10^{11} \times 20^{\circ}$
$ = 40 \times 1.5 \times {10^6} = 6 \times {10^7}\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question:
A thin copper wire of length $L$ increases in length by one percent when heated from
${t_1}^\circ C$ and ${t_2}^\circ C$. The percentage change in area when a thin copper plate having dimension $2 L \times L$ is heated from ${t_1}^\circ C$ to ${t_2}^\circ C$ is
explanation:
$\dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100=1$
$\Rightarrow \alpha(\Delta t)=10^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta A}{A} \times 100=2 \alpha(\Delta t) 100=2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question:
The diameter of iron wheel is $1\,cm$. If its temperature is increased by $700^\circ C$ what is the increase in circumference of the wheel?
$\left( {\alpha = 12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}/^\circ C} \right)$
explanation:
Increase is circumference $=\pi D \alpha \Delta t$ where $D=$ diameter of the wheel
circumference, $C = \pi D$
Increase is circumference, $\Delta C = \left( {\alpha \,\Delta \,T} \right)\pi D$
$ = 12 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 400 \times \pi \times 1\,cm$
$ = 0.0264\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: If a cylinder of diameter $1.0\,cm$ at $30^\circ C$ is to be slid into a hole of diameter $0.9997\,cm$ in a steel plate present at the same temperature, the minimum required rise in the temperature of the plate is : (Coefficient of linear expansion of steel $\left. { = 12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}/^\circ C} \right)$
explanation:
$r_{2}=r_{1}(1+\alpha \Delta \theta) \Rightarrow \Delta r=r \alpha(\Delta \theta)$
$\Delta r = 0.5\,cm - 0.49985\,cm = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,cm$
$1.5 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,cm = 0.5\,cm \times 12 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times \Delta \theta $
$ \Rightarrow \Delta \theta = 25^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A bimetal made of copper and iron strips welded together is straight at room temperature. It is held vertically so that the iron strip is towards the left hand and copper strip is towards right hand. The bimetal strip is then heated. The bimetal strip will $\left( {{\alpha _{cu}} > {\alpha _{Fe}}} \right)$
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956039/preptracker/questions/q_38269_explanation_1764956038984.png More a metal bend on convex shape when heated. So correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: Two metal rods have coefficients of linear expansion $1.1 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$ and $1.65 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$ respectively. The difference in lengths is $10{\rm{ }}cm$ at all temperatures. Their initial lengths must be respectively .
explanation:
Given that the difference in lengths remain same
$\Delta l_{1}=\Delta l_{2}$
$l_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta \theta=l_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta \theta$
$ \Rightarrow {l_1}{\alpha _1} = {l_2}{\alpha _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${l_1} - {l_2} = 10{\rm{ }}({\rm{Given}})\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's (1) and (2)
$\Rightarrow l_{1}-\dfrac{l_{1} \alpha_{1}}{\alpha_{2}}=10$
$ \Rightarrow {l_1} = \frac{{10{\alpha _2}}}{{{\alpha _2} - {\alpha _1}}} = \frac{{10 \times 1.65 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{0.55 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 30\,cm$
$ \Rightarrow {l_2} = 20\,cm$.
subject: Physics

question:
A clock while keeps correct time at $ 30^\circ C$ has a pendulum rod made of brass. The number of seconds it gains (or) loses per second when the temperature falls to $ 10^\circ C$ is
$\left[\alpha\right.$ brass $\left. { = 18 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}/^\circ C} \right]$
explanation:
The loss or gain in time per day is
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{\Delta T}{T} & =\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \Delta \theta \\& =\dfrac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 \\& =180 \times 10^{-6}=18 \times 10^{-5} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In pressure-temperature $(P-T)$ phase diagram of water, the slope of the melting curve is found to be negative.


Reason: Ice contracts on melting to water
explanation:
In the pressure-temperature $(P-T)$ phase diagram of water, the melting curve typically has a negative slope. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956034/preptracker/questions/q_38190_explanation_1764956034188.png On either side of melting curve (AB), we have ice and water. By supplying heat, we increase the temperature of ice. Clearly, pressure is decreasing in that process.
$\Rightarrow$ Slope is negative.
This is due to the change of phase, as the transition from ice to liquid water involves a decrease in volume (contraction), which is why the slope is negative. So, both the Assertion and Reason are correct, and the Reason provides the correct explanation for the Assertion.
So, correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of cubical expansion of mercury is $0.00018/^\circ C$ and that of brass $0.00006/^\circ C$. If a barometer having a brass scale were to read $74.5\,cm$ at $30^\circ C$, find the true barometric height at$0^\circ C$. The scale is supposed to be correct at $15^\circ C$.
explanation:
As, ${\alpha _{brass{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{{\gamma {\,_{brass{\rm{ }}}}}}{3} = \frac{{0.00006}}{3}$
$ = 0.00002 = 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}{\rm{/}}{}^0C$
The brass scale is true at $15^\circ C$, therefore at $30^\circ C$, its graduations will increase in length and so observed reading will be less than actual reading at $30^{\circ}$.
$\therefore$ The change in reading,
$\Delta l = l{\alpha _{{\rm{brass }}}}(\Delta T)$
$ = 74.5 \times 2 \times {10^{ - 5}}(30 - 15)$
$ = 0.02235\,cm$
$\therefore \quad$ Actual reading at $30^\circ C$
${l_{30}} = {l_{{\rm{observed }}}} + \Delta l$
$ = 74.5 + 0.02235$
$ = 74.522\,cm$
Assuming area of cross - section A to be constant, we have
$V_{0} \rho_{0}=V_{30} \rho_{30}$
$\Rightarrow A h_{0} \rho_{0}=A h_{30} \rho_{30}$
$\Rightarrow$ true height at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be ;
$h_{0}=h_{30} \dfrac{\rho_{30}}{\rho_{0}}=\dfrac{h_{30}}{\left(1+\gamma_{H g} \Delta T\right)}$
Here, $V_{2}=\dfrac{3}{4} V_{0}, V_{1}=\dfrac{2}{3} V_{0}$
$ \Rightarrow {h_0} = \frac{{74.522}}{{1 + 0.00018 \times 30}} = \frac{{74.522}}{{1.0054}}$
$ = 74.122\,cm$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A non-conducting body floats in a liquid at $20^\circ C$with $2 / 3$ of its volume immersed in the liquid. When liquid temperature is increased to $100^\circ C,3{\rm{/}}4$ of body's volume is immersed in the liquid. Then, the coefficient of real expansion of the liquid is (neglecting the expansion of container of the liquid)
explanation:
Coefficient of real expansion,
$\gamma_{R}=\dfrac{V_{2}-V_{1}}{V_{1}\left(t_{2}-t_{1}\right)}$
Here ${V_2} = \frac{3}{4}{V_0},\,{V_1} = \frac{2}{3}{V_0}$
and$\left( {{t_2} - {t_1}} \right) = (100 - 20) = 80^\circ C$
$\therefore {\gamma _R} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{3}{4} - \frac{2}{3}} \right)}}{{\frac{2}{3}(80)}} = \frac{1}{{640}}$
$ = 15.6 \times {10^{ - 4}}^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: A clock with a metal pendulum beating seconds keeps correct time at $0^\circ {\rm{C}}$. If it loses $12.5\,s$ a day at $25^\circ C$, the coefficient of linear expansion of metal pendulum is
explanation:
Number of seconds lost in a day,
$\Delta t=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \Delta \theta \times 86400$
The coefficient of linear expansion of metal pendulum,
$\alpha=\dfrac{2 \Delta t}{\Delta \theta \times 86400}=\dfrac{2 \times 12.5}{25 \times 86400}$
$ \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{1}{{86400}}/^\circ C$
So, correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A bimetallic strip consists of metals $X$ and $Y$. It is mounted rigidly at the base as shown. The metal $X$ has a higher coefficient of expansion compared to that for metal $Y$. When bimetallic strip is placed in a cold bath,
explanation:
The metal $X$ has a higher coefficient of expansion compared to that for metal $Y$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956400/preptracker/questions/q_38175_explanation_1764956398215.png Bending takes place. As both $X$ and $Y$ are of same width, outer circumference is to be more than inner circumference.
Hence, the strip overall will bend towards the left.
So correct option is (2).

options.text: It will bend towards the right
It will bend towards the left
It will not bend but shrink
It will neither bend nor shrink

subject: Physics

question: A thin bimetallic strip is rigidly attached on its bottom surface as shown. Coefficient of linear expansion of strip A is more than strip B. If temperature of system is decreased, find correct option among the following
explanation: As metal A contracts more than metal $B\left(\because \alpha_{A}>\alpha_{B}\right)$, the bimetallic strip bends more towards the side of $A$. So ption (1) is correct
subject: Physics

question: A rod, of length $L$ at room temperature and uniform area of cross-section $A$, is made of a metal having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha /^\circ C$. It is observed that an external compressive force $F$, is applied on each of its ends, prevents any change in the length of the rod, when its temperature rises by $\Delta TK$. Young's modulus $Y$, for this metal is
explanation:
As the temperature of rod rises,
Extension in the rod, $\Delta L = L\alpha \Delta T$
Young's modulus of the rod, $Y = \frac{{F/A}}{{\Delta L/L}}$
$Y = \frac{{FL}}{{A\Delta L}} = \frac{{FL}}{{AL\alpha \Delta T}} = \frac{F}{{A\alpha \Delta T}}$
subject: Physics

question: A steel rail of length $5\,m$ and area of cross section $40\,c{m^2}$ is prevented from expanding along its length while the temperature rises by $10^\circ C$. If coefficient of linear expansion and young's modulus of steel are $1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$ and $2 \times {10^{11}}N{m^{ - 2}}$ respectively, the force developed in the rail is approxmately:
explanation:
$Y=\dfrac{F / A}{\Delta l / l} \Rightarrow F=\dfrac{Y A \Delta l}{l}$
$F=Y A \alpha \Delta T$
( where $\alpha \Delta T$ is the thermal strain )
$ = 2 \times {10^{11}} \times 40 \times {10^{ - 4}} \times 1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 10$
$ = 9.6 \times {10^4} = 1 \times {10^5}\,N$
subject: Physics

question: A compressive force, $F$ is applied at the two ends of a long thin steel rod. It is heated, simultaneously, Such that its temperature  increases by $\Delta T$. The net charge in its length is zero. Let $l$ be the length of the rod, A its area of cross-section, $Y$ its Young's modulus, and $\alpha$ its coefficient of linear expansions. then $F$ is equal to:
explanation:
Due to thermal expansion, change in length
$\begin{gathered}Y=\dfrac{F / A}{\Delta l / l} \\F=A Y \alpha \Delta T \quad\left(\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}=\alpha \Delta T\right)\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: A simple pendulum made of a bob of mass $m$ and a metallic wire of negligible mass has time period $2\,s$ at $T = 0^\circ C$. If the temperature of the wire is increased and the corresponding change in its time period is plotted against its temperature, the resulting graph is a line of slop $S$. If the coefficient of linear expansion of metal is $\alpha$ then the value of $S$ is :
explanation:
The initial time period is
$t_{\mathrm{o}}=2=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l_{0}}{g}}$
The initial time period after rise in temperature is
$\begin{aligned}& t=2 \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{l_{\mathrm{o}}(1+\alpha T)}{g}} \\& =2(1+\alpha T)^{1 / 2}=2+\alpha T \\& t-t_{0}=\alpha T \Rightarrow \Delta t=\alpha T\end{aligned}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955989/preptracker/questions/q_37890_explanation_1764955989204.png
subject: Physics

question: On a linear temperature scale $Y$, water freezes at $ - 160^\circ Y$ and boils at $ - 500^\circ Y$. On this Y scale, a temperature of $340\,K$ would be read as: (Water freezes at $273\,K$ and boils at $373\,K$)
explanation:
The two scales are shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956015/preptracker/questions/q_37886_explanation_1764956015304.png
From the figure we can write
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{-50^{\circ} Y-\left(-160^{\circ} Y\right)}{x-\left(-160^{\circ} Y\right)}=\dfrac{37 K-273 K}{340 K-273 K} \\& \Rightarrow \quad x=-86.3^{\circ} Y\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of the coefficient of volume expansion of a glass container to that of a viscous kept inside the container is $1: 4$. What dfraction of the inner volume of the container should the liquid occupy so that the volume of the remaining vacant space will be same at all temperatures?
explanation:
Given that $\dfrac{\gamma_{c}}{\gamma_{l}}=\dfrac{1}{4}$
If the vacant space remains constant then change in volumes of liquid and total container must be equal
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta V_{l}=\Delta V_{c} \Rightarrow x \gamma_{l} \Delta T=V_{0} \gamma_{c} \Delta T \\& \dfrac{x}{V_{0}}=\dfrac{\gamma_{c}}{\gamma_{l}}=\dfrac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$
( $x$ - initial volume of the liquid)
( $V_{0}$ - initial volume of the container)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: The variation of lengths of two metal rods \[A\] and \[B\] with change in temperature are shown in figure. The coefficient of linear expansion \(\alpha_{A}\) for the metal \[A\] will be: (Given \[{\alpha _B} = 9 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C\] )
subject: Physics

question: A solid cube is first floating in a liquid. The coefficient of linear expansion of cube is $\alpha$ and the coefficient of volume expansion of liquid is $\gamma$. On increasing the temperature of (liquid + cube) system, the cube will sink if
explanation: If $\gamma>3 \alpha$ then for a same temperature rise the liquid expands more than solid and the liquid density decreases. So the cube should submerge more to balance its weight
subject: Physics

question: A solid body of linear expansion coefficient $2 \times {10^{ - 5}}{\left( {^\circ C} \right)^{ - 1}}$ floats in a liquid with $20 \%$ of its volume out of the liquid at a temperature $20^\circ C$. The volumetric expansion coefficient of liquid is 3 times that of the solid body's volumetric expansion coefficient. If the temperature is increased to $70^\circ C$, the new dfraction of the body's volume out of the liquid will be approximately;
explanation:
Let $V_{S}$ is the volume submerged, $V_{T}$ is the total volume of the solid
$\dfrac{V_{S}}{V_{T}}=\dfrac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{L}}=0.8$
$\rho_{b}-$ density of block; $\rho_{L}-$ density of liquid
$\begin{aligned}\Delta T & =50^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\\dfrac{V_{S}^{\prime}}{V_{T}^{\prime}} & =\dfrac{\rho_{b}^{\prime}}{\rho_{L}^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{L}} \times \dfrac{1+\gamma_{L} \Delta T}{1+\gamma_{b} \Delta T} \\& =0.8 \times\left(\dfrac{1+18 \times 10^{-5} \times 50}{1+6 \times 10^{-5} \times 50}\right) \\& =0.8 \times\left(\dfrac{1+9 \times 10^{-3}}{1+3 \times 10^{-3}}\right) \\& =0.8 \times\left(1+6 \times 10^{-3}\right) \\\dfrac{V_{T}^{\prime}-V_{S}^{\prime}}{V_{T}^{\prime}} & =1-0.8-0.8 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \\= & 0.1952\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two rods of different metals having the same area of cross-section $A$ are placed between the two massive walls as shown in figure. The first rod has a length $l_{1}$, coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha_{1}$ and Young's modulus $Y_{1}$. The corresponding quantities for second rod are $l_{2}, \alpha_{2}$ and $Y_{2}$. The temperature of both the rods is now raised by $\Delta T$. The displacement of the junction is
explanation:
Let $\Delta l$ is the displacement of the junction towards right. If the rods are free to expand without any constrains then the two rods should expand by ${l_1}{\alpha _1}\Delta T\,\& \,{\rm{ }}{l_2}{\alpha _2}\Delta T$ as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956013/preptracker/questions/q_37859_explanation_1764956013030.png
fig (1)-Initial position of the combination before rising temperature.
fig (2)- Expected natural extensions in the rods after rising temperature.
fig (3)- Final position of the combination after rising temperature.
By comparing fig (2) & fig (3) we can conclude that both rods are in compression.
$\text { Strain }=\dfrac{l_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta T-\Delta l}{l_{1}}$
Force exerted by the first rod at the junction
$F_{1}=\dfrac{Y_{1} A\left(l_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta T-\Delta l\right)}{l_{1}}$
Strain in the second rod
$\text { Strain }=\dfrac{l_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta T+\Delta l}{l_{2}}$
Force exerted by the second is
$F_{2}=\dfrac{Y_{2} A\left(l_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta T+\Delta l\right)}{l_{2}}$
As the system is in equilibrium $F_{1}=F_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \Delta l=\dfrac{l_{1} l_{2} \Delta T\left(Y_{1} \alpha_{1}-Y_{2} \alpha_{2}\right)}{Y_{1} l_{2}+Y_{2} l_{1}}$
subject: Physics

question:
Two rods are joined between fixed supports as shown in the figure. Condition for no change in the lengths of individual rods with the increase of temperature will be
$\left(\alpha_{1}, \alpha_{2}=\right.$ linear expansion coefficients $A_{1}, A_{2}=$ Area of rods $Y_{1}, Y_{2}=$ Young modulus)
explanation:
$Y = \frac{{{\rm{ Stress }}}}{{{\rm{ Strain }}}} = \frac{{T/A}}{{\Delta l/l}}$
$T = \frac{{Y \cdot \Delta l}}{l}A = Y \cdot A\alpha \Delta T$
In both the rods tension will be same so
$\begin{aligned}& T_{1}=T_{2}, \text { Hence } Y_{1} A_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta T=Y_{2} A_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta T \\& \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\dfrac{Y_{2} \alpha_{2}}{Y_{1} \alpha_{1}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A steel scale measures the lenght of a copper rod as $80.0\,cm\,$, when both are at $20^\circ C$ (the calibration temperature for scale). What would be the scale read for the length of the rod when both are at $40^\circ C$ ? (Given ${\alpha _{{\rm{steel }}}} = 11 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\rm{per }}^\circ C$ and ${\alpha _{{\rm{copper }}}} = 11 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\rm{per }}^\circ C$ )
explanation:
The initial positions of the rod and scale are shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955959/preptracker/questions/q_37851_explanation_1764955959143.png
Given that $\alpha_{\mathrm{Cu}}>\alpha_{\text {steel }}$
Let $x^{\text {th }}$ centimeter mark on the scale coincides with the right end of the $cu$-rod.
After expansion $x\left( {1 + {\alpha _s}\Delta T} \right) = 80\,cm\left( {1 + {\alpha _{Cu}}\Delta T} \right)$
$x \approx 80.0096\,cm{\rm{ }}$
subject: Physics

question: As a result of a temperature rise of $32^\circ C$, a bar with a crack at its centre buckles upward as shown in the figure. If the fixed distance is ${L_0} = 3.77\,m$ and the coefficient of linear expansion is $25 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$, find $x$, the distance to which the centre rises.
explanation:
Let $\dfrac{L}{2}$ is the length of each part after rising temperature. From the right angle triangle
${\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2} = {x^2} + {\left( {\frac{{{L_0}}}{2}} \right)^2}$
$\frac{{{L^2}}}{4} = {x^2} + \frac{{L_0^2}}{4}$
${x^2} = \frac{1}{4}\left( {{L^2} - L_0^2} \right)$
$x = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {{L^2} - L_0^2} = \frac{{{L_0}}}{2}\sqrt {{{(1 + \alpha \Delta T)}^2} - 1} $
$x = \frac{{{L_0}}}{2}\sqrt {1 + 2\alpha \Delta T - 1} = \frac{{{L_0}}}{2}\sqrt {2\alpha \Delta T} $
$x = \frac{{3.77}}{2} \times \sqrt {2 \times 25 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 32} $
$x = 7.5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A wooden wheel of radius $R$  is made of two semicircular part (see figure). The two parts are held together by ring made of a metal strip cross sectional area $A$ and length $L$. $L$ is slightly less than $2 \pi R$. To fit the ring on the wheel, it is heated so that its temperature rises by $\Delta T$ and it just steps over the wheel. As it cools down to surrounding temperature, it presses the semicircular parts together. If the cofficient of linear eaxpansion of the metal is $\alpha$, and its Young's modulus is Y, the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part is:
explanation:
The Youngs modulus is given as
$Y=\dfrac{F / A}{\Delta L / L}$
Here it is given as
$Y = \frac{F}{{A2\pi \Delta R}} \times 2\pi R\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} [L = 2\pi R]$
$ \Rightarrow Y = \frac{{FR}}{{S \cdot \Delta R}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The coefficient of linear expansion is given as
$\alpha = \frac{{\Delta R}}{{R\Delta T}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta R}}{R} = \alpha .\Delta t \Rightarrow \frac{R}{{\Delta R}} = \frac{1}{{\alpha \cdot \Delta T}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From equations (1) and (2)
$Y=\dfrac{F}{S . \alpha \Delta T} \Rightarrow F=Y . S . \alpha \Delta T$
$\therefore$ The ring presses the wheel from both sides, Thus $F_{\text {net }}=2 F=2 Y S \alpha \Delta T$
subject: Physics

question:
As shown in the picture a cylinder with volumetric thermal expansion coefficient of ${\gamma _s} = 3 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$ is completely submerged and floating in a fluid at the temperature $50^\circ C$. The fluid has volumetric thermal expansion coefficient of ${\gamma _l} = 8 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$.
If we cool down the cylinder and fluid to $0^\circ C$ what percentage of the cylinder's height will be out of the fluid?
explanation:
As the cylinder is completely submerged in the liquid the densities of cylinder and liquid are same at $50^\circ C$. Let $\rho_{0}$ is the initial density. After decreasing the temperature
${\rho _s} = \frac{{{\rho _0}}}{{\left( {1 - {\gamma _s}50} \right)}}\,\& \,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {\rho _l} = \frac{{{\rho _0}}}{{\left( {1 - {\gamma _l}50} \right)}}$
At equilibrium of the cylinder
$\begin{gathered}F_{g}=F_{b} \\m g=\rho_{l} V^{\prime} g \\\rho_{s} V g=\rho_{l} V^{\prime} g \\\rho_{s} h A g=\rho_{l} h^{\prime} A g \\\dfrac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{l}}\end{gathered}$
$h$ - total height of the cylinder
$h^{\prime}$ - height of the cylinder present inside the liquid.
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{h^{\prime}}{h} & =\dfrac{\left(1-\gamma_{l} 50\right)}{\left(1-\gamma_{s} 50\right)}=\left(1-\gamma_{l} 50\right)\left(1-\gamma_{s} 50\right)^{-1} \\\dfrac{h^{\prime}}{h} & =\left(1-\gamma_{l} 50\right)\left(1+\gamma_{s} 50\right) \\\dfrac{h^{\prime}}{h} & =\left[1-\left(\gamma_{l}-\gamma_{s}\right) 50\right] \\\dfrac{h^{\prime}}{h} & =\left[1-7.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 50\right] \\\dfrac{h^{\prime}}{h} & =0.99615\end{aligned}$
The percentage of the cylinder present outside is $(1-0.99615) \times 100=0.38 \%$
subject: Physics

question: A one litre flask contains certain quantity of mercury. If the volume of air inside the flask remains the same at all temperatures then the volume of mercury in the flask is (volume expansion coefficient of mercury is 20 times that of flask)
explanation:
Let $V_{c_{o}}$ and $V_{l_{o}}$ are the initial volumes of container and liquid $V_{c_{o}}-V_{l_{o}}=$ constant (Given)
$V_{c}-V_{l}=V_{c_{0}}\left(1+\gamma_{c} \Delta T\right)-V_{l_{0}}\left(1+\gamma_{l} \Delta T\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& V_{c}-V_{l}=V_{c_{0}}-V_{l_{0}}+V_{c_{0}} \gamma_{c} \Delta T-V_{l_{0}} \gamma_{l} \Delta T \\& V_{c_{0}} \gamma_{c}=V_{l_{0}} \gamma_{l}\end{aligned}$
Given $V_{c_{0}}=1000 c c, \gamma_{l}=20 \gamma_{c}$
$\Rightarrow V_{l_{0}}=50 c c$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: Two steel rods and an aluminium rod of length $l_{0}$ and equal cross section are joined rigidly at their eand as shown in the figure below. All the rods are in a state of zero tension at $0^\circ C$. Find the length of the system when the temperature is raised to $\Delta T$. Coefficient of linear expansion of aluminium and steel are $\alpha_{a}$ and $\alpha_{s}$ respectively. Young's modulus of aluminium and steel are $Y_{a}$ and $Y_{s}$ respectively $\left(\alpha_{s}>\alpha_{a}\right)$.
explanation:
If the three rods are free to expand then their extensions are
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta l_{s}=l_{0} \alpha_{s} \Delta T \\& \Delta l_{a}=l_{0} \alpha_{a} \Delta T\end{aligned}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955935/preptracker/questions/q_37830_explanation_1764955934692.png
fig (1)- Initial position of the combination before rising temperature.
fig (2)- Expected natural extensions in the rods after rising temperature.
fig (3)- Final position of the combination after rising temperature.
As the three rods are rigidly fixed they will have a common extension $\Delta l$. As $\alpha_{s}>\alpha_{a}$ the steel rods try to exert force in forward direction on the aluminium rod.
As the system is in equilibrium
${F_a} = 2{F_s}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The strain in steel rods
${\rm{strain }} = \frac{{\left( {{l_0}{\alpha _s}\Delta T - \Delta l} \right)}}{{{l_0}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {F_s} = \frac{{{Y_s}A\left( {{l_0}{\alpha _s}\Delta T - \Delta l} \right)}}{{{l_0}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Similarly
${F_a} = \frac{{{Y_a}A\left( {\Delta l - {l_0}{\alpha _a}\Delta T} \right)}}{{{l_0}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From the three eq's we get
$\Delta l=\dfrac{l_{0} \Delta T\left(2 Y_{s} \alpha_{s}+Y_{a} \alpha_{a}\right)}{\left[Y_{a}+2 Y_{s}\right]}$
subject: Physics

question: A resistance $R$ carries a current $I$. The rate of heat loss to the surroundings is $\lambda\left(T-T_{0}\right)$ where $\lambda$ is a constant, $T$ is the temperature of the resistance, and $T_{0}$ is the temperature of the atmosphere. If the coefficient of linear expansion is $\alpha$, the strain in the resistance is :
explanation:
The power loss is given by
$\begin{aligned}& I^{2} R=\lambda\left(T-T_{0}\right) ; \text { Strain }=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}=\alpha\left(T-T_{0}\right) \\& \dfrac{I^{2} R}{\left(\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right)}=\dfrac{\lambda\left(T-T_{0}\right)}{\alpha\left(T-T_{0}\right)} \\& \dfrac{I^{2} R}{\left(\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right)}=\dfrac{\lambda}{\alpha} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}=\dfrac{\alpha I^{2} R}{\lambda}\end{aligned}$

options.text: Proportional to the length of the resistance wire

subject: Physics

question: Two scales having different coefficients of linear expansion are caliberated at room temperature. Now the temperature of the scales are increased by $20^\circ C$  then $15\,cm$ mark of first scale coincides with $12\,cm$ mark of second scale. Find the mark on first scale that coincides with $10\,cm$ mark on second scale.
explanation:
After expansion as $15\,cm\,\,\& \,{\rm{ }}12\,cm$ coincide then
$15\,cm\left( {1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta T} \right) = 12\,cm\left( {1 + {\alpha _2}\Delta T} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Let $x$ is the mark on the first scale that coincides with $10\,cm$ mark on second scale
$x\left( {1 + {\alpha _1}\Delta T} \right) = 10\,cm\left( {1 + {\alpha _2}\Delta T} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
${\rm{Eq(1)}}\,{\rm{/}}\,{\rm{Eq(2)}}$
$\frac{{15}}{x} = \frac{{12}}{{10}} \Rightarrow x = 12.5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A copper and a tungsten plate having a thickness $d$ each are riveted together so that at $0^\circ C$ they form a flat bimetallic plate. Find the average radius of the curvature of this plate at temperature T. The coefficient of linear expansion for Copper and tungsten are $\alpha_{C u}$ and $\alpha_{t}$.
explanation:
The length of copper plate at a temperature $T$ is $l_{C}=l_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{C} T\right)$, where $l_{0}$ is the length of copper plate at $0^\circ C$. The length of the tungsten plate is $l_{t}=l_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{t} T\right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955927/preptracker/questions/q_37818_explanation_1764955927014.png
Length of copper plate $l_{C}=\phi(R+\delta / 2)$
Consequently,
$\phi (R + \delta /2) = {l_0}\left( {1 + {\alpha _C}T} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Similarly for tungsten plate
$\phi (R - \delta /2) = {l_0}\left( {1 + {\alpha _t}T} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
To eliminate the unknown quantities, and $l_{0}$ we divide the equation (1) by (2)
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{(R+\delta / 2)}{(R-\delta / 2)}=\dfrac{\left(1+\alpha_{C} T\right)}{\left(1+\alpha_{t} T\right)} \\& \Rightarrow R=\delta \dfrac{\left[2+\left(\alpha_{c}+\alpha_{t}\right) T\right]}{\left[2\left(\alpha_{c}-\alpha_{t}\right) T\right]}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A planar frame made of thin uniform rods, the length of section $A B$ and $E F$ is $l_{1}$ and its thermal linear coefficient of expansion is $\alpha_{1}$. The length of section $C D$ is $l_{2}$ and its thermal linear coefficient of expansion is $\alpha_{2} . C B$ and $D E$ are of same length having thermal linear coefficient of expansion $\alpha_{2}$. Points $A, B, E$ and $F$ reside on same line, that is, sections $A B$ and $E F$ overlap. Then the ratio of $\dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}$, for which the distance between end $A$ and $F$ remains the same at all temperatures, is:
explanation:
For distance between $A$ and $F$ to remain constant, extension in $C D=$ extension in $A B+$ extension in $E F$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \Delta l_{2}=2 \Delta l_{1} \Rightarrow l_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta \theta=2 l_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta \theta \\& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{\alpha_{2}}{2 \alpha_{1}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two resistances of equal magnitude $R$ and having temperature coefficient $\alpha$ respectively are connected in parallel. The temperature coefficient of the parallel combination is, approximately:
explanation:
As the resistors are connected in parallel
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{R_{e q}}=\dfrac{1}{R_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{R_{2}} \\& \dfrac{1}{R_{e q}}=\dfrac{1}{R(1+\alpha \Delta T)}+\dfrac{1}{R(1+\alpha \Delta T)} \\& \quad \dfrac{1}{R_{e q}}=\dfrac{1}{R}[2-(2 \alpha) \Delta T] \\& R_{e q}=\dfrac{R}{[2-(2 \alpha) \Delta T]} \\& R_{e q}=R[2-(2 \alpha) \Delta T]^{-1}=R[2+(2 \alpha) \Delta T] \\& R_{e q}=2 R[1+\alpha \Delta T] \\& =2 R\left[1+\alpha_{e q} \Delta T\right] \\& \Rightarrow \alpha_{e q}=\alpha\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Equivalent Temperature Coefficient of Linear Expansion

question: A metal rod of length $L$, cross - sectional area $A$, Young's modulus $Y$ and coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is heated to $t^\circ C$. The work that can be performed by the rod when heated is
explanation:
Increase in length if the rod is allowed to expand freely, $\Delta L = L\alpha t$
Work that can be performed by the rod,
$\Delta W$= change in potential energy of the rod
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times ({\rm{strain}}\,{\rm{energy}}\,{\rm{per}}\,{\rm{unit}}\,{\rm{volume)}}\, \times \,{\rm{volume}}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times Y \times {\left( {{\rm{strain}}} \right)^2} \times AL$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times Y \times {\left( {\frac{{\Delta L}}{L}} \right)^2} \times AL = \frac{1}{2} \times Y \times {\left( {\alpha t} \right)^2} \times AL$
$ = \frac{{YA{\alpha ^2}L{t^2}}}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A steel scale measures the length of a copper wire as $80.0\,cm$, when both are at $20^\circ C$ (the calibration temperature for scale). What would be the scale read for the length of the wire when both are at $40^\circ C$ ? (Given ${\alpha _{{\rm{steel }}}} = 11 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\mathop{\rm per}\nolimits} \,^\circ C$ and ${\alpha _{{\rm{copper }}}} = 17 \times {10^{ - 6}}{\mathop{\rm per}\nolimits} \,^\circ C$)
explanation:
With temperature rise (same $25^\circ C$ for both), both steel scale and copper wire expand. Hence length of copper wire w.r.to steel scale or apparent length of copper wire will increase after rise in temperature
${L_{app{\rm{ }}}} = L_{Cu}^\prime - L_{steel{\rm{ }}}^\prime $
$ = \left[ {{L_0}\left( {1 + {\alpha _{Cu}}\Delta \theta } \right) - {L_0}\left( {1 + {\alpha _s}\Delta \theta } \right)} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {L_{app{\rm{ }}}} = {L_0}\left( {{\alpha _{Cu}} - {\alpha _s}} \right)\Delta \theta $
$ = 80\left( {17 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} - 11 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right) \times 20$
$ = 80.0096\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A metal rod of length ' $L$ ' and cross - sectional area ' $A$ ' is heated through $T^\circ C$. What is the force required to prevent the expansion of the rod length wise?
explanation:
Increase in length of the rod (if allowed to expand) is given as
$\Delta L = \alpha LT\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\therefore {\rm{Newlength }}{L^\prime } = L[1 + \alpha T]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Considering the force (F) needed to return the rod to its original length,
Young's modulus $Y = \frac{{(F/A)}}{{\left( {\Delta L/{L^\prime }} \right)}} = \frac{{F{L^\prime }}}{{A\Delta L}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Using relation (2) and substituting in the relation (3), we get
$Y = \frac{F}{A} \cdot \frac{{L(1 + \alpha T)}}{{\Delta L}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}\Delta L = \frac{{FL(1 + \alpha T)}}{{AY}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
From eq. (1) and (4), we get
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{F L(1+\alpha T)}{A Y}=\alpha L T \\& \Rightarrow F=\dfrac{Y A \alpha T}{(1+\alpha T)}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A copper rod of $88\,cm$ and an aluminium rod of unknown length have their increase in length independent of increase in temperature. The length of aluminium rod is :$\left(\alpha_{c u}=1.7 \times 10^{-5} K^{-1}\right.$ and $\left.\alpha_{A l}=2.2 \times 10^{-5} K^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
Given that $\Delta L_{C u}=\Delta L_{A l}$
${\alpha _{Cu}}{L_{Cu}} = {\alpha _{Al}}{L_{Al}}$
$1.7 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times 88\,cm = 2.2 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times {L_{Al}}$
${L_{Al}} = \frac{{1.7 \times 88}}{{2.2}} = 68\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Coefficient of linear expansion of brass and steel rods are $\alpha_{1}$ and $\alpha_{2}$. Lengths of brass and steel rods are $\ell_{1}$ and $\ell_{2}$ respectively. If $\left(\ell_{2}-\ell_{1}\right)$ is maintained same at all temperatures, which one of the following relations holds good?
explanation:
Given that
$\begin{aligned}& \ell_{2}-\ell_{1}=\text { constant } \\& \Rightarrow \quad \ell_{2}-\ell_{1}=\ell_{2}^{1}-\ell_{1}^{1} \\& \ell_{2}^{1}-\ell_{2}=\ell_{1}^{1}-\ell_{1}\end{aligned}$
Change in length for both rods should be same
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta \ell_{1}=\Delta \ell_{2} \\& \ell_{1} \alpha_{1} \Delta T=\ell_{2} \alpha_{2} \Delta T \\& \ell_{1} \alpha_{1}=\ell_{2} \alpha_{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: A solid and hollow sphere of same diameter and same material when heated through the same temperature will expand by the same amount


Reason: The change in volume is independent of the original mass but depends on original volume.
explanation: The change in volume depends on original volume so, when heated through the same temperature, both will expand by the same amount. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question:
Assertion: Water expands both when heated or cooled from $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Reason: Density of water is minimum at $4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
explanation: At $4^\circ C$ the volume of water is minimum. When it is cooled below $4^\circ C$ or heated above $4^\circ C$, then its volume increases. As volume at $4^\circ C$ is minimum, thus its density will be maximum. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
The density of water remains constant as it is cooled from room temperature until its temperature reaches $4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Below $4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the density increases.
explanation: Density of water increases from $0^\circ C$ and it reaches maximum at $4^\circ C$and then it decreases. Option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

A change in the temperature of a body causes change in its dimensions
The dimensions of a body decreases due to the increase in its temperature.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question:
Copper expands more than glass for same rise in temperature.
Copper is far better conductor of heat than glass.
explanation: Metals generally have higher coefficient of linear expansion. So, copper expands five times more than glass.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.
