question: Two identical similar rods of metal are welded end to end as shown in figure. 20 $cal$ of heat flows through it in 4 minutes. If the rods are welded as shown in the figure the same amount of heat will flow through the rods in
explanation:
$\mathrm{As}, \dfrac{Q}{t}=\dfrac{k A \Delta \theta}{l}=\dfrac{\Delta \theta}{(l / k A)}=\dfrac{\Delta \theta}{R}$
($R$- Thermal resistance)
Given that $Q$ and $\Delta \theta$ are same for two combinations.
$\begin{aligned}& t \propto R \\& \dfrac{t_{p}}{t_{s}}=\dfrac{R_{p}}{R_{s}}=\dfrac{R / 2}{2 R}=\dfrac{1}{4} \\& t_{p}=\dfrac{t_{s}}{4}=\dfrac{4}{4}=1 \mathrm{~min} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Equivalent Thermal Resistance and Equivalent Temperature Coefficient of Linear Expansion

question: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerine is $49 \times 10^{-5} / K$. What is the fractional change in its density for a $30^\circ C$ rise in temperature?
explanation:
Given, coefficient of volume expension
$(\gamma)=49 \times 10^{-5} / K$
Rise in temperature $(\Delta T) = 30^\circ C$
Let initial volume of glycerine be $V_{0}$.
Volume of glycerine when temperature is increased by $30^\circ C$
$V = {V_0}[1 + \gamma \Delta T]$
$ = {V_0}\left[ {1 + 49 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 30} \right]$
$ = {V_0}[1 + 0.01470] = 1.0147{V_0}$
$\frac{{{V_0}}}{V} = \frac{1}{{1.0147}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\frac{\rho }{{{\rho _0}}} = \frac{{m/V}}{{m/{V_0}}} = \frac{{{V_0}}}{V} = \frac{1}{{1.0147}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Fractional change in density
$=\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_{0}}=\dfrac{\rho-\rho_{0}}{\rho_{0}}=\dfrac{\rho}{\rho_{0}}-1$
Substituting value from Eq. (2), we get
Fractional change in density $=\dfrac{1}{1.0147}-1$
$=-1.45 \times 10^{-2}$
Negative sign shows that the density of glycerine decrease with rise in temperature
subject: Physics

question: A hole is drilled in a copper sheet. The diameter of the hole is $4.24\,cm$ at $27^\circ C$. What is the change in the diameter of the hole when the sheet is heated to $227^\circ C$ ? Coefficient of linear expansion of copper is $1.70 \times {10^{ - 5}}/\,^\circ C$.
explanation:
Given, diameter of the hole
$\left( {{d_1}} \right) = 4.24\,cm$
Initial temperature
$T_{1}=27+273=300 K$
Final temperature ${T_2} = 227 + 273 = 500\,K$
Coefficient of linear expansion
$(\alpha ) = 1.70 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$
Coefficient of areal expansion $(\beta)=2 \alpha$
$ = 3.40 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$
Initial area of hole at $27^{\circ} C\left(A_{1}\right)=\pi r^{2}=\dfrac{\pi d_{1}^{2}}{4}$
$\frac{\pi }{4}{(4.24)^2} = 4.494\pi c{m^2}$
Area of hole at $227^{\circ} C\left(A_{2}\right)=A_{1}(1+\beta . \Delta t)$
$\begin{aligned}& =4.494 \pi\left[1+3.40 \times 10^{-5} \times(227-27)\right] \\& =4.495 \pi \times 1.0068\end{aligned}$
If diameter of hole becomes ${d_2}\,{\rm{at}}\,227^\circ C$, then
${A_2} = \frac{{\pi d_2^2}}{4}4.525\pi = \frac{{\pi d_2^2}}{4}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}{d_2} = 4.2544\,cm$
Change in diameter $(\Delta d) = {d_2} - {d_1}$
$ = 1.44 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The radius of a metal sphere at room temperature $T$ is $R$, and the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha$. The sphere is heated a little by a temperature $\Delta T$ so that its new temperature is $T+\Delta T$. The increase in the volume of the sphere is approximiately
explanation:
Let $V$ be the volume sphere of radius $R$ at temperature $T$ then $V=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$.
Increase in volume of sphere with rise in temperature $\Delta T$ is
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta V=\gamma V \Delta T=3 \alpha \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \Delta T \\& \Delta V=4 \pi R^{3} \alpha \Delta T\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A bimetallic strip is made of aluminium and steel $\left(\alpha_{A l}>\alpha_{\text {steel }}\right)$. On heating, the strip will
explanation: Since, $\alpha_{A l}>\alpha_{\text {steel }}$, so in bimetallic strip on heating, aluminium strip will expand more than steel strip. Due to it, aluminium strip will bend more on convex side and steel strip on concave side.

options.text: Will bend with aluminium on concave side
Will bend with steel on concave side

subject: Physics

question: As the temperature is increased, the time period of a pendulum.
explanation: With increase in temperature, the effective length $(l)$ of simple pendulum increases even through its centre of mass still remains at the centre of the bob. Time period, $T=2 \pi \sqrt{l / g}$ or $T \propto \sqrt{l}$. $T$ increases as temperature increases

options.text: Increase as the effective length increases even though its centre of mass still remains at the centre of the bob.
Decreases as its effective length increases even through its centre of mass still remains at the centre of the bob.
Increases as its effective length increases due to shifting of centre of mass below below centre of the bob
Decreases as its effective length increases remains same but centre of the mass shifts above the centre of the bob.

subject: Physics

question: An aluminium sphere is dipped into water. Which of the following is true?
explanation:
Let $V$ be the volume of the sphere and $\rho$ be the density of water. Buoyancy ($F$) on the sphere due to water is
$F=V \rho g$
Since, $\rho_{0{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} < \rho_{4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$, so, $F_{0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} < F_{4{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}$.

options.text: Buoyancy will be less in water at $0^\circ C$ than that is water at $4^\circ C$
Buoyancy will be more in water at $0^\circ C$ than that is water at $4^\circ C$
Buoyancy in water at $0^\circ C$will be same as that in water at $4^\circ C$
Buoyancy may be more or less in water at $4^\circ C$ depending on the radius of the sphere

subject: Physics

question: The variation of density of water with temperature is represented by the
explanation: Density of water is maximum at $4^\circ C$ and is less on either side of this temperature.
subject: Physics

question: If $d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$ are the densities of a liquid at ${t_1}^\circ C$ and ${t_2}^\circ C,$ then $\dfrac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}$ is $(\gamma=$ coefficient of real expansion)
explanation:
Let $d$ is the density of the liquid at $0^\circ C$ then
${d_1} = \frac{d}{{1 + \gamma {t_1}}}\,\& \,{d_2} = \frac{d}{{1 + \gamma {t_2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{d_1}}}{{{d_2}}} = \frac{{1 + \gamma {t_2}}}{{1 + \gamma {t_1}}}$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of apparent expansion of mercury in a glass vessel is $153 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$  and in a steel vessel is $144 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$. If $\alpha$ for steel is $12 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$, then $\alpha$ that of glass is :
explanation:
$\gamma_{a g}=\gamma_{r}-3 \alpha_{g}\left(\gamma_{a g}-\right.$ apparent expansion of glass)
$\gamma_{a s}=\gamma_{r}-3 \alpha_{s} \quad\left(\gamma_{a s}-\right.$ apparent expansion of steel)
From the above two eq's
${\gamma _{ag}} - {\gamma _{as}} = 3\left( {{\alpha _s} - {\alpha _g}} \right) \Rightarrow {\alpha _g} = 9 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: The figure shows a glass cylinder (linear coefficient of expansion is \(\alpha\) ) completely filled with a liquid of volume expansion co-efficient \(\gamma\). On heating length of the liquid column does not change. Choose the correct relation between \(\gamma\) and \(\alpha\)
explanation:
Let $A_{0}$ is the base area of the container initially. After increasing the temperature its base area is becomes $A$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954669/preptracker/questions/q_37213_explanation_1764954669133.png
The area of the base after increasing temperature is
$A = {A_0}(1 + 2\alpha \Delta T)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The volume of the liquid after increasing temperature is
$V = {V_0}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)$
$A{h_0} = {A_0}{h_0}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)$
$A = {A_0}(1 + \gamma \Delta T)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's (1) & (2)
$\gamma=2 \alpha$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: A glass flask of volume one litre at $0^\circ C$ is filled, level full of mercury at this temperature. The flask and mercury are now heated to $100^\circ C$. How much mercury will spill out, if coefficient of volume expansion of mercury is $1.82 \times {10^{ - 4}}/^\circ C$ and linear expansion of glass is $0.1 \times {10^{ - 4}}/^\circ C$ respectively?
explanation:
Due to volume expansion of both liquid and vessel, the change in volume of liquid relative to container is given by
$\Delta V=V_{0}\left[\gamma_{L}-\gamma_{g}\right] \Delta T$
Given ${V_0} = 1000\,cc,{\alpha _g} = 0.1 \times {10^{ - 4}}/^\circ C$
$\therefore {\gamma _g} = 3{\alpha _g} = 3 \times 0.1 \times {10^{ - 4}}/^\circ C = 0.3 \times {10^{ - 4}}/^\circ C$
$\therefore \quad \Delta V=1000\left[1.82 \times 10^{-4}-0.3 \times 10^{-4}\right] \times 100$
$=15.2 c c$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: An external pressure $P$ is applied on a cube at $0^\circ C$ so that it is equally compressed from all sides. $K$ is the bulk modulus of the material of the cube and $\alpha$ is its coefficient of linear expansion. Suppose we want to bring the cube to its original size of heating. The temperature should be raised by:
explanation:
As we know, Bulk modulus
$K = \frac{{\Delta P}}{{\left( {\frac{{ - \Delta V}}{V}} \right)}} \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = \frac{P}{K}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$\frac{{\Delta V}}{V} = \gamma \Delta t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$\therefore \frac{P}{K} = \gamma \Delta t$
The temperature that should be rised to make same strain is $\Delta t=\dfrac{P}{\gamma K}=\dfrac{P}{3 \alpha K}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A circular disc made of iron is rotated about its axis at a constant velocity $\omega$. Calculate the percentage change in the linear speed of a particle of the rim as the disc is slowly heated from $20^\circ C$ to $50^\circ C$ keeping the angular velocity constant. (Coefficient of linear expansion of iron $\left. { = 1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}/^\circ C} \right)$.
explanation:
The linear velocity
$v=r \omega$
$\dfrac{d v}{v}=\dfrac{d r}{r} \quad(\omega=$ constant $)$
$\dfrac{d v}{v}=\dfrac{r \alpha d T}{r}=\alpha d T$
$\dfrac{d v}{v} \times 100=\alpha d T \times 100=3.6 \times 10^{-2} \%$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of linear expansion of crystal in one direction is $\alpha_{1}$ and that in every direction perpendicular to it is $\alpha_{2}$. The coefficient of cubical expansion is
explanation:
$V=V_{0}(1+\gamma \Delta \theta)$
$\begin{aligned}L^{3} & =L_{0}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta \theta\right) L_{0}^{2}\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta \theta\right)^{2} \\& =L_{0}^{3}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta \theta\right)\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta \theta\right)^{2}\end{aligned}$
Since $L_{0}^{3}=V_{0}$ and $L^{3}=V$
$\begin{aligned}\text { Hence } & 1+\gamma \Delta \theta=\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta \theta\right)\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta \theta\right)^{2} \\\cong & \left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta \theta\right)\left(1+2 \alpha_{2} \Delta \theta\right) \\\cong & \left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta \theta+2 \alpha_{2} \Delta \theta\right)\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \gamma=\alpha_{1}+2 \alpha_{2}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A pendulum clock loses $12\,s$ a day if the temperature is $40^\circ C$ and gains $4\,s$ a day if the temperature is $20^\circ C$. The temperature at which the clock will show correct time, and the coefficient of linear expansion $(\alpha)$ of the metal of the pendulum shaft are respectively :
explanation:
According to given conditions
$86412 = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{\ell _{40}}}}{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$86396 = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{\ell _{20}}}}{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
$86400 = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{\ell }{g}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
From equations (1) and (3)
$12 = \frac{{2\pi }}{{\sqrt g }}\left[ {\sqrt {{\ell _{40}}} - \sqrt \ell } \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
From equations (2) & (3)
and $\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} 4 = \frac{{2\pi }}{{\sqrt g }}\left[ {\sqrt \ell - \sqrt {{\ell _{20}}} } \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 5 \right)$
On dividing (4) and (5)
$\begin{aligned}& 3=\dfrac{\left[\sqrt{l_{40}}-\sqrt{l}\right]}{\sqrt{l}-\sqrt{l_{20}}} \\& =\dfrac{[\sqrt{l(1+\alpha \Delta \theta)}-\sqrt{l}]}{\sqrt{l}-\sqrt{l+\alpha \Delta \theta}} \\& =\dfrac{\sqrt{1+\alpha(40-\theta)}-1}{1-\sqrt{1+\alpha(\theta-20)}} \\& \Rightarrow 3=\dfrac{40-\theta}{\theta-20} \text { (by Binomial theorem) } \\& \Rightarrow \theta=25^{\circ}\end{aligned}$
From eq's (1) and (3)
$\frac{{86412}}{{86400}} = \sqrt {1 + \alpha 15} \Rightarrow \alpha = 1.85 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A steel meter scale is to be ruled so that millimeter intervals are accurate within about $5 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,mm$ at a certain temperature. The maximum temperature variation allowable during the ruling is (Coefficient of linear expansion of steel $ = 10 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,{K^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
As we know that coefficient of linear expansion is
$\alpha = \frac{{\Delta L}}{{{L_0}\Delta \theta }} \Rightarrow \Delta \theta = \frac{{\Delta L}}{{\alpha {L_0}}} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}}{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 1}} = 5^\circ C.$
subject: Physics

question: Three rods of equal length $l$ are joined to form an equilateral triangle $P Q R$. $O$  is the mid point of $P Q$. Distance $O R$ remains same for small change in temperature. Coefficient of linear expansion for $P R$ ad $R Q$ is same, i.e., $\alpha_{2}$ but that for $P Q$ is $\alpha_{1}$. Then
explanation:
From the right angle triangle $PRO$
$(O R)^{2}=(P R)^{2}-(P O)^{2}=l^{2}-\left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^{2}$
After expansion
$\begin{aligned}& (\text { OR })^{2}=\left[l\left(1+\alpha_{2} t\right)\right]^{2}-\left[\dfrac{1}{2} l\left(1+\alpha_{1} t\right)\right]^{2} \\& l^{2}-\dfrac{l^{2}}{4}=l^{2}\left(1+\alpha_{2}^{2} t^{2}+2 \alpha_{2} t\right)-\dfrac{l^{2}}{4}\left(1+\alpha_{1}^{2} t^{2}+2 \alpha_{1} t\right)\end{aligned}$
Neglecting $\alpha_{2}^{2} t^{2}$ and $\alpha_{1}^{2} t^{2}$
$0=l^{2}\left(2 \alpha_{2} t\right)-\dfrac{l^{2}}{4}\left(2 \alpha_{1} t\right) \Rightarrow 2 \alpha_{2}=\dfrac{2 \alpha_{1}}{4} \Rightarrow \alpha_{1}=4 \alpha_{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A rail track made of steel having length $10\,cm$ is clamped on a railway line at its two ends (figure). On a summer day due to rise in temperature by $20^\circ C$. It is deformed as shown in figure. Find $x$ (displacement of the centre) if ${\alpha _{{\rm{steel }}}} = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$
explanation:
Consider the diagram
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954573/preptracker/questions/q_37182_explanation_1764954572618.png
Applying Pythagorean theorem in right angle triangle in figure
${\left( {\frac{{L + \Delta L}}{2}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)^2} + {x^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,x = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {\left( {{L^2} + \Delta {L^2} + 2L\Delta L} \right) - {L^2}} $
$ = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {\left( {\Delta {L^2} + 2L\Delta L} \right)} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
If $\Delta L$ is very small, then, neglecting $\left(\Delta L^{2}\right)$, we get
$x = \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt {2L\Delta L} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
But $\quad \Delta L=L \alpha \Delta t$
Substituting value of $\Delta L$ in Eq. (1) from Eq. (2)
$x = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {2L \times L\alpha \Delta t} = \frac{1}{2}L\sqrt {2\alpha \Delta t} $
$ = \frac{{10}}{2} \times \sqrt {2 \times 1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}} \times 20} $
$ = 5 \times \sqrt {4 \times 1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}} = 20\sqrt {30} \times {10^{ - 3}}\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: If two rods of length $L$ and $2\,L$ having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ and $2 \alpha$ respectively are connected so that total length becomes $3\,L$, the average coefficient of linear expansion of the composition rod equals:
explanation:
Effective linear coefficient
$\begin{gathered}\Delta L=\Delta L_{1}+\Delta L_{2} \\L \alpha \Delta T=\alpha_{1} L_{1} \Delta T+\alpha_{2} L_{2} \Delta T \\\alpha=\dfrac{L_{1} \alpha_{1}+L_{2} \alpha_{2}}{L_{1}+L_{2}}=\dfrac{L(\alpha)+2 L(2 \alpha)}{3 L}\end{gathered}$
$ = \frac{{5\alpha }}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: A uniform metal rod is used as a bar pendulum. If the room temperature rises by $10^\circ C,$ and the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal of the rod is $2 \times 10^{-6}$ per $^\circ C$, the period of the pendulum will have percentage increase of
explanation:
Fractional change in period
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{\Delta T}{T} & =\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \\\Rightarrow & \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \Delta \theta=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 10=10^{-5}\end{aligned}$
$\%$ change $=\dfrac{\Delta T}{T} \times 100=10^{-5} \times 100=10^{-3} \%$
subject: Physics

question: A clock with a metal pendulum beating seconds keeps correct time at $0^\circ C$. If it loses $12.5\,s$ a day at $25^\circ C$, the coefficient of linear expansion of metal of pendulum is
explanation:
Loss of time due to heating a pendulum is given as
$\Delta T = \frac{1}{2}\alpha \Delta \theta T$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} 12.5 = \frac{1}{2} \times \alpha \times (25 - 0)\,^\circ C \times 86400$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \alpha = \frac{1}{{86400}}^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A clock with an iron pendulum keeps correct time at $15^\circ C$. What will be the error in second per day, if the room temperature is $20^\circ C$? (The coefficient of linear expansion of iron is $0.000012^{\circ} C^{-1}$.)
explanation:
Here, $\Delta T = 20 - 15 = 5^\circ C$
$\alpha = 0.000012^\circ {C^{ - 1}} = 12 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
Time lost per day $ = \frac{1}{2}\alpha (\Delta T) \times 86400\,s$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 5 \times 86400\,s = 2.560\,s$
subject: Physics

question: Two rods, one of aluminium and the other made of steel, having initial length $l_{1}$ and $l_{2}$ are connected together to form a single rod of length $l_{1}+l_{2}$. The coefficients of linear expansion for aluminium and steel are $\alpha_{a}$ and $\alpha_{s}$ respectively. If the length of each rod increase by the same amount when their temperature is raised by $t^\circ C$, then find the ratio $\dfrac{l_{1}}{\left(l_{1}+l_{2}\right)}$
explanation:
Given $\Delta l_{1}=\Delta l_{2}$ or $l_{1} \alpha_{a} t=l_{2} \alpha_{s} t$
$\therefore \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{\alpha_{s}}{\alpha_{a}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{1}+l_{2}}=\dfrac{\alpha_{s}}{\alpha_{a}+\alpha_{s}}$
subject: Physics

question: The co-efficient of thermal expansion of a rod is temperature dependent and is given by the formula $\alpha=a T$, where $a$ is a positive constant and $T$ in $^\circ C$. If the length of the rod is $l$ at temperature $0^\circ C$, then the temperature at which the length will be $2 l$ is:
explanation:
As $d l=\alpha l d T$
$\therefore \quad \int_{l}^{2 l} \dfrac{d l}{l}=a \int_{0}^{T} T d T(\alpha=a T)$
$\ln 2=a \dfrac{T^{2}}{2} \quad \therefore \quad T=\left[\dfrac{\ln 4}{a}\right]^{1 / 2}$
subject: Physics

question: The design of some physical instrument requires that there be a constant difference in length of $10\,cm$ between an iron rod and a copper cylinder laid side by side at all temperatures. The lengths are $\left(\alpha_{F e}=11 \times 10^{-6 \circ} c^{-1}, \alpha_{c u}=17 \times 10^{-60} c^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
Let $l_{0}$ be the length of the copper at $0^\circ C$. The length of iron at $0^\circ C$ is ${l_0} + 10^\circ cm$. After a change of temperature $\Delta T$ the difference in their length must remain same
$\left( {{l_0} + 10\,cm} \right)\left( {1 + {\alpha _{Fe}}\Delta T} \right) - {l_0}\left( {1 + {\alpha _{cu}}\Delta T} \right) = 10\,cm$
Given that $\alpha_{c u}=17 \times 10^{-6 \circ} c^{-1}$
${\alpha _{Fe}} = 11 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}} \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {l_0} = 18.3\,cm$
so option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
What should be the lengths of a steel and copper rod at $0^\circ C$ so that the length of the steel rod is $5\,cm$ longer than the copper rod at any temperature
$\alpha \,\left( {Steel} \right){\rm{ }} = 1.1 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$ Copper $ = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,^\circ C$
explanation:
Here, $\alpha \,\left( {steel} \right){\rm{ }} = 1.1 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
$\alpha \left( {copper} \right) = 1.7 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
As the difference in the lengths remain constant
$l_{0}(s) \alpha(s)=l_{0}(c) \alpha(c)$
As, $\quad \dfrac{l_{0}(s)}{l_{0}(c)}=\dfrac{\alpha(c)}{\alpha(s)}=\dfrac{1.7 \times 10^{-5}}{1.1 \times 10^{-5}}=1.545$
$\therefore l_{0}(s)=1.545 l_{0}(c)$
Also $l_{0}(s)-l_{0}(c)=5$
$0.545{l_0}(c) = 5$
$ \Rightarrow {l_0}(c) = \frac{5}{{0.545}} = 9.17\,cm$
and ${l_0}(s) = 1.545 \times 9.17\,cm = 14.17\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A $Cu$ rod and a steel rod maintain a difference in their lengths constant at all temperatures. If their coefficients of expansion are $1.6 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$ and  $1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{K^{ - 1}}.$ If the length of steel $\operatorname{rod}$ is $10\,cm$ then the length of the $Cu$ rod is
explanation:
Let $l_{1}$ be the length of $Cu$ and $l_{2}$ that of steel
$\left(l_{2}^{1}-l_{1}^{1}\right)=\left(l_{2}-l_{1}\right)+\left(l_{2} \alpha_{2}-l_{1} \alpha_{1}\right) \Delta T$
$\Rightarrow l_{2} \alpha_{2}-l_{1} \alpha_{1}=0 \quad\left(l_{2}^{1}-l_{1}^{1}=l_{2}-l_{1}\right)$
that is, $\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}=\dfrac{\alpha_{1}}{\alpha_{2}}$
$\dfrac{10}{l_{1}}=\dfrac{1.6}{1.2}$
${\rm{or}}$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{l_1} = 7.5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of apparent expansion of a liquid when determined using two different vessels $A$ and $B$ are $\gamma_{1}$ and $\gamma_{2}$ respectively. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the vessel $A$ is $\alpha$, the coefficient of linear expansion of the vessel $B$ is
explanation:
The relation between apparent and real expansion of a liquid is
$\gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_{\text {app }}+\gamma_{\text {vessel }} ; \gamma_{\text {vessel }}=3 \alpha$
For vessel '$A$' $\Rightarrow \gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_{1}+3 \alpha_{A}$
For vessel '$B$' $\Rightarrow \gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_{2}+3 \alpha_{B}$
Hence, $\gamma_{1}+3 \alpha_{A}=\gamma_{2}+3 \alpha_{B} \Rightarrow \alpha_{B}=\dfrac{\gamma_{1}-\gamma_{2}}{3}+\alpha_{A}$
$\alpha_{B}=\dfrac{\gamma_{1}-\gamma_{2}}{3}+\alpha$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: A solid whose volume does not change with temperature floats in a liquid. For two different temperatures $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ of the liquid, dfractions $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ of the volume of the solid remain submerged in the liquid. The coefficient of volume expansion of the liquid is equal to
explanation:
As with the rise in temperature, the liquid undergoes volume expansion therefore the fraction of solid submerged in liquid increases. Initially when the block is floating at temperature $t_{1}$
${F_b} = {F_g} \Rightarrow {V_1}{\rho _l}g = {V_0}{\rho _s}g$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {f_1} = \frac{{{V_1}}}{{{V_0}}} = \frac{{{\rho _s}}}{{{\rho _l}}} = \frac{{{\rho _{{s_0}}}\left( {1 + {\gamma _l}{t_1}} \right)}}{{{\rho _{{l_0}}}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Here $V_{0}$ is total volume of block which is constant and $\rho_{s}$ is also constant that is $\rho_{s}=\rho_{s_{0}} \cdot V_{0}$ is the volume of the solid present inside the liquid.
Similarly at temperature $t_{2}$
${f_2} = \frac{{{\rho _{{s_0}}}}}{{{\rho _{{l_0}}}}}\left( {1 + {\gamma _l}{t_2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), $\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{1+\gamma t_{1}}{1+\gamma t_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \gamma=\dfrac{f_{1}-f_{2}}{f_{2} t_{1}-f_{1} t_{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: A glass flask is filled up to a mark with $50\,cc$ of mercury at $18^\circ C$. If the flask and contents are heated to $38^\circ C$, how much mercury will be above the mark ( $\alpha$ for glass is $9 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$ and coefficient of real expansion of mercury is $180 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$ )
explanation:
Due to volume expansion of both mercury and flask, the change in volume of mercury relative to flask is given by
$\Delta V = {V_0}\left[ {{\gamma _L} - {\gamma _g}} \right]\Delta \theta = {V_0}\left[ {{\gamma _m} - 3{\alpha _g}} \right]\Delta \theta $
$ = 50\left[ {180 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} - 3 \times 9 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right](38 - 18) = 0.153\,cc$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: A uniform pressure $P$ is exerted by an external agent on all sides of a solid cube at temperature $t^\circ C$. By what amount should the temperature of the cube be raised in order to bring its volume back to its original volume before the pressure was applied if the bulk modulus is $B$ and co-efficient of volumetric expansion is $\gamma$ ?
explanation: $d V=-\dfrac{p V}{B} \Rightarrow V \gamma \Delta T=\dfrac{p V}{B} \Rightarrow \Delta T=\dfrac{p}{\gamma B}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: The figure shows a rectangular brass plate at $0^\circ C$ in which there is a rectangular hole of dimensions indicated. If the temperature of the plate is raised to $150^\circ C$.
explanation:
For isotropic material separation between two points varies as
$l=l_{0}(1+\alpha \Delta T)$
After heating, all the dimensions $x,y\,\& \,z$ increase.
subject: Physics

question: An isosceles triangle is formed with in a rod of length $l_{1}$ and coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha_{1}$ as the base and two thin rods each of length $l_{2}$ and coefficient of linear expansions $\alpha_{2}$ as the two sides. If the distance between the apex and the midpoint of the base remain unchanged as the temperature is varied, then
explanation:
Let $l_1^1\,\& \,\,l_2^1$ are new lengths of the rods after increasing temperature $\Delta T$ as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954152/preptracker/questions/q_37033_explanation_1764954151628.png
As $h$ remains constant
$\sqrt{l_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{l_{1}^{2}}{4}}=\sqrt{l_{2}^{l^{2}}-\dfrac{l_{1}^{l^{2}}}{4}}$
square on both sides.
$\begin{aligned}& l_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{l_{1}^{2}}{4}=l_{2}^{1^{2}}-\dfrac{l_{1}^{1^{2}}}{4} \\& l_{2}^{2}-\dfrac{l_{1}^{2}}{4}=l_{2}^{2}\left(1+\alpha_{2} \Delta T\right)^{2}-\dfrac{l_{1}^{2}\left(1+\alpha_{1} \Delta T\right)^{2}}{4} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=2 \sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A pendulum clock (fitted with a small heavy bob that is connected with a metal rod ) is 5 seconds fast each day at a temperature of  $15^\circ C$ and 10 seconds slow at a temperature of $30^\circ C$. The temperature at which it is designed to give correct time, is
explanation:
Fractional loss of time per second $=\dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \Delta T$
Therefore
$\frac{1}{2}\alpha \left( {{T_0} - 15} \right) \times (24hrs) = 5\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and
$\frac{1}{2}\alpha \left( {30 - {T_0}} \right) \times (24hrs) = 10\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
from eqn (1) and (2)
on solving ${T_0} = 20^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A wheel is $80.3\,cm$ in circumference. An iron tyre measures $80.0\,cm$ around its inner face. If the coefficient of linear expansion for iron is $1.2 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$, the temperature of the tyre must be raised by
explanation:
Here, $\Delta l = 80.3 - 80.0 = 0.3\,cm$
$l = 80\,cm,\alpha = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}$
Rise in temperature $\Delta T=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l \alpha}$
$\Delta T = \frac{{0.3}}{{80 \times 1.2 \times {{10}^{ - 5}}}} = 312.5^\circ C$
subject: Physics

question: A steel wire of uniform area $2\,\,m{m^2}$ is heated upto $50^\circ C$ and is stretched by tying its ends rigidly. The change in tension when the temperature falls from $50^\circ C$ to $30^\circ C$ is (Take $Y = 2 \times {10^{11}}N{m^{ - 2}},\alpha  = 1.1 \times {10^{ - 5^\circ }}{C^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
As, $F=\alpha(\Delta T) A Y$
$ = 1.1 \times {10^{ - 5}} \times (50 - 30) \times 2 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 2 \times {10^{11}}$
$ = 88\,N$
subject: Physics

question: A wire of length $L_{0}$ is supplied heat to raise its temperature by $T$. If $\gamma$ is the coefficient of volume expansion of the wire and $Y$ is young's modulus of the wire then the energy density stored in the wire is
explanation:
Due to heating the length of the wire increases.
$\therefore \quad$ Longitudinal strain is produced
$\begin{aligned}\Rightarrow & \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}=\alpha \times \Delta T \\E & =\dfrac{1}{2} \times \text { Stress } \times \text { Strain }=\dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times(\text { Strain })^{2}\end{aligned}$$\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times\left(\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times \alpha^{2} \times \Delta T^{2}$
[As $\gamma=3 \alpha$ and $\Delta T=T$ (given)]
$\Rightarrow E=\dfrac{1}{2} \times Y \times\left(\dfrac{\gamma}{3}\right)^{2} \times T^{2}=\dfrac{1}{18} \gamma^{2} Y T^{2}$
subject: Physics

question: The coefficient of linear expansion of an in homogeneous rod changes linearly from $\alpha_{1}$ to $\alpha_{2}$ from one end to the other end of the rod. The effective coefficient of linear expansion of rod is
explanation:
The coefficient of linear expansion of the rod as a function of $x$ is
$\alpha=\alpha_{1}+\left(\dfrac{\alpha_{2}-\alpha_{1}}{L}\right) x$
Let $d L$ is the small change in the length of the small part of length $d x$, for a rise in temperature of $\Delta T$
$\begin{aligned}d L & =\alpha d x T \\\Delta L & =\int_{0}^{L} \alpha d x \Delta T \\L & =\left(\dfrac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{2}\right) L \Delta T ; \quad \alpha_{e f f}=\left(\dfrac{\alpha_{1}+\alpha_{2}}{2}\right)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A 2 litres glass flask contains some mercury. It is found that at all temperatures the volume of the air inside the flask remains the same. The volume of the mercury inside the flask is $\left( {\alpha  = 9 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}},{\gamma _m} = 1.8 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}\,^\circ {C^{ - 1}}} \right)$
explanation:
It is given that the volume of air in the flask remains the same. This means that the expansion in volume of the container is exactly equal to the volume expansion of mercury,
$\begin{aligned}& \text { i.e. } \Delta V_{v}=\Delta V_{m} \\& c \Rightarrow V_{c} \gamma_{c} \Delta T=V_{m} \gamma_{m} \Delta T \\& \therefore V_{c}=\dfrac{V_{c} \gamma_{c}}{\gamma_{m}}=\dfrac{V_{c} \times 3 \alpha}{\gamma_{m}} \quad\left(\because \gamma_{c}=3 \alpha\right) \\& \Rightarrow V_{m}=\dfrac{2000 \times 3 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}} \\& V_{m}=300 c c\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: The absolute coefficient of expansion of a liquid is 7 times that the volume coefficient of expansion of the vessel. Then the ratio of absolute and apparent expansion of the liquid is
explanation:
Apparent coefficient of volume expansion $\gamma_{a p p .}=\gamma_{L}-\gamma_{s}=7 \gamma_{s}-\gamma_{s}=6 \gamma_{s}\left(\right.$ given $\left.\gamma_{L}=7 \gamma_{s}\right)$
ratio of absolute and apparent expansion of liquid
$\dfrac{\gamma_{L}}{\gamma_{\text {app. }}}=\dfrac{7 \gamma_{s}}{6 \gamma_{s}}=\dfrac{7}{6}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Coefficient of Apparent Expansion of a Liquid in a Vessel

question: The coefficient of volume expansion of a liquid is $49 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,{K^{ - 1}}$. Calculate the fractional change in its density when the temperature is raised by $30^\circ C.$
explanation:
When the temperature of a liquid is increased by $\Delta T^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the mass will remain unchanged while due to thermal expansion volume will increase and becomes $V^{\prime}$.
$V^{\prime}=V(1+\gamma \Delta T)$
where $\gamma$ is the coefficient of volume expansion of liquid
$\therefore \quad \rho^{\prime}=\dfrac{m}{V^{\prime}}=\dfrac{m}{V(1+\gamma \Delta T)}=\dfrac{\rho}{1+\gamma \Delta T}$
Fractional change in density $=\dfrac{\rho-\rho^{\prime}}{\rho}$
$\begin{aligned}& =\left(1-\dfrac{\rho^{\prime}}{\rho}\right)=\left(1-\dfrac{1}{1+\gamma \Delta T}\right) \\& =\dfrac{\gamma \Delta T}{1+\gamma \Delta T}=\dfrac{49 \times 10^{-5} \times 30}{1+49 \times 10^{-5} \times 30} \\& =\dfrac{0.0147}{1.0147}=0.0145 \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A vessel is partly filled with a liquid. Coefficients of cubical expansion of material of vessel and liquid are $\gamma_{V}$ and $\gamma_{L}$ respectively. If the system is heated, then volume unoccupied by the liquid will necessarily
explanation: Since the vessel is partly filled, volume of the vessel is greater than that of the liquid. Hence on heating expansion of vessel will be greater than that of liquid, since $\gamma_{V}=\gamma_{L}$. It means unoccupied volume will necessarily increase. So, option (2) is correct.
subject: Physics

question: A metal cube of length $10.0\,mm$ at $0^\circ C$ is heated to $200^\circ C$. Its coefficient of linear expansion is $2 \times {10^{ - 5^\circ }}{C^{ - 1}}$. The percent change of its volume is
explanation: Percentage change in volume $=\gamma t \times 100$ $=3 \alpha t \times 100=300 \alpha t=300 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 200=1.2$

explanation: The hole also expands and hence values of ${r_1},\,{r_2}$ increases

question: A rectangular block is heated from $0^\circ C$ to $100^\circ C$. The percentage increases in its length is $0.2 \%$. What is the percentage increases in its volume?
explanation: As, $V=L^{3}$ $\therefore \dfrac{\Delta V \times 100}{V}=3 \dfrac{\Delta L \times 100}{L}=3 \times 0.2 \%=0.6 \%$

question: A solid ball of metal has a concentric spherical cavity with in it. If the ball is heated, the volume of the cavity will
explanation: When the ball is heated, expansion of ball and cavity both occurs, hence volume of cavity increases.

question: An iron rod of length $50\,cm$ is joined at an end to an aluminium rod of length $100\,cm$. All measurements refer to $20^\circ C$. The coefficients of linear expansion of iron and aluminium are $12 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$ and $24 \times {10^{ - 6}}/^\circ C$, respectively. The length of composite system is:
explanation:
Initially (at $20^\circ C)$ length of composite system $L = 50 + 100 = 150\,cm$
Length of iron rod at $100^\circ C$
$ = 50\left[ {1 + 12 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times (100 - 20)} \right] = 50.048\,cm$
Length of aluminium rod at $100^\circ C$
$ = 100\left[ {1 + 24 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times (100 - 20)} \right] = 100.192\,cm$
Finally (at $100^\circ C$ ) length of composite system ${L^\prime } = 50.048 + 100.192 = 150.24\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: When the temperature of a rod increases from to $t+\Delta t$, its moment of intertia increases from $I$ to $I+\Delta I$. If $\alpha$ be the coefficient of linear expansion of the rod, then the value of $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I}$ is
explanation:
Moment of inertia of a rod,
$I = \frac{1}{{12}}M{L^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where $M$ is the mass of the $\operatorname{rod}$ and $L$ is the length of the rod
$\therefore \Delta I = \frac{1}{{12}}2ML\Delta L\,\,\,\left( {1pt\because M\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{is}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{a}}\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{constant}}} \right){\text{ }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 2 \right)$
Divide (2) by (1), we get
$\frac{{\Delta I}}{I} = 2\frac{{\Delta L}}{L}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
As $\Delta L = L\alpha \Delta T\,{\text{or}}\,\frac{{\Delta L}}{L} = \alpha \Delta t\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
$\dfrac{\Delta I}{I}=2 \alpha \Delta t$
subject: Physics

question: An iron bar of length $10\,m$ is heated from $0^\circ C$ to $100^\circ C$. If the coefficient of linear thermal expansion of iron is $10 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the increase in the length of bar is
explanation:
Increase in length $\Delta L=L_{0} \alpha \Delta T$
$ = 10 \times 10 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times (100 - 0) = {10^{ - 2}}\,m = 1\,cm.$

explanation:

So stress is independent of length of the rod.

question: The density of a substance at $0^\circ C$ is $10\,g/cc$ and at $100^\circ C$, its density is $9.7\,g/cc.$ The coefficient of linear expansion of the substance is:
explanation:
Density of substance is $d=d_{0}(1-\gamma \theta)$
$\begin{aligned}& 9.7=10(1-\gamma 100) \\& \Rightarrow \gamma=3 \times 10^{-4}\end{aligned}$
Coefficient of linear expansion is
$\alpha=\dfrac{\gamma}{3}=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{3}=10^{-4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: A uniform solid brass sphere of radius $a_{0}$ and mass $m$ set spinning with angular speed $\omega_{0}$ about a diameter at temperature $T_{0}$. If its temperature be increased to $T$ without disturbing the sphere, its new angular velocity $\omega$ will be assuming that its new radius is $a$ :
explanation:
From conservation of angular momentum,
$I_{0} \omega_{0}=I \omega$
Now $I_{0}=\dfrac{2}{5} m a_{0}^{2}$ and $I=\dfrac{2}{5} m a^{2}$. Hence
$\omega=\left(\dfrac{I_{0}}{I}\right) \omega_{0}=\left(\dfrac{a_{0}}{a}\right)^{2} \omega_{0}$ where $a$ is the radius of the sphere at $T$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question: If the length of a cylinder on heating increases by $2 \%$, the area of its base will increase by
explanation:
The relation between dfractional change in area and length of a body is $A=L^{2}$
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}=2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta A}{A}=2 \times 2=4 \%\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Areal and Volume Expansion of Solids

question:
A steel rod of diameter $1\,cm$ is clamped firmly at each end when its temperature is $25^\circ C$ so that it cannot contract on cooling. The tension in the rod at $0^\circ C$ is approximately
$\left( {\alpha = {{10}^{ - 5}}/^\circ C,Y = 2 \times {{10}^{11}}N{m^{ - 2}}} \right):$
explanation:
Strain $=\dfrac{\Delta l}{l}=\alpha \Delta \theta$
Stress $=Y \times$ Strain $=Y \alpha \Delta \theta$
$\therefore$ Force or tension,
$T=$ Stress $\times$ Area $=Y A \alpha \Delta \theta$
$\begin{aligned}& =\dfrac{\pi Y \alpha d^{2} \Delta \theta}{4}\left(A=\dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}\right) \\T & =\dfrac{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-4} \times 25}{4} \\& =3926 N \approx 4000 N\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: An iron bar of length $l$ and having a cross-section $A$ is heated from 0 to $100^\circ C$. If this bar is so held that it is not permitted to expand or bend, the force that is developed, is
explanation:
$Y=\dfrac{F L}{A \Delta L}$ where $Y$ is young's modulus, $A$ is area
$ \Rightarrow F = \frac{{YA\Delta L}}{L}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
From the formula for linear expansion
$\alpha = \frac{{\Delta L}}{{L \times 100}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
According to the condition the bar should not bend or expand
Now from equations (1) and (2)
$F=Y A \times 100 \alpha$
Hence, force is independent of length $L$.

options.text: Independent of the length of the bar
Inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area of the bar
Directly proportional to the length of the bar
Inversely proportional to the length of the bar

subject: Physics

question: Two uniform rods $A B$ and $B C$ have Young's modulus $1.2 \times {10^{11}}\,N/{m^2}$ and $1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,N/{m^2}$ respectively. If coefficient of linear expansion of $AB$ is $1.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}/^\circ C$ and both have equal area of cross section, then coefficient of linear expansion of $BC$, for which there is no shift of the junction at all temperatures, is
explanation:
If the junction does not not shift then force exerted by both rods must be same.
$\begin{gathered}F=A Y \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}=A Y \alpha \Delta T \\Y_{1} \alpha_{1}=Y_{2} \alpha_{2} \Rightarrow \alpha_{B C}=\dfrac{Y_{A B} \alpha_{A B}}{Y_{B C}}=1.2 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} C\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
